docs.title: Crucible
docs.text:
Crucible is a CLI-first, modular EVM bridge security research testbed for historical exploit reconstruction, deterministic local reruns, baseline reset, structured research artifacts, and plug-and-play attack packs.

It ships with 11 attack packs: 6 benchmark reproductions (Wormhole, Nomad, Poly Network, Horizon, BNB Bridge, Multichain), 2 demo packs, 1 LLM agent attack framework, 1 L4 multi-chain validation pack, and 1 bridge demo (Nomad). 5 of them have L2 reproducibility (Wormhole, Nomad, Poly Network, Horizon, BNB Bridge), 1 narrative with L1 reproducibility (Multichain), plus 4 fixture and demonstration packs.

Looking for a broader overview? See the Technical Overview for system philosophy, core concepts, and design principles, or the Design Decisions document for the rationale behind every major architectural choice.

docs.title: Quick Start for Researchers
docs.text: Get up and running with Crucible in under 5 minutes to reproduce your first historical exploit.

docs.title: 1. Clone & Install
docs.text:
Crucible requires Python 3.11+ and Foundry (anvil, forge, cast). 

git clone https://github.com/AsokaKrsna/crucible_testbed #the project is currently in limited access mode.
cd crucible-testbed

# Install dependencies using uv (recommended for speed)
pip install uv
uv sync

# Initialize the workspace directories
crucible init

docs.title: 2. Configure Environment
docs.text:
Crucible needs archive node RPC URLs to fork historical blockchain states. Copy the template and add your providers.

cp .env.example .env
Edit your .env to include at least an Ethereum archive URL: 
ETH_RPC_URL=https://eth.llamarpc.com

docs.title: 3. Verify Health
docs.text:
Ensure your environment, Foundry installation, and RPC connections are perfectly configured: 

crucible doctor

docs.title: 4. Run a Benchmark Exploit
docs.text:
Run the Nomad Bridge L2 exploit reproduction ($190M hack). This command will fork Ethereum and Polygon at the exact historical block, verify the baseline determinism, and run the attack plugin automatically:

crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml

Prefer Docker? Crucible also supports a fully containerized workflow. See the Deployment Guide for zero-dependency execution.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text: Capability How Fork any EVM chain at any block (Some chains may require paid RPC tier) crucible fork --topology <name> Deterministic replay Fixed gas/fee/timestamp/order by default (Can opt-out) 3-tier reset soft (<5s) → medium (<30s) → hard (<5min) Structured artifacts 7 JSONL streams + SHA-256 integrity per run Plugin SDK AttackPlugin, BridgePlugin, AgentPlugin Auto-extract attacks crucible attack extract <tx_hash> --name <name> Publication gates 6 research-quality checks before every run Cache provenance Cold/warm/strict/permissive cache fingerprinting Experiment comparison crucible diff <run_a> <run_b> Export bundles Self-contained tarballs with integrity manifests Streamlit dashboard (Not recommended over CLI) crucible dashboard or streamlit run dashboard/app.py Interactive analysis crucible notebook — Jupyter with crucible.analysis helpers Post-run analytics from crucible.analysis import load_run, load_gas, ... — pandas DataFrames

docs.title: Academic Artifact Evaluation (AE) Walkthrough
docs.text: Purpose: This document is explicitly designed for Academic Artifact Evaluation Committees (AEC). It serves as a rapid, blind-testing checklist to verify badge claims for academic publication (e.g., USENIX Security, IEEE S&P, ACM CCS, NDSS). Target Time: <30 minutes for the demonstration path.

docs.title: 1. Badge Claims
docs.text: Badge Claimed Verification Metric Available [ok] The public repository is accessible and initializes successfully via the native CLI. Functional [ok] The demonstration experiment executes fully, generating standardized artifacts and reports. Reproduced [ok] Cross-provider reproduction executions generate deterministically matching assertion outcomes.

docs.title: 2. Quick Start
docs.text:
Ensure you have completed the prerequisite environment initialization detailed in the Deployment Guide before proceeding.

# 1. Clone the specific release branch
git clone <repo-url> --branch <release-tag>
cd crucible_testbed

# 2. Validate the environment and resolve dependencies
crucible doctor --fix

docs.title: 3. Demonstration Experiment
docs.text:
Execute the safe preview mode first to ensure the pipeline is operational, followed by the complete execution.

# 1. Execute safe preview (dry-run)
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --dry-run

# 2. Execute the complete demonstration experiment
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml

# 3. Verify the experiment history and outcome
crucible history --outcome success --limit 5

docs.title: 4. Publication Mode Constraints
docs.text:
To evaluate the artifact under strict research-quality gates, execute the experiment utilizing --publication-mode.

crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --publication-mode

This strict execution profile enforces: - Evidence completeness verification against the declared fidelity level. - Baseline infrastructure verification. - Mandatory provenance completeness.

docs.title: 5. Artifact Verification and Export
docs.text:
Upon successful completion, Crucible bundles the deterministic execution artifacts for archival distribution. Reviewers can verify the cryptographic integrity of the bundle prior to export.

# 1. Verify the integrity of the run manifest and artifacts
cat logs/demo_experiment/<run_id>/log_integrity.json

# 2. Export the bundled publication artifact
crucible export <run_id>

Note: For comprehensive details regarding directory structures, deterministic guarantees, hardware constraints, or expected artifact payloads, refer directly to the Reproducibility Guide.

docs.title: Analysis & Notebooks Guide
docs.text: Purpose: Interactive experiment analysis with Jupyter notebooks. Commands: crucible notebook | Module: crucible.analysis

docs.title: 1. Quick Start
docs.text:
# Install dependencies
pip install "crucible[jupyter]"
# or: uv sync --extra jupyter

# Launch Jupyter with the notebooks/ directory
crucible notebook

# Or with custom options
crucible notebook --port 9999 --no-browser

Jupyter opens at http://localhost:8888/tree with the notebooks/ directory as the root.

docs.title: 2. The crucible.analysis Module
docs.text:
The analysis module provides pandas-based helpers for loading experiment run data:

from crucible.analysis import (
    load_run,          # Load run manifest as dict
    load_events,       # Load events as DataFrame
    load_gas,          # Load gas profile as DataFrame
    load_storage_diffs,# Load storage changes as DataFrame
    load_cross_chain,  # Load cross-chain messages as DataFrame
    load_balance_diffs,# Load balance changes as DataFrame
    load_agent_reasoning, # Load LLM agent reasoning traces as DataFrame
    find_run,          # Find run directory by ID
    list_runs,         # List all available runs
)

# Load run data
manifest = load_run("DS-2026-00001")
events = load_events("DS-2026-00001")
gas = load_gas("DS-2026-00001")
diffs = load_storage_diffs("DS-2026-00001")
agent_traces = load_agent_reasoning("DS-2026-00001")

# Explore
print(f"Outcome: {manifest['outcome']}")
print(f"Gas by phase: {gas.groupby('phase')['gas_used'].sum()}")

# Compare two runs
from crucible.diff import compare_runs
result = compare_runs(find_run("DS-2026-00001"), find_run("DS-2026-00002"))
print(f"Deterministic: {result['storage_equal'] and result['events_equal']}")

docs.title: 3. Included Notebooks
docs.text:
notebooks/experiment_analysis.ipynb provides 6 ready-to-run cells:

  1. Load experiment data — imports and loads a run by ID
  2. Gas analysis — bar chart, pie chart, publication-ready gas tables
  3. Event timeline — scatter plot of decoded contract events
  4. Storage diff analysis — top modified contracts and slot counts
  5. Run comparison — determinism check between two runs
  6. LaTeX export — publication-ready gas profile tables

docs.title: 4. Discovering Run IDs
docs.text:
from crucible.analysis import list_runs

for r in list_runs():
    print(f"{r['run_id']} — {r['scenario']} ({r['outcome']})")

docs.title: 5. Operational Guidelines
docs.text:
• Targeting Runs: Set RUN_ID at the top of the notebook to your target experiment ID.
  • Output Routing: The figures/ directory is created automatically for exported charts.
  • Rendering: Use %matplotlib inline for inline rendering (already the default in Jupyter).

Core principle: The researcher writes exploit logic. Crucible handles everything else - chain forking, instrumentation, artifact capture, integrity hashing, and provenance tracking.

docs.title: 4. Data Flow: Experiment Lifecycle
docs.text:
A single crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml triggers this sequence:

sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User / CLI
    participant ER as ExperimentRunner
    participant CFG as Config Loader
    participant PG as Publication Gates
    participant SE as ScenarioEngine
    participant CM as ChainManager
    participant IP as Instrumentation Proxy
    participant PR as PluginRegistry
    participant AP as AttackPlugin
    participant LM as LogManager
    participant PROV as Provenance

    U->>CFG: load_config() + load_experiment()
    U->>ER: run(experiment_path)
    ER->>ER: _check_foundry_version()
    ER->>ER: _enforce_cleanup()
    ER->>PG: check_all() (if publication mode)
    ER->>LM: new LogManager(run_dir)

    rect rgb(230, 240, 255)
        Note over SE,CM: Phase 1 - Environment Setup
        ER->>SE: run(scenario_name, context)
        SE->>CM: start_chain("ethereum")
        CM->>CM: anvil_command() â†’ Popen()
        CM->>CM: _await_ready() (poll eth_blockNumber)
        SE->>SE: execute YAML steps (log, assert, impersonate...)
    end

    rect rgb(230, 255, 230)
        Note over CM,IP: Phase 2 - Instrumentation
        ER->>CM: start_instrumentation("ethereum", chain_log)
        CM->>IP: attach_instrumentation(upstream_url, log)
        IP->>IP: HTTPServer.start() on random port
        Note over CM: runtime_for() now returns proxy URL
    end

    rect rgb(255, 240, 230)
        Note over PR,AP: Phase 3 - Attack Execution
        ER->>PR: load("attack", attack_name)
        PR->>AP: importlib â†’ instantiate plugin class
        ER->>AP: plugin.run(context)
        AP->>CM: runtime_for("ethereum") â†’ proxy URL
        AP->>IP: eth_sendTransaction (via proxy)
        IP->>IP: forward to Anvil â†’ capture receipt â†’ trace
        IP->>LM: emit(gas, events, storage_diffs, balances)
    end

    rect rgb(245, 230, 255)
        Note over ER,PROV: Phase 4 - Teardown & Artifacts
        ER->>CM: stop_instrumentation() (drain + stop)
        ER->>PROV: build_run_manifest()
        ER->>LM: write_report()
        ER->>LM: write_integrity()
    end
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

docs.title: Key Design Property: Transparent Auto-Instrumentation
docs.text:
Crucible removes the burden of manual telemetry from the researcher. The attack plugin operates completely unaware that it is being instrumented.

In traditional testbeds, researchers must write complex tracking logic inside their exploit scripts to capture gas usage, storage diffs, and events. Crucible eliminates this requirement through a transparent RPC Proxy.

graph LR
    subgraph "Attack Phase"
        AP[Attack Plugin<br/>(e.g., Python / cast)]
    end

    subgraph "Crucible Background"
        PRX[Instrumentation Proxy<br/>Port 9545]
        ANV[Anvil Node<br/>Port 8545]
        ART[Artifact Logger<br/>JSONL Streams]
    end

    AP -- "1. eth_sendTransaction" --> PRX
    PRX -- "2. Forward tx" --> ANV
    ANV -- "3. Return receipt" --> PRX
    PRX -- "4. eth_traceTransaction" --> ANV
    ANV -- "5. Return trace data" --> PRX
    PRX -- "6. Extract & Save" --> ART
    PRX -- "7. Return result" --> AP

    style AP fill:#f9d0c4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PRX fill:#d4e6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ANV fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

How it works under the hood: When start_instrumentation() is called, the ChainManager launches the proxy and secretly updates the connection URL. When the attack plugin asks for the blockchain URL via context.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url, it is handed the Proxy URL instead of the direct Anvil URL:

# chain_manager.py — transparent URL substitution
def runtime_for(self, chain_name):
    rpc_url = self._anvils[chain_name].rpc_url
    # If a proxy is running, secretly intercept all traffic
    if chain_name in self._proxies:
        rpc_url = self._proxies[chain_name].proxy_url  
    return ChainRuntime(chain_name, rpc_url, ...)

Because of this seamless swap, any attack — whether it's written in Python using web3.py, a native Solidity forge script, or raw bash cast send commands — blindly sends its transactions to the proxy. The proxy captures publication-quality artifacts (gas profiles, balance diffs, storage diffs) in the background and returns the result to the plugin as if nothing happened.

docs.title: Plugin Type Hierarchy
docs.text:
classDiagram
    class CruciblePlugin {
        <<abstract>>
        +metadata() dict
    }

    class AttackPlugin {
        +run(ctx)
        +prepare(ctx)
        +execute(ctx, phase) AttackResult
        +verify(ctx) VerificationResult
        +rollback(ctx)
        +cleanup(ctx)
    }

    class BridgePlugin {
        +deploy(ctx) dict
        +configure(ctx, config)
        +connect(ctx)
        +send_message(ctx, src, dst, data) dict
        +reset(ctx)
        +destroy(ctx)
    }

    class AgentPlugin {
        +run(ctx)
        +prepare(ctx, agent_ctx)
        +observe(ctx, agent_ctx) dict
        +think(obs, ctx, agent_ctx) AgentAction
        +respond(result, ctx, agent_ctx)
        +is_done(ctx, agent_ctx) bool
        +verify(ctx, agent_ctx) VerificationResult
    }

    CruciblePlugin <|-- AttackPlugin
    CruciblePlugin <|-- BridgePlugin
    CruciblePlugin <|-- AgentPlugin
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

docs.title: Plugin Discovery & Loading
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    subgraph Filesystem
        A1["attacks/nomad_replay/plugin.yaml"]
        A2["attacks/nomad_replay/nomad_replay.py"]
        B1["bridges/demo_bridge/plugin.yaml"]
        P1["plugins/agent_llm/plugin.yaml"]
    end

    subgraph PluginRegistry
        DISC["discover()"]
        VAL["validate()"]
        LOAD["load()"]
    end

    A1 --> DISC
    B1 --> DISC
    P1 --> DISC
    DISC --> VAL
    VAL -->|"check manifest + entry + evidence"| LOAD
    LOAD -->|"importlib.util.spec_from_file_location()"| INST["Plugin Instance"]
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

Search directories: attacks/, bridges/, plugins/

Manifest format (plugin.yaml): 

name: nomad_replay
version: 1.0.0
type: attack
entry: nomad_replay.py
class: NomadReplayAttack     # modern format
# class_name: NomadReplayAttack  # legacy format (also supported)

Attack plugins must include evidence.yaml alongside plugin.yaml. The registry validates evidence structure during discovery.

Bridge plugins deployed: | Plugin | Contracts | Vulnerability | Lifecycle | |---|---|---|---| | nomad_bridge | NomadReplica.sol | committedRoot == 0x00 | deploy -> configure -> connect -> send_message -> reset -> destroy | | poly_network_bridge | EthCrossChainManager + EthCrossChainData | Hash collision selector dispatch | Same lifecycle | | wormhole_bridge | WormholeCore + TokenBridge | Guardian bypass mode | Same lifecycle |

All three are discovered and loaded by the PluginRegistry, validated against evidence.yaml, and wired into the experiment lifecycle via bridge_lifecycle.py.

docs.title: ExperimentContext - The Plugin's Interface to Crucible
docs.text:
classDiagram
    class ExperimentContext {
        +config: TestbedConfig
        +topology: TopologyConfig
        +chains: ChainManager
        +log: LogEmitter
        +workspace: Path
        +run_id: str
        +run_config: dict
        +bridge_plugin: BridgePlugin?
    }
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

Every plugin method receives an ExperimentContext. Through it, the plugin can: - Read chain state: context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum") â†’ JsonRpcClient - Log events: context.log.step("message"), context.log.event(...) - Access config: context.config.chains["ethereum"].fork_block - Use bridges: context.bridge_plugin.send_message(...)

docs.title: Anvil Subprocess Lifecycle
docs.text:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Configured: load testbed.yaml
    Configured --> Starting: start_chain()
    Starting --> WaitingReady: Popen(anvil ...)
    WaitingReady --> Running: _await_ready() (poll eth_blockNumber)
    Running --> Instrumented: start_instrumentation()
    Instrumented --> Draining: drain() (flush traces)
    Draining --> Running: stop_instrumentation()
    Running --> Stopped: stop_all()
    Stopped --> [*]: meta.json deleted
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

docs.title: Anvil Command Construction
docs.text:
ChainManager.anvil_command() builds a deterministic Anvil command:

anvil \
  --fork-url ${ETH_RPC_URL} \
  --fork-block-number 15259100 \
  --host 127.0.0.1 \
  --port 8545 \
  --chain-id 1 \
  --gas-price 0 \
  --block-base-fee-per-gas 1000000000 \
  --auto-impersonate \
  --order fifo \
  --cache-path .fork_cache/ethereum/15259100

docs.title: 3-Tier Reset System
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    SOFT["Soft Reset<br/>< 5 seconds"]
    MED["Medium Reset<br/>< 30 seconds"]
    HARD["Hard Reset<br/>< 5 minutes"]

    SOFT -->|"anvil_loadState"| STATE["Restore from snapshot"]
    MED -->|"kill + relaunch + restore"| RELAUNCH["Fresh Anvil + restore"]
    HARD -->|"re-fork from RPC"| REFORK["Full re-fork + bootstrap"]
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom Tier Method Speed Fidelity Use Case Soft anvil_loadState <5s Snapshot-perfect Matrix runs, parameter sweeps Medium Kill â†’ restart â†’ restore <30s Clean process After process corruption Hard Re-fork from upstream RPC <5min Full re-fetch After cache corruption, topology change

docs.title: RPC Provider Failover
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    REQ["RPC Request"]
    P1["Provider 1 (primary)"]
    P2["Provider 2 (llamarpc)"]
    P3["Provider 3 (ankr)"]
    P4["Provider 4 (publicnode)"]

    REQ --> P1
    P1 -->|"429 / timeout"| P2
    P2 -->|"429 / timeout"| P3
    P3 -->|"429 / timeout"| P4
    P4 -->|"all failed"| ERR["RpcError"]

    P1 -->|"success"| OK["JsonRpcClient"]
    P2 -->|"success"| OK
    P3 -->|"success"| OK
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

Providers are configured per-chain in testbed.yaml and tried in priority order. Each provider has its own TokenBucket rate limiter.

docs.title: 7. Instrumentation Proxy
docs.text: The instrumentation proxy is the architectural centerpiece - it makes artifact capture automatic and universal.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Attack Plugin"
        SEND["eth_sendTransaction"]
    end

    subgraph "Instrumentation Proxy (HTTPServer thread)"
        FWD["Forward to Anvil"]
        SCHED["Schedule trace"]
    end

    subgraph "ThreadPoolExecutor"
        RECEIPT["eth_getTransactionReceipt"]
        TRACE["debug_traceTransaction<br/>(prestateTracer diffMode)"]
        PARSE["Parse trace â†’ artifacts"]
    end

    subgraph "Anvil Fork"
        ANVIL["Process TX"]
    end

    subgraph "LogEmitter"
        GAS["gas_profile.jsonl"]
        EVENTS["events.jsonl"]
        STORAGE["storage_diffs.jsonl"]
        BALANCE["balance_diffs.jsonl"]
    end

    SEND --> FWD
    FWD --> ANVIL
    ANVIL --> FWD
    FWD --> SCHED

    SCHED --> RECEIPT
    RECEIPT --> TRACE
    TRACE --> PARSE
    PARSE --> GAS
    PARSE --> EVENTS
    PARSE --> STORAGE
    PARSE --> BALANCE
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

docs.title: What Gets Captured Per Transaction
docs.text: Artifact Source Stream Gas used eth_getTransactionReceipt â†’ gasUsed gas_profile.jsonl Contract events Receipt â†’ logs[] â†’ decode topic[0] events.jsonl Storage diffs debug_traceTransaction â†’ pre/post storage storage_diffs.jsonl Balance changes debug_traceTransaction â†’ pre/post balance balance_diffs.jsonl

docs.title: Multi-Chain Proxy Isolation
docs.text:
Each chain gets its own proxy instance with its own handler class:

Ethereum: Attack â†’ Proxy :9001 â†’ Anvil :8545
BSC:      Attack â†’ Proxy :9002 â†’ Anvil :8547
Polygon:  Attack â†’ Proxy :9003 â†’ Anvil :8546

The _make_handler() closure captures upstream_url, log, and chain per instance, preventing shared-state bugs across chains.

docs.title: Shutdown Sequence
docs.text:
1. proxy.drain()    â†’ ThreadPoolExecutor.shutdown(wait=True)
                      (all pending traces complete)
2. proxy.stop()     â†’ HTTPServer.shutdown() + server_close()
3. log.write_integrity()  â†’ close file handles + hash files

drain() must be called before stop() and before log handles close. This ensures all async trace tasks complete before artifacts are finalized.

docs.title: Log Directory Structure
docs.text:
logs/
  nomad_experiment/
    DS-2026-00042/                    â† run directory
      run.jsonl                       â† experiment lifecycle events
      run_manifest.json               â† provenance, config, outcome
      log_integrity.json              â† SHA-256 hashes of all streams
      report.md                       â† human-readable summary
      chain_ethereum/                 â† per-chain artifacts
        run.jsonl                     â† chain-specific lifecycle
        events.jsonl                  â† contract events
        gas_profile.jsonl             â† gas usage per transaction
        storage_diffs.jsonl           â† storage slot changes
        balance_diffs.jsonl           â† ETH/token balance changes
      chain_bsc/
        ...
      bridge/                         â† cross-chain correlation
        cross_chain.jsonl             â† bridge message tracking
        events.jsonl

docs.title: 7 JSONL Artifact Streams
docs.text:
The testbed produces 6 core streams managed by LogEmitter through the standard emit API, plus 1 agent-specific stream written directly by the LLM agent plugin:

Stream Content Written By run.jsonl Experiment lifecycle (phase start/end, errors) LogManager.step() events.jsonl Contract events (Transfer, Approval, custom) LogEmitter.event() via proxy storage_diffs.jsonl Storage slot changes (contract, slot, before, after) LogEmitter.storage_diff() via proxy gas_profile.jsonl Gas used per transaction LogEmitter.gas() via proxy balance_diffs.jsonl Balance changes (ETH and token) LogEmitter.balance_diff() via proxy cross_chain.jsonl Bridge message correlation IDs LogEmitter.cross_chain() agent_reasoning.jsonl LLM agent reasoning traces (observation, action, conversation snapshot) - written outside the standard LogEmitter system, so not tracked in log_integrity.json LLMAgentPlugin._log_reasoning_step()

docs.title: LogManager vs LogEmitter
docs.text:
classDiagram
    class LogManager {
        -_root: LogEmitter
        -_chain_emitters: dict
        -_bridge_emitter: LogEmitter
        +step(message)
        +for_chain(name) LogEmitter
        +bridge() LogEmitter
        +write_report()
        +write_integrity()
    }

    class LogEmitter {
        -_handles: dict[stream, file]
        +emit(stream, fields)
        +step(message)
        +event(chain, event_name)
        +storage_diff(chain, contract, slot, before, after)
        +gas(step, gas_used)
        +balance_diff(chain, contract, account, before, after)
        +cross_chain(correlation_id, source, dest)
        +close()
        +write_integrity()
    }

    LogManager --> LogEmitter : root
    LogManager --> LogEmitter : chain_ethereum
    LogManager --> LogEmitter : chain_bsc
    LogManager --> LogEmitter : bridge
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

LogManager is the experiment-level orchestrator. It creates per-chain LogEmitter instances lazily via for_chain(). Attack plugins interact with context.log (which is a LogManager) - lifecycle events go to the root emitter, while chain-specific data routes to the per-chain subdirectory.

docs.title: Diff Engine - Determinism Verification
docs.text:
crucible diff <run-a> <run-b> canonicalizes and compares four metrics:

flowchart LR
    A["Run A"] --> CA["Canonical Sort"]
    B["Run B"] --> CB["Canonical Sort"]

    CA --> CMP["Compare"]
    CB --> CMP

    CMP --> S["storage_equal: true/false"]
    CMP --> E["events_equal: true/false"]
    CMP --> G["gas_equal: true/false"]
    CMP --> BL["balances_equal: true/false"]
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

All four must be true for deterministic reproduction.

Note: The 6 core streams appear in STREAMS in crucible/logging.py. The 7th (agent_reasoning.jsonl) is emitted by LLMAgentPlugin directly and bypasses integrity hashing - this is a known gap.

docs.title: Built-In Validations
docs.text:
• Port uniqueness - no two chains can share a port (model-level validator)
  • Chain ID uniqueness - no two chains can share a chain_id
  • Port range - must be 1â€“65535
  • Matrix detection - load_experiment() rejects matrix files with a helpful error message

docs.title: 10. Scenario Engine
docs.text: Scenarios define the chain environment before the attack runs. They set up contract state, impersonate accounts, and configure initial conditions.

docs.title: Scenario Structure
docs.text:
# scenarios/nomad_replay/scenario.yaml
phases:
  - name: setup
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Setting up Nomad Replica contract state"

      - type: impersonate
        chain: ethereum
        account: "0x..."

      - type: send_tx
        chain: ethereum
        to: "0xB92..."
        data: "0x..."

      - type: assert_balance
        chain: ethereum
        account: "0xB92..."
        min_value: "1000000000000000000"

  - name: attack_ready
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Environment ready for attack"

docs.title: Step Dispatch (Registry Pattern)
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    YAML["YAML Step<br/>{type: 'send_tx', ...}"] --> DISPATCH["handle_step()"]
    DISPATCH --> REG["Step Registry"]
    REG --> H1["@register_step('log')"]
    REG --> H2["@register_step('send_tx')"]
    REG --> H3["@register_step('impersonate')"]
    REG --> H4["@register_step('assert_balance')"]
    REG --> H5["@register_step('forge_script')"]
    REG --> H6["@register_step('python_script')"]
    REG --> H7["@register_step('advance_time')"]
    REG --> H8["@register_step('assert')"]
    REG --> H9["@register_step('verify_tracing')"]
    REG --> H10["@register_step('bridge_action')"]
    REG --> H11["@register_step('plugin')"]
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

Step handlers are registered via decorator - no if/elif chain:

@register_step("send_tx")
def _handle_send_tx(step, phase, context, workspace):
    ...

New step types can be added by registering a new handler without modifying the engine.

Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

docs.title: Agent Tool Registry
docs.text: Tool Privilege Description chain_id LOW Get chain ID of running fork get_balance LOW Get ETH balance (wei) get_code LOW Get runtime bytecode get_storage LOW Get storage at slot call_contract LOW Read-only eth_call get_logs LOW Get event logs for address impersonate MEDIUM Impersonate an account send_transaction MEDIUM Send a transaction advance_time HIGH Advance EVM timestamp

docs.title: Privilege Levels
docs.text:
LOW     â†’ Read-only queries (5 tools)
MEDIUM  â†’ Impersonate + transact (7 tools, default)
HIGH    â†’ Full access including time manipulation (9 tools)

Configured per-experiment in the YAML: 

run:
  agent_provider: openai
  agent_privilege: medium
  agent_allowed_accounts: ["0x..."]

docs.title: Determinism Boundary
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Crucible Guarantees"
        DET1["Fixed gas price (0)"]
        DET2["Fixed block base fee"]
        DET3["FIFO tx ordering"]
        DET4["Pinned fork block"]
        DET5["Fork-block timestamp"]
        DET6["Auto-mine (instant)"]
    end

    subgraph "Not Guaranteed"
        ND1["Cross-RPC-provider<br/>response ordering"]
        ND2["Multi-chain relay<br/>timing"]
        ND3["Python dict ordering<br/>(mitigated by sort_keys)"]
    end
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

docs.title: Provenance Tracking
docs.text:
Every run produces a run_manifest.json:

{
  "run_id": "DS-2026-00042",
  "scenario": "nomad_replay",
  "attack": "nomad_replay",
  "topology": "single",
  "outcome": "success",
  "provenance": {
    "git_commit": "a1b2c3d...",
    "git_dirty": false,
    "foundry_version": "forge 0.2.0 (nightly)",
    "python_version": "3.12.4",
    "platform": "Linux-x86_64",
    "docker_mode": "container",
    "config_fingerprint": "sha256:abc123..."
  }
}

docs.title: Integrity Verification
docs.text:
log_integrity.json contains SHA-256 hashes of every artifact file:

{
  "files": {
    "run.jsonl": "sha256:...",
    "events.jsonl": "sha256:...",
    "storage_diffs.jsonl": "sha256:...",
    "gas_profile.jsonl": "sha256:...",
    "balance_diffs.jsonl": "sha256:..."
  }
}

docs.title: Fidelity Levels
docs.text:
flowchart TB
    L0["L0 - Narrative Only<br/>No code, description only"]
    L1["L1 - Conceptual<br/>Deploys vulnerable contract"]
    L2["L2 - Fork-State Behavioral<br/>Fork at historical block, check behavior"]
    L3["L3 - Exact Transaction Replay<br/>Replay real exploit tx on fork"]
    L4["L4 - Multi-Chain Full<br/>Multiple chains + bridge simulation"]

    L0 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4

    L2 -.->|"requires"| REQ2["chains + affected_contracts"]
    L3 -.->|"requires"| REQ3["exploit_transactions"]
    L4 -.->|"requires"| REQ4["excluded_components declared"]
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

docs.title: 7 Publication Gates
docs.text:
Gate What It Checks Evidence evidence.yaml has all required sections Fidelity Fidelity-level-specific requirements met Baseline Golden baseline exists and verifies Foundry Foundry version pinned (no nightly drift) Git Provenance Workspace is a git repo with a known commit Artifacts Complete All expected trace artifacts were captured (no auto-trace failures) Provenance Git commit clean, platform recorded

All 7 gates must pass before a run can be exported for publication.

docs.title: Two Operational Modes
docs.text:
Mode Command When to Use Single-container (default) docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "fork && experiment run" Most experiments - Anvil runs inside orchestrator Daemon + orchestrator docker compose up -d anvil-ethereum && docker compose run orchestrator experiment run Multi-session workflows, streaming

Recommended: Single-container mode. Simpler, fully isolated, avoids daemon fork-block mismatch.

â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ poly_network_bridge.yaml # ethereum + polygon + bsc + poly_network_bridge
â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ wormhole_bridge.yaml     # ethereum + polygon + wormhole_bridge
â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ single_bsc.yaml          # BSC only
â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ poly_network_full.yaml   # Ethereum + Polygon + BSC (no bridge)
â”‚   â””â”€â”€ harmony_bridge.yaml      # Ethereum + Harmony + bridge
â”œâ”€â”€ states/                      # Chain state (meta files, snapshots, baselines)
â”œâ”€â”€ logs/                        # Experiment output artifacts
â”œâ”€â”€ datasets/                    # Exported publication bundles
â”œâ”€â”€ docs/                        # Documentation (23 files + 2 case studies + 2 design docs + 12 internal)
â”œâ”€â”€ tests/                       # Test suite (798+ tests)
â”œâ”€â”€ testbed.yaml                 # Main configuration

docs.title: 17. Key Design Decisions
docs.text: For a deeper treatment of each decision - including the tradeoffs considered and why the current path was chosen - see the Design Decisions document.

docs.title: CLI-First, UI as Wrapper
docs.text: Every action is available via crucible <command>. The Streamlit dashboard is a separate read-only viewer. This makes experiments scriptable, CI-able, and Docker-friendly.

docs.title: Topology-Driven Composition
docs.text:
Topology YAML files declare which chains to fork. The same attack can run on different topologies. Adding a new chain config doesn't affect existing experiments.

# topologies/single.yaml - Ethereum only
name: single
chains: [ethereum]
bridge: null

# topologies/bridge.yaml - Two chains + bridge (demo)
name: bridge
chains: [ethereum, polygon]
bridge: demo_bridge

# topologies/nomad_bridge.yaml - Two chains + Nomad bridge plugin
name: nomad_bridge
chains: [ethereum, polygon]
bridge: nomad_bridge

# topologies/wormhole_bridge.yaml - Two chains + Wormhole bridge plugin
name: wormhole_bridge
chains: [ethereum, polygon]
bridge: wormhole_bridge

# topologies/poly_network_bridge.yaml - Three chains + Poly Network bridge plugin
name: poly_network_bridge
chains: [ethereum, polygon, bsc]
bridge: poly_network_bridge

docs.title: Transparent Instrumentation
docs.text: The instrumentation proxy intercepts all RPC traffic. Researchers don't need to add logging code. Any attack method (Python, Forge, cast send) produces complete artifacts.

docs.title: Snapshot-Driven Reset
docs.text: Baselines are captured once after setup. Soft resets use anvil_loadState - no chain restart needed. This enables fast iteration during development.

docs.title: Determinism by Default
docs.text: Deterministic flags (gas_price: 0, order: fifo, timestamp: fork_block) are opt-out, not opt-in. If a researcher does nothing, their experiments are deterministic.

docs.title: Plugins for Capabilities
docs.text: New functionality arrives as plugins, not core rewrites: - attack - exploit reproductions - bridge - bridge protocol simulation - agent - LLM-driven autonomous agents

docs.title: Fidelity Levels Are Enforced
docs.text: The L0â€“L4 fidelity system isn't just documentation - publication gates check requirements programmatically. An L2 reproduction without affected_contracts will fail the gate.

docs.title: Separation of Scenario and Experiment
docs.text: Scenarios define environments (chain state, contract setup). Experiments pair a scenario + attack plugin for formal runs. This means the same scenario can be used with different attacks, and the same attack can run against different scenarios.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers docs/TECHNICAL_OVERVIEW.md System philosophy, core concepts, data flow, design principles (broad overview) docs/internal/DESIGN_DECISIONS.md 15 key architectural decisions with rationale, tradeoffs, and alternatives docs/internal/BRIDGE_PLUGIN_LIMITATIONS.md Bridge plugin coverage, limitations, and future improvement scope docs/REPRODUCTION_SCOPE.md Fidelity levels, in-scope vs out-of-scope attack types docs/ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md Creating attack plugins and scenarios

docs.title: Core Architecture
docs.text:
An attack pack is a self-contained directory structure that provides the Crucible testbed with the necessary components to execute a specific exploit against a target environment. This encompasses both historical reproductions (re-executing past incidents) and novel attack development (testing zero-days against a deployed scenario).

(For details on how the execution environment is decoupled from the attack logic, see the Scenario Authoring Guide).

docs.title: 1. plugin.yaml (Plugin Manifest)
docs.text:
Declares plugin metadata, entrypoints, and runtime requirements for the Crucible engine.

name: "your_attack_name"
type: attack
version: "1.0.0"
author: "Author Name"
entry: "your_attack.py"
class: "YourAttackClass"
requires:
  crucible: ">=1.0.0"
  chains:
    - ethereum
  topology: single

docs.title: 2. evidence.yaml (Research Evidence Manifest)
docs.text:
The primary research artifact. It formally declares the on-chain data, sources, and success criteria for the exploit.

  • Historical Reproductions: Documents the exact transaction hashes, affected contracts, and fork blocks from the mainnet incident. The historical_scope.fork_blocks configuration serves as the authoritative source of truth for the testbed's automatic fork management.
  • Novel Attacks: The incident and sources sections document the hypothetical vulnerability, while reproduction_scope is typically set to L0 (Simulation) or L1 (Proof of Concept).
incident:
  name: "Vulnerability Name"
  date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
  affected_bridge: "Protocol Name"
  funds_lost_usd: 611000000
  summary: >
    Technical summary of the root cause and vulnerability mechanics.

sources:
  advisories: ["https://..."]
  postmortems: ["https://..."]
  tx_explorers: ["https://etherscan.io/tx/0x..."]

historical_scope:
  chains: ["ethereum"]
  fork_blocks:
    ethereum: 15259100
  exploit_transactions: ["0x..."]
  affected_contracts:
    TargetContract: "0xVerifiedAddress..."
  attacker_addresses: ["0x..."]
  vulnerability_function: "vulnerableFunction"

reproduction_scope:
  fidelity_level: L2
  exact_tx_replay: false
  modeled_components: ["On-chain state manipulation via contract X"]
  excluded_components: ["Off-chain relayer architecture"]

acceptance:
  required_assertions:
    - name: exploit_succeeds
      description: "The exploit transaction executes without reverting"
      expected: true

docs.title: 3. <name>.py (Attack Implementation)
docs.text:
The Python implementation defining the exploit logic. Must inherit from AttackPlugin and implement execute() and verify(). It may also optionally implement prepare(), rollback(), and cleanup() for full lifecycle management.

from crucible.sdk import AttackPlugin, AttackResult, ExperimentContext, VerificationResult
from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient

class YourAttack(AttackPlugin):
    def metadata(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "name": "your_attack",
            "version": "1.0.0",
            "type": "attack",
            "fidelity_level": "L2",
        }

    def execute(self, context: ExperimentContext, phase: int | None = None) -> AttackResult:
        rpc_url = context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url
        client = JsonRpcClient(rpc_url)

        # Implementation logic
        context.log.step("Exploit execution completed",
                         phase="attack", action="exploit", status="success")
        return AttackResult(success=True, phase_results=[])

    def verify(self, context: ExperimentContext) -> VerificationResult:
        return VerificationResult(passed=True, assertions=[], errors=[])

    # Optional lifecycle hooks:
    # def prepare(self, context: ExperimentContext) -> None:
    #     """Set up attacker accounts, approve tokens, deploy helpers."""
    #     pass

    # def rollback(self, context: ExperimentContext) -> None:
    #     """Undo persistent changes not handled by snapshot restore."""
    #     pass

    # def cleanup(self, context: ExperimentContext) -> None:
    #     """Remove temp files, kill helper processes."""
    #     pass

docs.title: 4. scenario.yaml (Environment Definition)
docs.text:
Initializes the required environment before yielding to the attack plugin. See the Scenario Authoring Guide for full documentation.

name: my_attack_pre_exploit
chains:
  - ethereum
phases:
  - name: setup
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Starting reproduction at fork block"
  - name: attack
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Attack plugin executed by experiment runner"

docs.title: 5. experiments/<name>_replay.yaml (Experiment Orchestration)
docs.text:
Wires the components together and defines runtime parameters such as artifact capture toggles.

name: my_attack_replay
scenario: my_attack_pre_exploit
attack: my_attack
topology: single
run:
  traces: true
  storage_diffs: true
  gas_profiling: true
tags: [my_attack, l2]

docs.title: Scaffolding from Historical Transactions
docs.text:
Crucible provides a utility to automatically scaffold attack packs from historical on-chain transactions:

crucible attack extract <tx_hash> --name <name> --chain <chain>

Capabilities: - Extracts ABI definitions from public registries. - Decodes transaction calldata and function signatures. - Pre-fills evidence.yaml with contract addresses, attacker wallets, and the correct fork block (derived from the transaction's block minus one). - Scaffolds the plugin.yaml, scenario.yaml, and Python implementation file.

Limitations: - ABI decoding is best-effort; complex nested structures or proxy patterns may fail to decode. - The generated Python file contains functional stubs; the actual execute() logic must be manually implemented. - Unverified contracts will yield empty ABIs.

docs.title: Scaffolding Novel Attacks
docs.text:
For novel attack development where no historical transaction exists, use the initialization command:

crucible attack init <name>

This generates a clean boilerplate pack without attempting to derive fork blocks or ABIs from an explorer.

docs.title: Research Methodology & Data Collection
docs.text:
Accurate execution requires rigorous on-chain data collection prior to implementation. For novel attacks, this primarily involves identifying the deployment block of your target smart contracts. For historical reproductions, this requires exact incident data:

Data Requirement Definition Retrieval Source Fork Block The state prior to the exploit. For reproductions: the block of the first exploit tx minus 1. For novel attacks: any block after the target contract deployment. Block explorer transaction details. Exploit Tx Hash Target transaction for calldata verification and L3 exact replay (Reproductions only). Block explorer or security postmortems. Verified Contracts Target smart contracts for the attack payload. Block explorer (must show verified source code). Attacker Wallet The transaction initiator (msg.sender). "From" field on the exploit transaction (or a generated mock address).

docs.title: Deriving the Fork Block
docs.text:
The fork block defines the state of the network immediately before the vulnerability is triggered.

For historical reproductions: 1. Identify the first malicious transaction on the block explorer. 2. Record the block height of that transaction. 3. Subtract 1 to determine the fork block. 4. Verify that the target contract is deployed and contains the vulnerable state at this block.

For novel attacks: 1. Identify the block where the target contract was deployed and fully initialized. 2. Select any block subsequent to initialization as your fork block.

[!WARNING] Contract Address Verification The most common error during pack authoring is confusing an EOA (Externally Owned Account) with a smart contract. Always verify on a block explorer that the target address contains deployed bytecode and verified source code before declaring it in evidence.yaml.

docs.title: Core SDK Operations
docs.text: Operation Implementation API RPC Client Instantiation client = JsonRpcClient(context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url) State Verification code = client.get_code("0x...") Storage Reading val = client.get_storage_at("0x...", "0x1") Account Impersonation client.impersonate_account("0x...") Transaction Dispatch client.send_transaction({"from": addr, "to": addr, "data": "0x...", "gas": hex(300000)}) Execution Logging context.log.step("Action description", phase="attack", action="action_type", status="success")

docs.title: Reference Implementations
docs.text:
Study existing benchmarks to understand complex implementations:

Attack Implementation Key Concepts Demonstrated demo_attack Minimal baseline plugin structure. nomad_replica_fraud Storage slot analysis, ABI encoding, multi-step execution. wormhole_guardian_bypass Constant definitions, rigorous verification logic. poly_network_privkey Hash collision calculations, dynamic calldata construction, contract impersonation.

docs.title: Translating Exploit Transactions to Code
docs.text:
When porting a historical exploit transaction to a plugin, avoid copying raw hex calldata directly. Raw calldata often contains hardcoded addresses, nonces, or environment-specific parameters that are invalid on a localized fork.

  1. Decompose the Transaction: Use a block explorer's decoding tool to understand the function parameters (e.g., verifyHeaderAndExecuteTx(bytes header, bytes proof)).
  2. Reconstruct Calldata: Utilize an ABI encoding library (eth_abi) to dynamically pack the arguments.
# Improper: Copying raw historical calldata
calldata = "0x41973cd900000..." # Contains historical state specific to mainnet

# Proper: Programmatic reconstruction
from eth_abi import encode
args = encode(["bytes", "bytes"], [header_data, proof_data])
calldata = "0x41973cd9" + args.hex()

docs.title: Validation and Testing Workflow
docs.text:
Execute the following commands to validate the attack pack locally or via Docker.

# 1. Structural Validation
docker compose run --rm orchestrator plugin validate attacks/my_attack

# 2. Registration Verification
docker compose run --rm orchestrator plugin list --type attack

# 3. Execution (Auto-merges fork_block from evidence.yaml)
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  rm -f states/*.meta.json && \
  uv run crucible fork --topology single && \
  uv run crucible experiment run experiments/my_attack_replay.yaml --no-baseline
"

# 4. Result Analysis
docker compose run --rm orchestrator logs list

# 5. Determinism Verification (Execute twice, compare results)
docker compose run --rm orchestrator diff <run_a> <run_b>

docs.title: L3: Exact Transaction Replay
docs.text:
L3 reproductions execute the exact historical transaction against the forked state, demanding identical calldata and resulting state transitions.

  1. Declare exact_tx_replay: true in evidence.yaml.
  2. Provide the precise historical transaction hash in exploit_transactions.
  3. Construct calldata in the plugin that perfectly matches the historical execution.

docs.title: L4: Multi-Chain Bridge Topologies
docs.text:
L4 reproductions require executing attacks across multiple EVM forks simultaneously, facilitated by an instrumented bridge plugin.

Required Configuration: 

# experiments/my_l4_experiment.yaml
topology: nomad_bridge   # Must specify a multi-chain bridge topology

Bridge Plugin Interface: 

def execute(self, context: ExperimentContext, phase=None) -> AttackResult:
    # Bridge is automatically deployed by the orchestrator
    bridge = context.bridge_plugin

    # Dispatch cross-chain payload
    result = bridge.send_message(
        context=context,
        source_chain="ethereum",
        dest_chain="polygon",
        data="0x..."  # Forged calldata
    )

docs.title: Common Execution Failures
docs.text:
If you encounter errors during implementation (e.g., contract state missing, double hex prefixes, or L3 fidelity violations), consult the centralized Troubleshooting Guide.

The troubleshooting guide contains detailed fixes for every common authoring pitfall, including: - Addressing "0x" get_code() results (EOA vs Contract confusion). - Fixing keccak256 hashing discrepancies. - Resolving state availability issues on paid archive RPCs. - Debugging bridge deployment and cross-chain execution failures.

docs.title: External Resources
docs.text: Domain Resource ABI Specification Solidity ABI Spec Keccak Hashing eth-hash Library Storage Layout Solidity Storage Internals Function Signatures 4byte Directory ABI Encoding/Decoding eth-abi Python Library

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Reference Purpose docs/SCENARIO_AUTHORING.md Scenario definition reference docs/CLI_REFERENCE.md Testbed command-line interface specification docs/FIDELITY_GUIDE.md Fidelity tier definitions and enforcement mechanisms docs/REPRODUCIBILITY.md Technical mechanisms for ensuring deterministic execution

docs.title: Benchmark Attack Packs
docs.text: Crucible includes 6 benchmark attack packs reproducing $1.9B+ in historical bridge exploit losses. Each pack includes a concrete exploit plugin, evidence manifest, scenario definitions, and experiment YAML ready for deterministic reproduction.

docs.title: Included Benchmarks
docs.text:
The following table catalogs the available benchmark reproductions. For complete transparency, it explicitly documents the upper boundary of reproduction fidelity and the constraints preventing it from achieving higher fidelity (e.g., missing non-EVM execution environments or archive node limitations).

Attack Pack Experiment File Declared Fidelity Demonstrates Reproduction Constraints nomad_replica_fraud nomad_replay.yaml L2 Fork-state process() call with forged message; deterministic diff proven. Requires exact proxy ABI resolution to reconstruct the exact historical transaction. wormhole_guardian_bypass wormhole_replay.yaml L2 EVM-side VAA processing via completeTransfer(); WormholeCore + TokenBridge contracts verified. Root cause was Solana-side. Only the EVM-side effects (forged VAA acceptance) are reproducible. Cannot achieve L3 without Solana execution environment. poly_network_privkey poly_network_replay.yaml L2 Ethereum-side putCurEpochConPubKeyBytes via hash collision; determinism proven. Ethereum-only. BSC and Polygon archives at exploit blocks require paid providers. Multi-chain relay not simulated. horizon_multisig_drain horizon_replay.yaml L2 Fork-state reproduction at block 15005526; 3 bridge contracts verified; multisig authorization flow demonstrated. Infrastructure compromise. This was a compromised-multisig attack, not a contract bug. Reproducing the tx proves nothing about vulnerability. Harmony chain state is not EVM-forkable. bnb_token_hub_proof bnb_token_hub_replay.yaml L2 Fork-state reproduction at block 22166659 on BSC; target contract and selector verified; malformed IAVL proof attempt confirmed. BSC-only. Requires paid archive RPC. The reproduction confirms contract existence and documents the mechanism at L2. multichain_router_permit multichain_replay.yaml L1 Infrastructure narrative with evidence of MPC key shard compromise. Constrained to L1. MPC node infrastructure compromise, not a smart contract vulnerability. No on-chain reproduction is possible.

docs.title: EVM Boundary Constraints
docs.text: Crucible is strictly EVM-only. For attacks involving non-EVM components (e.g., Solana, Harmony), the non-EVM portion must be declared in excluded_components and modeled explicitly via the EVM environment.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers Technical Overview System architecture, core concepts, data flow, design principles Design Decisions 15 key architectural decisions with rationale and tradeoffs Plugin Registry Catalog of available infrastructure, bridge, and agent plugins Attack Pack Guide End-to-end attack creation workflow Fidelity Guide Fidelity levels (L0-L4), enforcement rules, --no-fidelity usage

docs.title: Bridge Plugin System â€” Current Coverage & Limitations
docs.text: Date: June 2026 (Updated) Audience: Researchers, contributors, and reviewers evaluating Crucible's bridge experimentation capabilities. Purpose: Document Crucible's bridge protocol simulation capabilities â€” what's covered by the 3 implemented bridge plugins and what are the known limitations.

docs.title: 1. Executive Summary
docs.text:
Crucible now ships with 3 real bridge plugin implementations covering the most significant historical bridge protocols:

Bridge Plugin Solidity Contracts Vulnerability Model Chains NomadBridge (bridges/nomad_bridge/) NomadReplica committedRoot == 0x00 â€” forged message accepted ethereum + polygon PolyNetworkBridge (bridges/poly_network_bridge/) EthCrossChainManager + EthCrossChainData Hash collision in _executeCrossChainTx selector dispatch ethereum + polygon + bsc WormholeBridge (bridges/wormhole_bridge/) WormholeCore + TokenBridge Guardian bypass â€” VAAs accepted without real signatures ethereum + polygon

Each plugin: - Deploys real Solidity contracts to forked chains (compiled via forge build) - Configures the vulnerable initial state (zero root, default keeper, bypass mode) - Supports the full BridgePlugin lifecycle: deploy â†’ configure â†’ connect â†’ send_message â†’ reset â†’ destroy - Produces cross-chain artifacts through the existing instrumentation pipeline

These are protocol-simulating plugins, not full protocol replicas. They deploy minimal contracts that model the vulnerability path, enabling L4 experiments without running the complete production protocol (which can require non-EVM components, off-chain relayers, and governance processes).

docs.title: 2.1 BridgePlugin ABC
docs.text:
File: crucible/sdk.py

A full lifecycle interface:

Method Purpose Default Implementation deploy() Deploy bridge contracts to both chains Returns {} configure() Set validator set, relayer addresses, guardian keys No-op connect() Start message-passing simulation (relayer process) No-op send_message() Send a cross-chain message Logs only, returns empty result reset() Reset bridge state to post-deploy baseline No-op destroy() Tear down relayer and clean up deployed contracts No-op

Status: [ok] Well-designed ABC â€” all 3 bridge plugins override every method with real implementations.

docs.title: 2.2 Bridge Lifecycle Wiring
docs.text:
File: crucible/bridge_lifecycle.py

The experiment runner automatically: 1. Loads the bridge plugin from topology config (topology.bridge) 2. Calls deploy() â†’ configure() â†’ connect() before scenario setup 3. Calls destroy() in finally blocks (both run() and resume())

Status: [ok] Production-ready wiring â€” all 3 bridge plugins benefit from this automatically.

docs.title: 2.3 Scenario Engine Integration
docs.text:
File: crucible/scenario_steps.py

A bridge_action step type with 4 actions:

Action Purpose Current Behavior send_message Send cross-chain message Delegates to bridge_plugin.send_message() relay_messages Trigger relayer to process pending messages Calls bridge_plugin.connect() check_delivery Verify a specific message was delivered Log-only verify_state Check bridge contract state Log-only

Symbolic address resolution (bridge.ethereum -> hex address from scenario) is wired in.

Status: [ok] Works with all 3 bridge plugins. send_message and relay_messages delegate to real plugin implementations.

docs.title: 2.4 Cross-Chain Observability
docs.text:
File: crucible/logging.py

  • Dedicated bridge subdirectory in log output: logs/<experiment>/<run>/bridge/
  • cross_chain.jsonl stream for message correlation IDs
  • LogManager.bridge() emitter for bridge-specific events

Status: [ok] Production-ready â€” captures real bridge traffic from all 3 plugins.

docs.title: 2.5 Topology Configuration
docs.text:
Files: topologies/nomad_bridge.yaml, topologies/poly_network_bridge.yaml, topologies/wormhole_bridge.yaml, topologies/bridge.yaml, topologies/harmony_bridge.yaml

name: nomad_bridge
chains: [ethereum, polygon]
bridge: nomad_bridge

Status: [ok] 3 dedicated bridge topology files, each pointing to its respective bridge plugin.

docs.title: 2.6 Evidence Validation
docs.text:
File: crucible/evidence.py

validate_bridge_evidence() checks that bridge plugins declare: - protocol.name and protocol.version - features (list of modeled features) - limitations (what's excluded or simplified) - chains (supported chains)

Status: [ok] Validates metadata correctly. All 3 bridge plugins pass validation.

docs.title: 3.1 Relayer Timing Model
docs.text:
All 3 bridge plugins use synchronous connect() â€” there is no configurable message relay latency. Real bridge exploits often depend on:

  • Relayer latency (minutes to hours across chains)
  • Guardian quorum timing (waiting for N signatures)
  • Message ordering (which message arrives first affects state)

Impact: Cannot simulate timing-dependent attack scenarios (e.g., front-running a relayer, oracle timing attacks).

docs.title: 3.2 L4 Fidelity Enforcement Is Structural
docs.text:
The FidelityEnforcer checks that a bridge plugin is declared in the topology config, but it cannot verify that the bridge plugin actually works (message passing, state synchronization). L4 is currently a structural declaration, not a behavioral guarantee.

Impact: An experiment that declares L4 but never calls send_message() will still pass the gate if a bridge plugin is in the topology.

docs.title: 3.3 Solidity Contracts Are Simplified
docs.text:
The contracts deployed by all 3 plugins are minimal implementations that model only the vulnerability path:

Real Protocol Feature Modeled? Impact Full Merkle tree verification No (simplified hash-chain) Cannot test advanced proof manipulation Real guardian/VAA signing No (bypass mode) Signing round simulation not available Governance / timelock No Cannot test governance-based attacks Token minting / burning No (log-only) Token supply changes are not modeled

Impact: These plugins are designed for L4 attack reproduction, not production-grade protocol testing.

docs.title: 3.4 No Bridge Protocol Templates
docs.text: There is no crucible bridge init scaffold generator for creating new bridge plugins. Researchers who want to build a custom bridge must do it from scratch.

docs.title: [ok] Covered By Current Implementation
docs.text: Use Case How Deploy a fresh Nomad Replica on 2+ chains topologies/nomad_bridge.yaml + NomadBridge plugin Deploy a fresh Wormhole Core + TokenBridge topologies/wormhole_bridge.yaml + WormholeBridge plugin Deploy Poly Network Manager + Data contracts topologies/poly_network_bridge.yaml + PolyNetworkBridge plugin Forge a cross-chain message (Nomad) send_message() with forged process() calldata Replace keeper keys (Poly Network) send_message() with hash-collision calldata Submit a forged VAA (Wormhole) send_message() with guardian-bypass completeTransfer() Set up pre-exploit bridge state Use bridge_action scenario steps with real plugins Reproduce a historical bridge exploit where the bridge is already deployed Fork the chain at the exploit block â€” the bridge contracts are part of the fork state Verify that a vulnerable state existed at a specific block L2: use get_code() + get_storage_at() in an AttackPlugin Replay an exploit transaction against a forked chain L3: forge exact calldata and send via client.send_transaction() Analyze bridge contract behavior during an exploit Auto-captured in 6 JSONL streams by the RPC proxy Test an attack plugin against different fork blocks Use experiment run with different fork_block values in testbed.local.yaml

docs.title: Partially Covered (Architecture Exists, Limited Implementation)
docs.text: Use Case Limitation Experiment with relay timing assumptions No relayer timing model â€” connect() is synchronous Test what-if scenarios with modified bridge parameters Must manually edit Solidity contracts before forge build Deploy a custom bridge protocol from scratch Must write full BridgePlugin + Solidity contracts from nothing

docs.title: Not Covered (Architecture Gap)
docs.text:
Use Case Why Non-EVM bridge components Crucible is EVM-only by design Real cross-chain relay with actual finality Anvil auto-mines instantly Multi-guardian consensus simulation No signing/verification round model MEV-aware bridge attack sequencing Anvil uses FIFO ordering Token minting / burning on cross-chain transfer Not modeled in simplified contracts

---|---|---| | optimistic-bridge | Nomad-style fraud-proof bridge | 3-4 days | | light-client-bridge | Wormhole-style guardian/light-client bridge | 3-4 days | | lock-mint-bridge | Simple token bridge (Portal-style) | 2-3 days |

docs.title: 5.3 Phase 3: Behavioral Assertions for L4 (2-3 days)
docs.text:
Upgrade L4 fidelity enforcement to verify actual bridge behavior:

Deliverable: - Bridge plugin declares behavioral assertions in evidence.yaml - L4 gate checks: message delivery confirmed, state synchronized across chains - FidelityEnforcer validates bridge behavior, not just structural presence

docs.title: 5.4 Total Remaining Investment
docs.text: Phase Effort Timeline (1 person) Phase 1: Relayer timing 2-3 days Week 1 Phase 2: Bridge templates (3 templates) 8-12 days Weeks 2-3 Phase 3: L4 enforcement 2-3 days Week 4 Total remaining 12-18 days 3-4 weeks

docs.title: 5. How Researchers Can Use The Bridge Plugins
Quick Start
docs.text:
# experiments/my_l4_experiment.yaml
name: my_l4_experiment
scenario: my_bridge_scenario
attack: my_attack_plugin
topology: nomad_bridge               # Use a bridge topology
run:
  traces: true
  storage_diffs: true
  gas_profiling: true

docs.title: From An Attack Plugin
docs.text:
def execute(self, context: ExperimentContext, phase=None) -> AttackResult:
    # The bridge is already deployed and configured by the lifecycle
    bridge = context.bridge_plugin

    # Send a forged cross-chain message
    result = bridge.send_message(
        source_chain="ethereum",
        dest_chain="polygon",
        data="0xdeadbeef..."  # forged calldata
    )

    # Use the existing RPC for chain-specific operations
    client = JsonRpcClient(context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url)
    # ... exploit logic using both chains and the bridge

docs.title: From A Scenario
docs.text:
# scenarios/my_bridge_scenario/scenario.yaml
phases:
  - name: setup
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Bridge is deployed and configured by the lifecycle"

  - name: attack
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Attack runs here (bridges ready)"

  - name: verify
    steps:
      - type: bridge_action
        action: verify_state
        chain: ethereum

docs.title: Validate The Bridge Plugin
docs.text:
crucible plugin list --type bridge
crucible plugin validate bridges/nomad_bridge
crucible plugin validate bridges/poly_network_bridge
crucible plugin validate bridges/wormhole_bridge

docs.title: Manual Cross-Chain Simulation (Without Bridge Plugin)
docs.text:
This pattern is still available for custom bridge protocols not covered by the 3 plugins:

# Chain A: call bridge.send_message()
client_a.send_transaction({"to": BRIDGE_A, "data": message_data})
# Chain B: simulate relayer by calling bridge.receive_message()
client_b.send_transaction({"to": BRIDGE_B, "data": message_data})

Crucible's instrumentation proxy captures every interaction automatically.

docs.title: 6. Summary
docs.text: Area Status BridgePlugin ABC [ok] Complete, well-designed Lifecycle wiring (deploy -> destroy) [ok] Production-ready Scenario step type (bridge_action) [ok] Wired, works with real plugins Cross-chain logging [ok] Production-ready Topology configuration [ok] 3 bridge topology files Evidence validation [ok] Validates metadata Real bridge plugins [ok] 3 plugins (Nomad, Poly Network, Wormhole) Solidity contracts for 3 protocols [ok] Compiled via forge build Integration tests [ok] 17+ unit tests in test_bridge_plugins.py

docs.title: Crucible - Reproduction Scope & Capabilities
docs.text: This document defines the feasibility parameters for experiments within the Crucible framework. It enumerates supported attack classes, compatible chains, available fidelity levels, and operational boundaries.

docs.title: Is Your Experiment In Scope?
docs.text:
The following decision matrix facilitates assessment of reproduction feasibility.

Question If YES If NO Does the exploit target an EVM-compatible chain? ✅ Proceed ❌ Out of scope (see Non-EVM Limitations) Does an archive RPC provider exist for that chain at the target block? ✅ Proceed ⚠️ Partial - The scenario may be modeled, but historical state forking is unsupported. Is the vulnerability a smart contract bug (not a key compromise)? ✅ Full reproduction possible ⚠️ Partial - the contract behaved as designed, but Crucible can still deploy the affected contracts via bridge plugins and demonstrate the exploit path (L2+) Does the exploit involve multiple chains? ✅ Use a multi-chain topology Single-chain topology is simpler and recommended Does the exploit require non-EVM components (e.g., Solana relayer)? ⚠️ Model the EVM side; declare the rest in excluded_components ✅ Full reproduction possible

docs.title: Supported Chain Types
docs.text:
Crucible utilizes Foundry's Anvil for historical state forking. Consequently, any EVM-compatible chain is supported, contingent upon the availability of an archive RPC endpoint capable of serving state at the target block.

This includes: - Mainnet Ethereum - L2s (Arbitrum, Optimism, Base, etc.) - Alt-L1s (BSC, Polygon, Avalanche C-Chain, etc.) - Custom or private EVM networks (by pointing rpc_url in testbed.yaml to your own node)

Archive Depth Warning: While Ethereum archive data is widely available on free tiers, fetching state for very old blocks on chains like BSC or Polygon often requires a paid RPC provider (e.g., GetBlock, QuickNode).

Heuristic: If the RPC endpoint supports cast call --rpc-url <YOUR_URL> --block <N> <CONTRACT> "balanceOf(address)", Crucible is capable of executing the fork.

docs.title: Supported Attack Categories
docs.text:
Because Crucible operates at the EVM execution layer, there is no whitelist of supported attack types. If the vulnerability can be triggered via standard EVM transactions on a forked chain, it can be reproduced.

Common examples include:

Category Typical Fidelity Reproduction Mechanism Smart contract logic bugs (reentrancy, access control, integer overflow, etc.) L2-L3 Fork at the pre-exploit block, deploy/execute the exploit contract, capture full traces Bridge protocol vulnerabilities (message verification bypass, guardian bypass, hash collision) L2-L4 Deploy bridge plugins to forked chains, simulate malicious cross-chain message flows Flash loan attacks L2-L3 Fork at the target block (historical liquidity pools are preserved), replay the flash loan sequence Oracle manipulation L2-L3 Fork includes historical oracle state; manipulate the oracle on the fork before executing the exploit Governance / Proxy attacks L2-L3 Fork, impersonate governance/multisig accounts using Anvil, execute malicious upgrades MEV / sandwich attacks L2 Fork and replay target transactions alongside exploit; Crucible sets strict FIFO ordering to guarantee determinism

docs.title: Custom Testing & Novel Attacks
docs.text:
Crucible is not limited to reproducing historical exploits. The exact same infrastructure—deterministic EVM forks, multi-chain bridge plugins, and automatic artifact capture—can be used for novel research:

Use Case Description Zero-Day Attack Development Develop and test novel exploits against live/forked mainnet state before responsible disclosure. "What-If" Counterfactuals Fork before a historical exploit, deploy a hypothetical patch to the vulnerable contract, and prove the exploit would have failed. Custom Contract Testing Deploy your own protocol contracts to a multi-chain fork environment to test cross-chain logic under realistic latency/state conditions. Agentic Security Research Use the AgentPlugin interface to give LLM agents access to a sandboxed, forked environment for autonomous vulnerability discovery.

See Custom Contract Testing for a guide on deploying your own Solidity code.

docs.title: Supported Fidelity Levels
docs.text:
Crucible defines a 5-level fidelity system that describes the depth of reproduction. The level is declared per attack in evidence.yaml and enforced at runtime.

Level Name What It Means When to Use L0 Narrative No executable code. The attack is documented but not reproduced. Attack is fundamentally non-reproducible (e.g., social engineering) L1 Conceptual A script runs and demonstrates the vulnerability concept, but does not replay historical transactions. Infrastructure compromises where contracts behaved as designed L2 Fork-State Behavioral Fork at the historical block, verify vulnerable contracts exist, send exploit calldata, verify state changes. Most smart contract exploit reproductions L3 Exact Transaction Replay All of L2 plus the exact historical transaction(s) are replayed byte-for-byte on the fork. High-fidelity academic reproductions L4 Multi-Chain Full All of L3 plus multiple chains forked simultaneously with an instrumented bridge plugin simulating cross-chain message passing. Bridge exploit reproductions requiring cross-chain state

Available L4 Bridge Plugins:

Plugin Topology Chains Vulnerability Class nomad_bridge topologies/nomad_bridge.yaml ethereum + polygon Message verification bypass (committedRoot == 0x00) poly_network_bridge topologies/poly_network_bridge.yaml ethereum + polygon + bsc Hash collision selector wormhole_bridge topologies/wormhole_bridge.yaml ethereum + polygon Guardian signature bypass

docs.title: Artifact Capture (Automatic)
docs.text:
Every experiment automatically produces publication-grade artifacts without any instrumentation code in the attack script:

Artifact What It Records run.jsonl Complete experiment lifecycle events, phase transitions, and final results events.jsonl All contract events emitted during the experiment storage_diffs.jsonl Every storage slot that changed (before/after values) gas_profile.jsonl Gas consumed per transaction balance_diffs.jsonl ETH/token balance changes per account cross_chain.jsonl Cross-chain message correlation (bridge experiments) agent_reasoning.jsonl LLM agent observe→think→act trace (if using AgentPlugin) run_manifest.json Full provenance: git commit, Foundry version, Python version, platform, config fingerprint log_integrity.json SHA-256 hash of every artifact file for tamper detection

docs.title: Determinism Guarantees
docs.text:
Crucible enforces deterministic execution by default:

Parameter Value Effect Gas price 0 Eliminates gas-related non-determinism Block base fee 1 Gwei (fixed) Consistent fee calculation Transaction ordering FIFO Prevents ordering-dependent result variation Timestamp Pinned to fork block No time-dependent drift Auto-mine Enabled Transactions are mined instantly

Two runs of the same experiment against the same fork block, using the same RPC provider and Foundry version, will produce identical artifacts. Use crucible diff <run_a> <run_b> to verify.

docs.title: Non-EVM Chains
docs.text:
Crucible is EVM-only. It cannot fork or execute transactions on:

  • Solana (e.g., Wormhole root cause was Solana-side)
  • Cosmos / IBC chains
  • Substrate-based chains (Polkadot, Kusama, Moonriver, etc.)
  • Harmony ONE (used by Horizon bridge)
  • Move-based chains (Aptos, Sui)

Mitigation: For cross-chain vulnerabilities involving a non-EVM counterpart, the EVM component may be reproduced while declaring the non-EVM elements within excluded_components in the evidence.yaml manifest. This paradigm is utilized by the native bridge attack packs for Wormhole (Solana component excluded) and Horizon (Harmony component excluded).

docs.title: Infrastructure Compromises
docs.text:
If the exploit was a private key compromise or an operational security failure, the smart contracts behaved exactly as designed. There is no on-chain bug to replay.

Examples: - Multichain (July 2023): MPC key shard theft - the authorized keys signed valid transactions - Horizon (June 2022): 2-of-5 multisig key compromise - the multisig contract worked correctly

The on-chain exploit transaction itself cannot be meaningfully reproduced because the contracts behaved as designed. However, Crucible can still deploy the affected contracts via bridge plugins and demonstrate the vulnerable contract interfaces at L2 fidelity.

docs.title: State Not Served by Archive RPC
docs.text:
Archive RPC providers may not serve complete state at very old blocks. This manifests as:

  • eth_getCode returning empty for contracts that existed at the fork block
  • anvil_setBalance failing for addresses that didn't exist at the fork block
  • Storage reads returning zero for slots that should have data

This is a provider limitation, not a Crucible limitation. Mitigations: - Use a paid archive provider with deep history (e.g., GetBlock, QuickNode, Alchemy Growth) - Use Crucible's fork cache (.fork_cache/) to avoid re-fetching state across runs - Check provider archive depth before starting an experiment

docs.title: Cross-Platform Bit-Identical Results
docs.text:
While Crucible maximizes determinism, exact bit-identical results across different environments are not guaranteed due to:

  • Different Foundry nightly builds may produce slightly different gas estimates
  • Different archive RPC providers may serve slightly different state at the same block (rare)
  • Docker vs. native execution may have minor differences

For formal publications: Utilize Docker (with a pinned Foundry version) and execute the experiment twice with crucible diff to confirm determinism on the specific host environment.

docs.title: RPC Provider Requirements
docs.text: Scenario What You Need Single-chain Ethereum experiment Any free archive RPC (Ankr, LlamaRPC, PublicNode) Single-chain BSC or Polygon at old blocks Paid archive RPC (GetBlock ~$39/mo, QuickNode) Multi-chain bridge experiment Archive RPC for each chain in the topology Custom chain Your own archive node with eth_getBlockByNumber, eth_getCode, debug_traceTransaction support

docs.title: Resource Requirements
docs.text: Topology RAM Disk Time to Fork Single chain ~2 GB per Anvil process ~500 MB cache per chain 10-60s depending on RPC latency Dual chain (bridge) ~4 GB ~1 GB 20-120s Triple chain (3-chain bridge) ~6 GB ~1.5 GB 30-180s

docs.title: LLM Agent Experiments
docs.text:
If your experiment uses the AgentPlugin interface (LLM-driven autonomous agents):

Requirement Options LLM Provider OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama (local), or mock (no API key needed) Determinism Not guaranteed - LLM responses are inherently stochastic Artifact stream agent_reasoning.jsonl captures the full observe→think→act trace

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers Deployment Guide Installation and setup Tutorial Reproduce your first exploit in 10 minutes Fidelity Guide Detailed fidelity level reference (L0-L4) Attack Pack Guide How to create a new attack plugin RPC Provider Guide Archive RPC provider comparison and costs Benchmarks Results from the 6 built-in benchmark attack packs Troubleshooting Common errors and fixes

docs.title: Workspace
docs.text:
crucible init                              # create runtime directories
crucible doctor                            # validate environment (Python, Foundry, config, RPC, dirs, Streamlit, Docker)
crucible doctor --ports                    # validate environment + check port availability
crucible doctor --foundry --python         # targeted checks only
crucible doctor --fix                      # auto-fix common environment issues (broken symlinks, missing dirs)
crucible status                            # show running chains, determinism flags, block numbers
crucible status --disk                     # show disk usage breakdown per directory
crucible summary                           # one-shot workspace overview (chains, runs, disk usage)
crucible clean                             # stop chains only
crucible clean --cache                     # remove fork cache only
crucible clean --checkpoints               # remove experiment checkpoints only
crucible clean --states                    # remove non-baseline snapshots only
crucible clean --logs                      # remove all log run directories (scratch tier only; saved/published preserved)
crucible clean --logs-older-than 30        # remove logs older than N days (tier-protected)
crucible clean --datasets --confirm        # remove exported dataset bundles (.protected preserved)
crucible clean --all                       # runtime artifacts only: cache + checkpoints + snapshots.
                                           # Does NOT touch logs/ or datasets/ — those are
                                           # research artifacts with separate protection.
                                           # Use --logs or --datasets explicitly to clean them.
crucible instrument                        # standalone RPC proxy for ad-hoc tx instrumentation (also supports --upstream, --chain, --output-dir, --port, --timeout)
crucible notebook                          # launch Jupyter for experiment analysis (also supports --port, --notebooks-dir, --no-browser)
crucible dashboard                         # launch Streamlit dashboard
crucible --version                         # show version
crucible --dry-run <command>               # preview without executing

docs.title: Chains
docs.text:
crucible fork --topology single            # start Anvil for single chain
crucible fork --topology bridge            # start Anvil for dual chain + bridge
crucible fork --topology bridge --fork-timeout 600  # wait up to 600s for chains to become ready
crucible setup --topology bridge           # fork + run bootstrap.py + guidance to capture baseline
crucible setup --skip-bootstrap            # fork only, skip state preparation

docs.title: Snapshots And Baselines
docs.text:
Baseline: A fingerprint of the chain state at a specific fork block. It detects environment drift — if the RPC provider changed, the topology was tweaked, or the fork block shifted between runs, the baseline hash won't match and the experiment fails early, before wasting time on false results.

When to use --no-baseline: Skip baseline verification on first runs (no baseline exists yet) or during attack development. The baseline is captured after the first successful run.

crucible snapshot create <name>            # named snapshot of running chains
crucible snapshot create <name> --chain ethereum  # snapshot specific chain
crucible snapshot restore <name> --chain ethereum # restore specific chain

crucible baseline capture <scenario> --topology bridge  # capture golden baseline
crucible baseline verify <scenario>       # verify baseline fingerprint

crucible reset --scenario <scenario>                  # soft reset (anvil_loadState, <5s)
crucible reset --scenario <scenario> --mode medium    # kill + relaunch + restore (<30s)
crucible reset --scenario <scenario> --mode hard      # re-fork + re-bootstrap + restore (<5min)
crucible hard-reset --scenario <scenario>             # alias for --mode hard

docs.title: Plugins
docs.text:
crucible plugin list                       # all discovered plugins
crucible plugin list --type attack         # filter by type
crucible plugin list --type bridge         # filter by type (bridge)
crucible plugin validate attacks/demo_attack  # validate plugin manifest + interface
crucible plugin validate bridges/nomad_bridge  # validate bridge plugin + evidence.yaml
crucible plugin validate bridges/poly_network_bridge
crucible plugin validate bridges/wormhole_bridge

Bridge plugins deploy real Solidity contracts to forked chains and support the full lifecycle: deploy -> configure -> connect -> send_message -> reset -> destroy. Each requires plugin.yaml and evidence.yaml describing the protocol version, supported chains, modeled features, and known limitations.

Available bridge plugins: - nomad_bridge — Nomad Replica with committedRoot == 0x00 vulnerability (ethereum + polygon) - poly_network_bridge — EthCrossChainManager + EthCrossChainData hash collision (ethereum + polygon + bsc) - wormhole_bridge — WormholeCore + TokenBridge guardian bypass (ethereum + polygon)

docs.title: Experiments
docs.text:
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml           # formal run with baseline verification
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --no-baseline  # skip baseline check
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --publication-mode  # enforce publication gates
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --name "my-label"  # add human-readable alias
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --tier saved  # preserve: scratch|saved|published
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --no-auto-merge     # skip auto-merge of fork_block from evidence.yaml (keep current fork block)
