crucible experiment run experiments/my_experiment.yaml --no-baseline

Note: The instrumentation proxy works identically in blank fork mode. Same artifacts, same format, same integrity hashing.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text: Available Capabilities Unsupported Capabilities Instant startup, offline capable, deterministic Historical chain state (no pre-deployed contracts) 10 pre-funded Anvil accounts Real token balances or DeFi protocols Full instrumentation + artifact capture eth_getCode for real contracts (they don't exist) Deterministic diff verification L2 fidelity verification (requires archive fork)

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
A set of 7 research-quality checks that must pass before an experiment can be exported for publication. They enforce:

Gate What It Checks Evidence evidence.yaml has all required sections Fidelity Fidelity-level-specific requirements met Baseline Golden baseline exists and verifies Foundry Foundry version pinned Git Provenance Workspace has a known git commit Artifacts Complete All expected trace artifacts captured Provenance All provenance fields present

docs.title: Significance
docs.text:
• Quality assurance ΓÇö catches incomplete or non-reproducible experiments before publication
  • Reviewer trust ΓÇö gates create an auditable chain of checks
  • Self-documenting ΓÇö validation failures definitively highlight missing prerequisites

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
# Run with publication mode gates
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --publication-mode

If publication mode is enabled in config:

# testbed.yaml
publication:
  enabled: true
  require_foundry_pinned: true
  require_baseline_verified: true
  require_provenance_complete: true
  fidelity_enforcement: true

Check gate results in the experiment output. Each gate passes or fails with a clear message.

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
The fork cache stores Anvil's downloaded state so subsequent forks at the same block are instant. The CacheManager fingerprints each cache entry with:

Fingerprint Field What It Tracks Provider identity Which RPC provider was used (ankr, llamarpc, etc.) Fork block Which block was forked Chain ID Which chain Foundry version Which Foundry version created the cache Config hash Which testbed config was active

docs.title: Significance
docs.text:
• Cold cache ΓÇö no data cached ΓåÆ slow fork (30sΓÇô5min)
  • Warm cache ΓÇö data cached from a previous run ΓåÆ instant fork (<5s)
  • Stale cache ΓÇö cache exists but was created by a different provider/version ΓåÆ invalidated in strict mode

Cache poisoning (using wrong data from a different provider) was responsible for countless irreproducible results in other testbeds. Cache fingerprinting eliminates this.

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
# Inspect cache
crucible cache inspect

# Result shows every cached chain/block with provider, version, and size:
# ΓöîΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö¼ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö¼ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö¼ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö¼ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÉ
# Γöé Chain    Γöé Fork Block Γöé Provider Γöé Size   Γöé Mode     Γöé
# Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö╝ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö╝ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö╝ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö╝ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöñ

# Clear cache
crucible cache clean

# Compute cache fingerprint (for inclusion in run manifests)
crucible cache fingerprint

docs.title: Cache Policies
docs.text:
Policy Behavior permissive (default) Warns on mismatch but does not invalidate ΓÇö best for development strict Invalidates cache on any mismatch ΓÇö best for publication runs

Set in testbed.yaml:

fork_cache:
  cache_policy: strict

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Three ways to reset chain state, each with a different speed/fidelity tradeoff:

Tier Method Speed When to Use Soft anvil_loadState snapshot restore <5s Same-chain, same-block iteration Medium Kill + relaunch + restore <30s After Anvil process corruption Hard Re-fork from archive RPC <5min After topology change, cache corruption

docs.title: Significance
docs.text:
• Soft ΓÇö fastest, perfect for iterating on attack parameters
  • Medium ΓÇö recovers from process issues without re-fetching state
  • Hard ΓÇö nuclear option that guarantees clean state

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
# Three-tier reset: soft (<5s) → medium (<30s) → hard (<5min)
crucible reset --scenario single --mode soft
crucible reset --scenario single --mode medium
crucible reset --scenario single --mode hard
crucible hard-reset --scenario single          # alias for --mode hard

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
LLM-driven agents have configurable privilege levels that gate which tools they can access:

Level Access Tools Available LOW Read-only queries chain_id, get_balance, get_code, get_storage, call_contract, get_logs MEDIUM Impersonate + transact (default) Above + impersonate, send_transaction HIGH Full access Above + advance_time

docs.title: Significance
docs.text:
• Safety ΓÇö prevents autonomous agents from accidentally causing damage
  • Progressive disclosure ΓÇö start at LOW, increase as you gain confidence
  • Auditability ΓÇö every agent action is logged with its privilege level

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
# experiments/my_agent.yaml
run:
  agent_provider: openai
  agent_privilege: medium
  agent_allowed_accounts:
    - "0xattacker..."

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text: The bridge plugin system simulates cross-chain message passing. When a bridge plugin is deployed, messages sent on one chain can be relayed to another.

docs.title: Significance
docs.text:
• L4 reproductions ΓÇö reproduce multi-chain exploits that span Ethereum, BSC, and Polygon
  • Bridge protocol validation ΓÇö test how bridge contracts handle forged messages
  • Cross-chain artifact tracking ΓÇö cross_chain.jsonl correlates messages across chains

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
Use a bridge topology:

# experiments/my_experiment.yaml
name: my_experiment
scenario: my_scenario
attack: my_attack
topology: nomad_bridge    # Uses nomad_bridge topology

Configure the topology:

# topologies/nomad_bridge.yaml
name: nomad_bridge
chains: [ethereum, polygon]
bridge: nomad_bridge

From an attack plugin:

bridge = context.bridge_plugin
result = bridge.send_message(
    context=context,
    source_chain="ethereum",
    dest_chain="polygon",
    data="0xforged_calldata",
    correlation_id="msg-001",
)

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text: A self-contained directory with all experiment artifacts, integrity manifests, and reproduction instructions. Suitable for publication, archiving, or sharing with reviewers.

docs.title: Bundle Structure
docs.text:
datasets/DS-2026-00042/
Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ run_manifest.json           # Provenance + outcome
Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ log_integrity.json          # SHA-256 hashes of all streams
Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ artifact_bundle_manifest.json  # SHA-256 of every file
Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ report.md                   # Human-readable summary
Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ REPRODUCE.md                # Step-by-step reproduction guide
Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ run.jsonl                   # Lifecycle events
Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ chain_ethereum/             # Per-chain artifacts
Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ events.jsonl
Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ gas_profile.jsonl
Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ storage_diffs.jsonl
Γöé   ΓööΓöÇΓöÇ balance_diffs.jsonl
ΓööΓöÇΓöÇ chain_bsc/
    ΓööΓöÇΓöÇ ...

docs.title: Significance
docs.text:
• Self-verifying ΓÇö SHA-256 hashes detect tampering
  • Self-contained ΓÇö everything needed to verify the reproduction
  • Portable ΓÇö can be uploaded to Zenodo, sent to reviewers, or archived

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
# Export a run
crucible export DS-2026-00042

# Verify the bundle
crucible verify datasets/DS-2026-00042

# Publish (mark as permanent)
crucible publish DS-2026-00042

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers Technical Overview System architecture and core concepts Design Decisions Why each feature was built this way Plugin Development Guide Using features from plugin code User Guide Getting started and daily usage Troubleshooting Common blockers and fixes

docs.title: Fidelity Guide
docs.text: Purpose: Understand Crucible's 5-level fidelity system — what each level guarantees, when to enforce it, and what to do when enforcement fails. This guide helps researchers decide the right fidelity level for their attack pack and debug fidelity violations.

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
Level What It Means When To Use When To Skip (--no-fidelity) L0 Narrative only — no reproduction code Research notes, incident descriptions N/A (always passes) L1 Conceptual — attack plugin returns a result Initial attack development, prototype First few iterations of a new attack L2 Fork-state behavioral — executes on a forked chain Most reproductions — standard benchmark target During early plugin development L3 Exact transaction replay — replays the exploit tx Formal reproductions, publication Demo/simulation experiments L4 Multi-chain full — cross-chain with bridge plugin Full bridge exploit reproductions Bridge demo experiments (e.g., nomad_bridge_l4)

Golden rule: Use the LOWEST fidelity level that truthfully describes your experiment. L2 is sufficient for most reproductions. Only use L3/L4 if you actually replay exact transactions or use a multi-chain bridge topology.

docs.title: L0 — Narrative (always passes)
docs.text:
No runtime checks. The experiment is a description or research note, not a reproduction.

What's enforced: Nothing.

Typical use case: Writing an incident report, documenting research findings, or planning an attack pack.

docs.title: L1 — Conceptual (attack plugin ran)
docs.text:
What's enforced: - The attack plugin's execute() returned a non-None result

What passes without this level: A plugin that crashes before returning a result.

What's NOT checked: Whether the result was success=True or success=False. L1 only checks that the plugin ran.

AgentPlugin note: LLM-driven agent experiments don't produce AttackResult — the fidelity check will fail for them. Always use --no-fidelity for agent experiments.

docs.title: L2 — Fork-State Behavioral (standard)
docs.text:
What's enforced: - All L1 checks - historical_scope.chains in evidence.yaml is non-empty - Each declared chain exists in the active topology - Trace artifacts exist (events.jsonl, gas_profile.jsonl, or storage_diffs.jsonl) - No transactions failed auto-instrumentation

Common failure reasons and fixes:

Error Message Cause Fix Fidelity level L2 requires historical_scope.chains to be non-empty in evidence.yaml Evidence missing chain list Add chains: under historical_scope: in evidence.yaml Chain 'X' declared in evidence.yaml is not in the active topology Evidence says chain X but topology doesn't have it Either add chain X to topology, or remove from evidence Fidelity level L2 requires fork-state execution evidence... No artifact streams found — attack did not interact with the forked chain. Attack didn't send any transactions Ensure the plugin calls send_transaction() or eth_call() Fidelity level L2 requires complete trace artifacts, but N transaction(s) failed auto-instrumentation debug_traceTransaction failed for some txs Check Anvil version; older nightlies may not support the tracer combo

docs.title: L3 — Exact Transaction Replay
docs.text:
What's enforced: - All L2 checks - historical_scope.exploit_transactions in evidence.yaml is non-empty - reproduction_scope.exact_tx_replay is true - At least one transaction action found in run.jsonl

Common failure reasons and fixes:

Error Message Cause Fix Fidelity level L3 requires historical_scope.exploit_transactions to be non-empty No exploit tx hashes in evidence Add the real exploit tx hash(es) from Etherscan Fidelity level L3 requires reproduction_scope.exact_tx_replay to be true Flag not set Set exact_tx_replay: true in evidence.yaml Fidelity level L3 requires transaction execution evidence. No transaction actions found in run.jsonl. Attack didn't send any txs, or ran in a different run dir Check that the plugin actually calls send_transaction()

docs.title: L4 — Multi-Chain Full
docs.text:
What's enforced: - All L3 checks - historical_scope.chains has at least 2 chains - reproduction_scope.excluded_components is declared - Topology has a bridge plugin configured

Common failure reasons and fixes:

Error Message Cause Fix Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires at least 2 chains in historical_scope.chains Only 1 chain declared Add the second chain to chains: Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires reproduction_scope.excluded_components to be declared in evidence.yaml No excluded_components field Add realistic exclusions (e.g., off-chain relayer, non-EVM state) Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires a bridge plugin in the topology configuration Topology has no bridge Use a bridge topology: nomad_bridge, poly_network_bridge, or wormhole_bridge

docs.title: When To Use --no-fidelity
docs.text: The --no-fidelity flag skips all fidelity enforcement — the experiment runs to completion regardless of whether the runtime behavior matches the declared fidelity level.

docs.title: Use --no-fidelity when:
docs.text: Situation Why Demo experiments (nomad_bridge_l4, demo_agent) Demo experiments are simulations — they don't need to meet formal fidelity requirements During attack development You're writing a new plugin and iterating quickly — fidelity checks would slow you down The declared fidelity level is aspirational Your evidence.yaml says L4 but you haven't implemented full multi-chain yet Archive limitations prevent L2+ Polygon/BSC archives can't serve state for all chains — you get partial results LLM agent experiments Agent behavior is non-deterministic — fidelity checks on agent runs are meaningless Skipping baseline on first run First run has no baseline yet — but fidelity is unrelated to baselines

docs.title: Do NOT use --no-fidelity when:
docs.text: Situation Why You're publishing a formal reproduction Reviewers expect the declared fidelity to be verified Running benchmark comparisons All benchmarks should meet their declared level for fair comparison Debugging a failing experiment Fidelity failures tell you WHAT went wrong — skipping them hides the root cause

docs.title: How to specify:
docs.text:
# Skip fidelity enforcement
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_bridge_l4.yaml --no-fidelity

# Keep fidelity enforcement (default)
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml

# Keep fidelity enforcement explicitly (enabled by default)
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml

docs.title: When fidelity PASSES
docs.text:
[ok] runtime fidelity verified

This means the experiment's runtime behavior matches the declared L2/L3/L4 level. The manifest will contain:

"fidelity_check": {
  "passed": true,
  "declared_level": "L2",
  "failures": [],
  "warnings": []
}

docs.title: When fidelity FAILS
docs.text:
[fail] runtime fidelity FAILED
         • Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires at least 2 chains in historical_scope.chains
         • Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires a bridge plugin in the topology configuration

The reasons are printed directly below the FAILED line — no need to dig through logs. The manifest also stores the full details:

"fidelity_check": {
  "passed": false,
  "declared_level": "L4",
  "failures": [
    "Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires at least 2 chains in historical_scope.chains",
    "Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires a bridge plugin in the topology configuration"
  ],
  "warnings": []
}

The experiment still writes the manifest — it doesn't crash at fidelity failure. The outcome is set to "failure" with exit code 7 (assertion failed).

docs.title: When you should read the run.jsonl
docs.text:
If you see [fail] runtime fidelity FAILED, your next action depends on what the error says:

  1. Evidence.yaml is missing fields → Open attacks/<name>/evidence.yaml and add them
  2. No trace artifacts found → Your attack didn't send transactions — is that expected?
  3. Missing chain/topology → Your topology doesn't match what evidence.yaml declares
  4. No transaction actions → The attack ran but didn't interact with the chain

If fidelity fails but you're okay with that (e.g., you're developing the attack), rerun with --no-fidelity.

docs.title: How Fidelity Differs From Other Checks
docs.text:
Check What It Does When It Runs Can You Skip It? Fidelity enforcement Validates runtime vs declared level After attack plugin runs --no-fidelity Baseline verification Validates fork state matches golden fingerprint Before experiment starts --no-baseline Evidence pre-flight Validates contracts have code on running chains Before attack plugin runs Cannot skip Publication gates Validates evidence, baseline, and provenance completeness Before experiment (if publication mode) Only without --publication-mode

Key insight: Fidelity is the LAST check before the manifest is written. If everything else passes but fidelity fails, the experiment still completes — it just records a failure.

docs.title: Designing An Attack Pack's Fidelity Level
When choosing a level, ask:
docs.text:
1. Is this a full reproduction? → L2+ (need fork state)
  2. Are you replaying the exact exploit transaction? → L3+ (need calldata reconstruction)
  3. Does the exploit span multiple chains? → L4 (need bridge topology)
  4. Is this a simulation or demonstration? → L2 without --no-fidelity, or L0-L1

docs.title: Retrospective upgrades:
docs.text:
# Convert L2 → L3: Reconstruct exact calldata
crucible attack extract <tx_hash> --name my_attack --chain ethereum --rpc-url <archive_rpc>

# Convert L3 → L4: Add bridge topology
# Use nomad_bridge, poly_network_bridge, or wormhole_bridge topology
# Update evidence.yaml: add bridge chain, excluded_components

Important: Don't claim a higher fidelity than you've actually implemented. Reviewers verify fidelity claims against your code and artifacts.

docs.title: Reference: All Fidelity Error Messages
docs.text: Every possible fidelity violation, what it means, and what to do:

docs.title: L1 Errors (1 possible)
docs.text:
Attack plugin returned no result — cannot verify fidelity
Meaning: The plugin's execute() returned None. Either it crashed, or it's an AgentPlugin. Fix: Ensure execute() returns an AttackResult. For agent experiments, the fidelity check is implicitly skipped.

docs.title: L2 Errors (4 possible)
docs.text:
"Fidelity level L2 requires historical_scope.chains to be non-empty in evidence.yaml"
Fix: Add chains: [ethereum] (or whatever chain you're using) under historical_scope in evidence.yaml.

"Chain 'X' declared in evidence.yaml is not in the active topology"
Fix: Either add chain X to the topology, or remove it from evidence.yaml's chains list.

"Fidelity level L2 requires fork-state execution evidence (events.jsonl, gas_profile.jsonl, or storage_diffs.jsonl). No artifact streams found"
Fix: Ensure your attack plugin actually sends transactions. The instrumentation proxy creates artifact files when it intercepts RPC calls.

"Fidelity level L2 requires complete trace artifacts, but N transaction(s) failed auto-instrumentation"
Fix: Check run.jsonl for failed auto_trace entries. This is often an Anvil version issue — some nightly builds fail with prestateTracer + diffMode.

docs.title: L3 Errors (3 possible)
docs.text:
"Fidelity level L3 requires historical_scope.exploit_transactions to be non-empty in evidence.yaml"
Fix: Add the real exploit transaction hash(es) from Etherscan.

"Fidelity level L3 requires reproduction_scope.exact_tx_replay to be true in evidence.yaml"
Fix: Set exact_tx_replay: true under reproduction_scope.

"Fidelity level L3 requires transaction execution evidence. No transaction actions found in run.jsonl."
Fix: Ensure the attack sends at least one transaction that gets recorded in run.jsonl.

docs.title: L4 Errors (3 possible)
docs.text:
"Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires at least 2 chains in historical_scope.chains"
Fix: Add the second chain (e.g., polygon) to evidence.yaml's chains list.

"Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires reproduction_scope.excluded_components to be declared in evidence.yaml"
Fix: Add excluded_components: with realistic off-chain limitations.

"Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires a bridge plugin in the topology configuration"
Fix: Use a bridge topology (nomad_bridge, poly_network_bridge, wormhole_bridge). Plain single/dual topologies don't have bridge plugins.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers docs/ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md Creating attack plugins — including fidelity level setup docs/CLI_REFERENCE.md Fidelity enforcement and --no-fidelity flag docs/REPRODUCIBILITY.md Provenance and determinism docs/TROUBLESHOOTING.md Common errors and fixes

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The instrumentation layer is an RPC proxy that sits transparently between any code that talks to the local Anvil fork (attack packs, Foundry scripts, LLM agents, manual cast send calls) and the Anvil process itself.

Every transaction is automatically traced — the attack pack does zero artifact collection work.

docs.title: Data Flow Diagram
docs.text:
flowchart TB
    ARCHIVE["Archive RPC Provider<br/>eth.llamarpc.com / ankr / publicnode"]

    subgraph Researcher["Researcher"]
        AP["Attack Pack<br/>(Python Plugin)"]
        FS["Foundry Script<br/>(forge script)"]
        LLM["LLM Agent<br/>(future — dashed outline)"]
        CS["cast send / curl"]
    end

    subgraph Crucible["Crucible Testbed"]
        ER["ExperimentRunner"]
        CM["ChainManager"]
        subgraph Proxy["RpcProxy (per chain)"]
            HS["HTTP Server<br/>127.0.0.1:random"]
            EX["ThreadPoolExecutor<br/>max 10 workers"]
        end
        LE["LogEmitter"]
        DIFF["diff.py compare_runs"]
    end

    subgraph Anvil["Anvil Fork (local)"]
        ANV["anvil --fork-url <ARCHIVE_RPC>"]
    end

    subgraph Artifacts["Artifact Streams"]
        GAS["gas_profile.jsonl"]
        EVT["events.jsonl"]
        STO["storage_diffs.jsonl"]
        BAL["balance_diffs.jsonl"]
    end

    subgraph Downstream["Downstream"]
        REPORT["report.md"]
        INTEGRITY["log_integrity.json"]
        DIFF_OUT["storage_equal<br/>events_equal<br/>gas_equal<br/>balances_equal"]
    end

    ARCHIVE -->|eth_getBlockByNumber, eth_getStorageAt| ANV

    ER --> CM
    CM -->|start_instrumentation| Proxy
    ER -->|attack.run| AP

    AP -->|JSON-RPC via proxy_url| HS
    FS -->|JSON-RPC via proxy_url| HS
    LLM -.->|JSON-RPC via proxy_url| HS
    CS -->|JSON-RPC via proxy_url| HS

    HS -->|forward all requests| ANV
    ANV -->|response| HS
    HS -->|response| Researcher

    HS -->|eth_sendTransaction detected| EX
    EX -->|trace_async| ANV
    ANV -->|receipt + debug_traceTransaction| EX
    EX -->|gas_used| LE
    EX -->|events| LE
    EX -->|storage_diffs| LE
    EX -->|balance_diffs| LE

    LE --> GAS
    LE --> EVT
    LE --> STO
    LE --> BAL

    GAS --> DIFF
    EVT --> DIFF
    STO --> DIFF
    BAL --> DIFF

    LE --> REPORT
    LE --> INTEGRITY
    DIFF --> DIFF_OUT
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

Legend: Solid arrow = synchronous call · Dashed arrow = planned/future · Thick arrow = data artifact

docs.title: Blank Fork Mode — Zero Remote RPC Dependency
docs.text: The diagrams above show the archive fork path: Anvil fetches historical state from a remote archive RPC provider. However, the instrumentation proxy is completely agnostic to how Anvil was started — it only cares that Anvil is listening on an HTTP port.

docs.title: How Blank Forks Work
docs.text:
In blank fork mode (fork_block: null), Anvil starts without --fork-url:

# Archive fork: Anvil fetches remote state
anvil --fork-url https://eth.llamarpc.com --fork-block-number 15259100 --accounts 0 --balance 0

# Blank fork: No remote dependency — Anvil is a standalone local EVM
anvil --accounts 0 --balance 0

The proxy forwards to whatever URL it's given — it does not inspect or care about the upstream chain state. The same RpcProxy class, the same attach_instrumentation() call, the same ThreadPoolExecutor background tracing. No code changes, no config changes.

docs.title: Data Flow (Blank Fork)
docs.text:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Researcher["Researcher"]
        AP["Attack Pack<br/>(Python Plugin)"]
        FS["Foundry Script<br/>(forge script)"]
        CS["cast send / curl"]
    end

    subgraph Crucible["Crucible Testbed"]
        ER["ExperimentRunner"]
        CM["ChainManager"]
        subgraph Proxy["RpcProxy (per chain)"]
            HS["HTTP Server<br/>127.0.0.1:random"]
            EX["ThreadPoolExecutor<br/>max 10 workers"]
        end
        LE["LogEmitter"]
    end

    subgraph Anvil["Anvil (local, no fork)"]
        ANV["anvil --accounts 0 --balance 0<br/>(standalone EVM — no --fork-url)"]
    end

    subgraph Artifacts["Artifact Streams"]
        GAS["gas_profile.jsonl"]
        EVT["events.jsonl"]
        STO["storage_diffs.jsonl"]
        BAL["balance_diffs.jsonl"]
    end

    ER --> CM
    CM -->|start_instrumentation| Proxy
    ER -->|attack.run| AP

    AP -->|JSON-RPC via proxy_url| HS
    FS -->|JSON-RPC via proxy_url| HS
    CS -->|JSON-RPC via proxy_url| HS

    HS -->|forward all requests| ANV
    ANV -->|response| HS
    HS -->|response| Researcher

    HS -->|eth_sendTransaction detected| EX
    EX -->|trace_async| ANV
    ANV -->|receipt + debug_traceTransaction| EX
    EX -->|gas_used| LE
    EX -->|events| LE
    EX -->|storage_diffs| LE
    EX -->|balance_diffs| LE

    LE --> GAS
    LE --> EVT
    LE --> STO
    LE --> BAL
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

Notice what's missing: The "Archive RPC Provider" box and the eth_getBlockByNumber/ eth_getStorageAt arrows. They simply don't exist in blank fork mode. Everything else — the proxy, the artifact streams, the trace pipeline — is identical to archive fork mode.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
Property Archive Fork Blank Fork Remote RPC needed? Yes — archive provider No — fully offline Startup time 30s–5 min (cold cache) <1s — instant Determinism Depends on archive state Fully deterministic CI/hermetic testable Needs fixture replay Yes — directly Artifact collection Same proxy + streams Same proxy + streams Fidelity levels L2 (verified fork state) L3/L4 (validation, no history)

The artifact collection pipeline is identical in both modes. The instrumented data — gas, events, storage diffs, balance diffs — has the same format, the same integrity hashing, the same diffability. The only difference is whether Anvil fetches historical state at startup.

docs.title: Practical Use Cases
docs.text:
• L4 validation experiments (l4_full_validation, nomad_bridge_l4): Two blank forks (ethereum + polygon), bridge plugin deployed on both, bidirectional cross-chain messaging. Zero RPC calls during the entire run — the proxy only talks to local Anvil instances.
  • Offline demo/testing: Run crucible experiment run with blank forks on a plane, in CI, or on an air-gapped machine. Artifact collection works identically.
  • Development workflow: Iterate on attack packs without waiting for archive forks. Switch to archive forks only when ready for L2 fidelity verification.

Important caveat: Blank forks can fail on first startup due to an Anvil nightly-build quirk with --accounts 0 --balance 0. See Troubleshooting §7C and Testbed Scope Guide — Blank Fork Caveats for recovery steps.

docs.title: Sequence: Experiment Run with Instrumentation
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant ER as ExperimentRunner
    participant CM as ChainManager
    participant SC as ScenarioEngine
    participant PX as RpcProxy
    participant AN as Anvil Fork
    participant ARC as Archive RPC
    participant AK as Attack Pack
    participant LE as LogEmitter

    ER->>SC: run(scenario)
    SC->>CM: start_topology()
    CM->>AN: spawn anvil --fork-block 15259100
    AN->>ARC: fetch historical state (lazy, on-demand)
    ARC-->>AN: accounts, storage, code
    AN-->>CM: ready at http://127.0.0.1:8545
    CM-->>SC: runtime
    SC-->>ER: done

    ER->>CM: start_instrumentation("ethereum", log)
    CM->>CM: read meta file for upstream URL
    CM->>PX: attach_instrumentation(upstream, log)
    PX->>PX: start HTTP server on 127.0.0.1:random
    PX-->>CM: proxy_url = http://127.0.0.1:port
    Note over AK,AN: Now runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url == proxy_url

    ER->>AK: run(context)

    AK->>PX: eth_sendTransaction({...})
    PX->>AN: forward eth_sendTransaction
    AN-->>PX: tx_hash
    PX-->>AK: tx_hash

    par Background Trace (via ThreadPoolExecutor)
        PX->>AN: poll eth_getTransactionReceipt(tx_hash)
        AN-->>PX: receipt {gasUsed, logs, status}
        PX->>AN: debug_traceTransaction(tx_hash, prestateTracer + diffMode)
        AN-->>PX: {pre: {accounts}, post: {accounts}}
        PX->>LE: gas(step="auto_tx", gas_used=21000)
        PX->>LE: event(chain, event_name="Transfer", ...)
        PX->>LE: storage_diff(chain, contract, slot, before, after)
        PX->>LE: balance_diff(chain, contract, account, before, after)
    end

    AK-->>ER: AttackResult(success=true)

    ER->>CM: stop_instrumentation()
    CM->>PX: drain() — wait for pending traces
    PX->>PX: executor.shutdown(wait=True, timeout=10s)
    CM->>PX: stop() — shutdown HTTP server

    ER->>LE: write_report(manifest_path)
    ER->>LE: write_integrity() — hashes + closes handles
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

docs.title: Integration Points
docs.text: Component Role File RpcProxy HTTP proxy + background tracer (gas, events, storage diffs, balance) crucible/instrumentation.py ChainManager Starts/stops proxy per chain; runtime_for() returns proxy URL transparently crucible/chain_manager.py ExperimentRunner Starts proxy before attack, drains/stops in finally block crucible/experiment.py LogEmitter Receives traced artifacts into typed JSONL streams crucible/logging.py diff.py Compares artifact streams across runs for determinism check crucible/diff.py

docs.title: Proxy URL Transparency
docs.text:
# Before instrumentation:
runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url  # → "http://127.0.0.1:8545"

# After start_instrumentation("ethereum", log):
runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url  # → "http://127.0.0.1:54321" (proxy)

All existing code continues to work unchanged — the proxy is invisible to attack packs, scenario steps, and bridge plugins.

docs.title: Standalone Mode (crucible instrument)
docs.text:
Beyond experiment-mode auto-instrumentation, crucible instrument starts the proxy standalone — no fork, no experiment runner, no scenario. This is useful for ad-hoc research where you:

  1. Start an Anvil fork manually (or use an existing one)
  2. Point the proxy at it with --upstream
  3. Send transactions through the proxy URL (via cast send, Foundry scripts, Python scripts)
  4. Get complete artifact streams without writing any instrumentation code
# Terminal 1: Start Anvil manually
anvil --fork-url https://eth.llamarpc.com --fork-block-number 15259100

# Terminal 2: Start the instrumentation proxy
crucible instrument --upstream http://127.0.0.1:8545 --chain ethereum --output-dir ./logs/my_run

# Terminal 3: Send transactions through the proxy
cast send --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:<proxy-port> 0xTarget --value 1ether

docs.title: Standalone Options
docs.text:
Flag Default Description --upstream, -u http://127.0.0.1:8545 Upstream RPC URL to proxy (typically the Anvil fork) --chain, -c ethereum Chain name for artifact tagging and subdirectory naming --output-dir, -o ./instrument_output Directory to write artifact streams into --port, -p 0 (random) Proxy listen port. Use a specific port for scripting/chaining --timeout, -t 0 (run until Ctrl+C) Auto-exit after N seconds. Useful for scripted runs

Artifacts are written to --output-dir with the same JSONL stream structure as experiment-mode captures.

docs.title: Thread Safety
docs.text: Concern Solution Multi-chain topologies _make_handler() factory creates a fresh handler class per proxy instance — each chain's proxy forwards to its own Anvil independently Rapid-fire tx loops ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=10) bounds concurrent traces; excess txs queue Race with LogEmitter.close() drain() → stop() ordering: executor shutdown waits for pending traces, then HTTP server stops, then write_integrity() closes handles

docs.title: Fallback Behavior
docs.text:
When debug_traceTransaction is unavailable or returns an error:

Artifact Captured? Source Gas used Yes eth_getTransactionReceipt Events / logs Yes eth_getTransactionReceipt Storage diffs Warning logged N/A — no trace available Balance changes Warning logged N/A — no trace available

A prominent failure step is logged showing the tx hash, what was lost (storage diffs + balance changes), what was saved (gas + events), and a reference to TROUBLESHOOTING.md for Anvil version advice.

docs.title: System Maintenance & Architecture Guide
docs.text: This document outlines the architectural map, internal directory structure, testing procedures, and technical boundaries for the Crucible testbed. It serves as the authoritative reference for maintaining and extending the core system.

docs.title: 1. Project Overview
docs.text:
Crucible is a modular EVM bridge security research testbed designed for deterministic exploit reproduction and rigorous artifact collection. Its primary capabilities include:

  • EVM State Forking: Forking any EVM chain at any historical block via Foundry/Anvil.
  • Deterministic Replay: Executing historical bridge exploits in a controlled, isolated environment.
  • Structured Artifact Collection: Emitting canonical JSONL logs, state diffs, and gas profiles.
  • Rapid Environment Reset: Utilizing a 3-tier state reset (soft, medium, hard) for rapid iteration.
  • Baseline Verification: Capturing and verifying golden baseline fingerprints to prevent environmental drift.

docs.title: Architecture at a Glance
docs.text:
CLI (crucible/cli.py → crucible/commands/*.py)
  │
  ├── Chain Manager — Orchestrates Anvil subprocesses and node lifecycle
  ├── Scenario Engine — Parses and executes discrete scenario steps
  ├── Experiment Runner — Manages formal research runs and baseline enforcement
  ├── Plugin Registry — Discovers, loads, and validates plugins
  ├── Baseline System — Captures golden baselines and verifies environment integrity
  ├── Structured Logger — Streams 5 distinct JSONL research artifacts
  └── Dashboard (Streamlit) — Provides a graphical interface for orchestration

docs.title: Key Subsystems
docs.text: File / Module Responsibility crucible/cli.py Command-line interface entry point. crucible/config.py Strict YAML configuration parsing via Pydantic. crucible/chain_manager.py Local blockchain (Anvil) process and state lifecycle management. crucible/rpc.py Resilient JSON-RPC client featuring provider failover and rate limiting. crucible/baseline.py Baseline capture, fingerprint computation, and state restoration. crucible/scenario.py Scenario YAML parsing and discrete step execution. crucible/experiment.py Formal experiment orchestration, matrix running, and checkpoint resumption. crucible/logging.py JSONL emitter for deterministic artifact generation. crucible/plugin.py Dynamic plugin discovery and schema validation. crucible/sdk.py Plugin interface definitions (AttackPlugin, BridgePlugin, etc.). crucible/diff.py Canonicalized comparison of experiment runs.

docs.title: 2. Directory Structure
docs.text: The repository is organized into distinct domains separating core execution logic from research artifacts and operational configurations.

docs.title: Root Directory
docs.text: Directory / File Purpose crucible/ Core Python package containing orchestration, execution, and instrumentation logic. dashboard/ Streamlit browser application and UI panels. docs/ Formal project documentation and design specifications. attacks/ Concrete attack plugins and exploit reproductions. scenarios/ Scenario definitions (setup and pre-exploit state configurations). experiments/ Formal experiment YAML files linking topologies, scenarios, and attacks. topologies/ Network topology profiles (e.g., single-chain, dual-chain, multi-chain bridge). abis/ Contract ABIs organized by protocol. bridges/ Infrastructure plugins modeling cross-chain message passing (e.g., Nomad, Wormhole). tests/ Unit and integration test suites. scripts/ Shell utilities for artifact evaluation and cross-provider reproduction. datasets/ Exported publication bundles and verifiable artifact archives (gitignored). logs/ Ephemeral experiment run logs and traces (gitignored). states/ Serialized Anvil state files for baseline management (gitignored except golden baselines). .fork_cache/ Local RPC response caching to minimize external provider load (gitignored). bootstrap.py Custom state preparation and deterministic seeding logic.

docs.title: 3. Testing Procedures
docs.text:
The testbed includes comprehensive unit testing to ensure deterministic behavior and core system integrity.

# Execute standard unit test suite
pytest tests/unit/ -q

# Execute with verbose output
pytest tests/unit/ -v

# Execute specific subsystems
pytest tests/unit/test_config.py -v

# Execute with coverage reporting
pytest tests/unit/ --cov=crucible

# Validate internal documentation link integrity
pytest tests/unit/test_docs_links.py -v

Note on Integration Testing: Integration and scenario tests housed in tests/integration/ require a live Anvil instance and valid RPC provider configuration. They are deliberately segregated from the fast unit test suite.

docs.title: 4. Extension and Authoring
docs.text:
The testbed is designed for extensibility. Refer to the specific guides for step-by-step authoring procedures:

  • Adding Attack Packs: Refer to the Attack Pack Guide and use the crucible attack extract <tx_hash> utility.
  • Adding Plugins: Refer to the Plugin Development Guide for implementing the AttackPlugin or BridgePlugin interfaces.
  • Adding Topologies: Define new .yaml profiles in the topologies/ directory.
  • CLI Usage: Refer to the CLI Reference for operational commands.

docs.title: 5. System Constraints & Technical Boundaries
docs.text: Transparency regarding the testbed's limitations is a critical requirement for rigorous research.

docs.title: Infrastructure Limitations
docs.text:
• EVM-Only Execution: The Crucible testbed explicitly models the Ethereum Virtual Machine. Attacks involving non-EVM execution environments (e.g., Solana, Harmony) are restricted to modeling the EVM-side consequences (e.g., forged VAA acceptance).
  • Archive Node Depth: Free-tier archive RPC providers strictly limit historical block depth. Simulating deep historical exploits on high-throughput chains (e.g., BSC, Polygon) necessitates paid provider tiers.
  • Provider Rate Limits: Standard multi-chain setups will quickly encounter HTTP 429 errors on free-tier providers. The testbed's crucible.rpc subsystem employs exponential backoff, but dedicated API keys are recommended for complex matrix runs.

docs.title: Operational Boundaries
docs.text:
• Publication Workflows: Ensuring full reproducibility requires formal baseline verification. Utilizing --no-baseline invalidates the deterministic guarantee and should only be used during local development.
  • Cross-Platform Compatibility: While the crucible Python CLI is cross-platform, certain artifact evaluation workflows housed in scripts/ utilize Bash and necessitate a POSIX-compliant environment (Linux, macOS, or WSL).

docs.title: Plugin Development Guide
docs.text: Audience: Researchers and developers writing attack, bridge, or agent plugins for Crucible.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Crucible supports three plugin types:

Type Base Class Purpose Attack AttackPlugin Reproduce a historical exploit Bridge BridgePlugin Simulate a cross-chain bridge protocol Agent AgentPlugin Create an LLM-driven autonomous agent

All plugins are Python classes that subclass the appropriate base class from crucible.sdk. They are discovered automatically by the PluginRegistry by scanning attacks/, bridges/, and plugins/ directories.

docs.title: Plugin Structure
docs.text:
Every plugin follows this directory convention:

attacks/my_exploit/
â”œâ”€â”€ plugin.yaml          # Plugin manifest (required)
â”œâ”€â”€ evidence.yaml        # Evidence declaration (required for attacks)
â”œâ”€â”€ my_exploit.py        # Plugin implementation
â””â”€â”€ ...                  # Additional files (ABI, bytecode, etc.)

docs.title: Plugin Manifest (plugin.yaml)
docs.text:
Crucible supports a modern manifest format that clearly declares dependencies. (The legacy format with class_name and chains_required is deprecated but still supported).

name: my_exploit
version: 1.0.0
type: attack           # attack | bridge | agent
entry: my_exploit.py
class: MyExploit       # The Python class name

# Optional metadata
author: "Security Researcher"
reproduction: {}       # Reserved for reproduction-specific metadata

# Dependency Requirements
requires:
  crucible: ">=0.1.0"
  chains: [ethereum]   # Required chains to run this plugin
  topology: single     # Required topology (e.g., single, bridge)

docs.title: Evidence Declaration (evidence.yaml)
docs.text:
Every attack plugin must include an evidence.yaml:

incident:
  name: "My Exploit"
  date: "2024-01-01"
  affected_bridge: "MyBridge"
  funds_lost_usd: 1000000
  summary: "Description of the vulnerability"

sources:
  advisories:
    - "https://example.com/advisory"
  postmortems:
    - "https://example.com/postmortem"

historical_scope:
  chains: ["ethereum"]
  fork_blocks:
    ethereum: 15000000
  exploit_transactions:
    - "0xabc..."
  affected_contracts:
    VulnerableContract: "0x123..."
  attacker_addresses:
    - "0xabc..."

reproduction_scope:
  fidelity_level: L2
  exact_tx_replay: false
  modeled_components:
    - "Fork-state call to vulnerable contract"
  excluded_components:
    - "Non-EVM chain state"

acceptance:
  required_assertions:
    - name: vulnerability_exists
      description: "The vulnerable state exists at fork block"
      expected: true
    - name: exploit_succeeds
      description: "The exploit transaction does not revert"
      expected: true

docs.title: Step 1: Scaffold
docs.text:
# Create from a real exploit transaction (populates evidence.yaml from on-chain data)
crucible attack extract 0xabc... --name my_exploit

# Or create from template (empty scaffold)
crucible attack init my_exploit --chain ethereum --fidelity L2 --author "Your Name"

docs.title: Step 2: Implement the Plugin
docs.text:
from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient
from crucible.sdk import AttackPlugin, AttackResult, ExperimentContext, VerificationResult


class MyExploit(AttackPlugin):
    def metadata(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "name": "my_exploit",
            "version": "1.0.0",
            "type": "attack",
            "fidelity_level": "L2",
            "damage_usd": 1000000,
        }

    def execute(self, context: ExperimentContext, phase: int | None = None) -> AttackResult:
        # 1. Get a client for the forked chain
        rpc_url = context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url
        client = JsonRpcClient(rpc_url)

        # 2. Check contract exists
        code = client.get_code("0x123...")
        if not code or code == "0x":
            return AttackResult(success=False, phase_results=[
                {"step": "check_contract", "passed": False, "detail": "no code"}
            ])

        # 3. Forge exploit calldata
        calldata = "0x..."

        # 4. Send exploit transaction
        tx_hash = client.call("eth_sendTransaction", [{
            "to": "0x123...",
            "data": calldata,
        }])

        # 5. Check result
        receipt = client.call("eth_getTransactionReceipt", [tx_hash])
        success = receipt and receipt.get("status") == "0x1"

        return AttackResult(success=success, phase_results=[
            {"step": "check_contract", "passed": True},
            {"step": "send_exploit", "passed": success},
        ])

    def verify(self, context: ExperimentContext) -> VerificationResult:
        client = JsonRpcClient(context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url)

        # Check on-chain state matches expectations
        balance = client.get_balance("0xattacker...")
        assertions = []
        errors = []

        if balance > 0:
            assertions.append({"name": "attacker_balance", "passed": True})
        else:
            errors.append("attacker balance did not increase")

        return VerificationResult(
            passed=len(errors) == 0,
            assertions=assertions,
            errors=errors,
        )

docs.title: Step 3: Create the Experiment
docs.text:
# experiments/my_exploit.yaml
name: my_exploit
scenario: my_scenario
attack: my_exploit
topology: single

docs.title: Step 4: Create the Scenario
docs.text:
# scenarios/my_scenario/scenario.yaml
phases:
  - name: setup
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Setting up exploit environment"
      - type: impersonate
        chain: ethereum
        account: "0xattacker..."
      - type: assert_balance
        chain: ethereum
        account: "0xattacker..."
        min_value: "1000000000000000000"  # 1 ETH

docs.title: Step 5: Run
docs.text:
crucible fork --topology single
crucible experiment run experiments/my_exploit.yaml

docs.title: The ExperimentContext
docs.text:
Every plugin method receives an ExperimentContext with these fields:

@dataclass
class ExperimentContext:
    config: TestbedConfig          # Full testbed configuration
    topology: TopologyConfig       # Active topology definition
    chains: ChainManager           # Chain lifecycle management
    log: LogEmitter | LogManager   # Artifact logging
    workspace: Path                # Project root path
    run_id: str                    # Unique run identifier (e.g., DS-2026-00042)
    run_config: dict               # Experiment-specific run config
    bridge_plugin: Any             # Deployed bridge plugin (if any)

docs.title: Accessing Chain State
docs.text:
# Get a JSON-RPC client for a chain
runtime = context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum")
client = JsonRpcClient(runtime.rpc_url)

# Read chain state
balance = client.get_balance("0x...")
code = client.get_code("0x...")
storage = client.get_storage_at("0x...", "0x0")
block = client.call("eth_getBlockByNumber", ["0x...", False])

# Send transactions
tx_hash = client.send_transaction({
    "to": "0x...",
    "data": "0x...",
    "from": "0x...",
})
receipt = client.call("eth_getTransactionReceipt", [tx_hash])

# Impersonate accounts (requires --auto-impersonate or manual anvil_impersonate)
client.impersonate_account("0x...")

docs.title: Logging
docs.text:
# Step logging (appears in run.jsonl)
context.log.step("message", phase="attack", action="exploit_execution", status="success")

# Event logging (appears in events.jsonl)
context.log.event(
    chain="ethereum",
    event_name="Transfer",
    address="0x...",
    topics=["0x..."],
    data="0x...",
)

# Storage diff logging
context.log.storage_diff(
    chain="ethereum",
    contract="0x...",
    slot="0x...",
    before="0x...",
    after="0x...",
)

# Gas logging
context.log.gas(step="exploit_tx", gas_used=21000, chain="ethereum")

docs.title: Writing a Bridge Plugin
docs.text:
Bridge plugins simulate cross-chain bridge protocols. They follow a full lifecycle:

from crucible.sdk import BridgePlugin, ExperimentContext


class MyBridge(BridgePlugin):
    def metadata(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "name": "my_bridge",
            "version": "1.0.0",
            "type": "bridge",
        }

    def deploy(self, context: ExperimentContext) -> dict[str, str]:
        # Deploy bridge contracts on all chains
        # Returns dict of chain_name -> deployed_address
        eth_client = context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum")
        # ... deploy contract ...
        return {"ethereum": "0xdeployed..."}

    def configure(self, context, config=None):
        # Set validator set, relayer addresses, guardian keys
        pass

    def connect(self, context):
        # Start message-passing simulation
        pass

    def send_message(self, context, source_chain, dest_chain, data, correlation_id=None):
        # Send a cross-chain message
        client = context.chains.runtime_for(source_chain)
        tx_hash = client.send_transaction({
            "to": context.bridge_address,
            "data": data,
        })
        return {"source_tx_hash": tx_hash, "correlation_id": correlation_id}

    def reset(self, context):
        # Reset bridge state to post-deploy baseline
        pass

    def destroy(self, context):
        # Tear down relayer and deployed contracts
        pass

docs.title: Bridge Plugin Manifest
docs.text:
# bridges/my_bridge/plugin.yaml
name: my_bridge
version: 1.0.0
type: bridge
entry: my_bridge.py
class: MyBridge

Bridge plugins also require evidence.yaml:

# bridges/my_bridge/evidence.yaml
protocol:
  name: "My Bridge Protocol"
  version: "1.0"

features:
  - "Cross-chain message passing"
  - "Validator set management"

limitations:
  - "No real relayer â€” simulation only"
  - "Single message per transaction"

chains: ["ethereum", "polygon"]

docs.title: Writing an Agent Plugin
docs.text:
Agent plugins replace traditional attack plugins with LLM-driven autonomous agents:

from crucible.sdk import AgentPlugin, ExperimentContext, VerificationResult
from crucible.agent_tools import AgentAction, AgentContext


class MyAgent(AgentPlugin):
    def metadata(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "name": "my_agent",
            "version": "1.0.0",
            "type": "agent",
        }

    def think(self, observation, context, agent_ctx):
        # Given the current observation, decide what action to take
        # Return an AgentAction, or None to end the loop
        return AgentAction(
            tool="get_balance",
            params={"address": "0x..."},
            reasoning="Check attacker balance",
        )

    def verify(self, context, agent_ctx):
        return VerificationResult(passed=True)

docs.title: Agent Experiment Config
docs.text:
# experiments/my_agent.yaml
name: my_agent
scenario: my_scenario
attack: my_agent
topology: single
run:
  agent_provider: openai        # openai | anthropic | ollama | mock
  agent_privilege: medium       # low | medium | high
  agent_allowed_accounts:
    - "0xattacker..."

docs.title: Attack Plugin
docs.text:
prepare() â†’ execute() â†’ verify() â†’ rollback() â†’ cleanup()
                      â†˜ run() â†—          (convenience wrapper)
Method Called Purpose prepare() Before execute Set up attacker accounts, approve tokens, deploy helpers execute() Main execution Run the attack, return AttackResult verify() After execute Check on-chain state against expected results rollback() On failure Undo persistent changes not handled by snapshot restore cleanup() Always Remove temp files, kill helper processes

docs.title: Bridge Plugin
docs.text:
deploy() â†’ configure() â†’ connect() â†’ send_message()* â†’ reset() â†’ destroy()
Method Called Purpose deploy() Once Deploy bridge contracts to all chains configure() After deploy Set validator set, relayer addresses, guardian keys connect() Before messaging Start message-passing simulation / relayer process send_message() Per message Send a cross-chain message reset() Between runs Reset bridge state to post-deploy baseline destroy() Teardown Tear down relayer and clean up deployed contracts

docs.title: Agent Plugin
docs.text:
prepare() â†’ [observe() â†’ think() â†’ respond()]* â†’ verify()
Method Called Purpose prepare() Once Set up agent environment observe() Per iteration Gather observations from the environment think() Per iteration Decide what action to take (return AgentAction or None) respond() Per iteration Process the result of the action verify() End Verify the agent achieved its objective

docs.title: 1. Always Return Structured Results
docs.text:
Always return AttackResult with phase_results containing individual step results:

return AttackResult(success=True, phase_results=[
    {"step": "check_contract", "passed": True, "detail": "Contract has code"},
    {"step": "exploit_execution", "passed": True, "detail": tx_hash},
])

Each phase result appears in the run manifest and is visible in crucible history.

docs.title: 2. Use the LogEmitter
docs.text:
Don't print() â€” use context.log.step():

context.log.step(
    "Verifying contract code at fork block",
    phase="attack", action="check_code", status="success",
    contract=CONTRACT_ADDRESS,
    fork_block=fork_block,
)

docs.title: 3. Handle Chain Unavailability Gracefully
docs.text:
When creating multi-chain plugins, wrap per-chain operations in try/except:

for chain_name in ["ethereum", "polygon"]:
    try:
        client = context.chains.runtime_for(chain_name)
        code = client.get_code(CONTRACT_ADDRESS)
        if code and code != "0x":
            results[chain_name] = True
    except Exception as exc:
        context.log.step(
            f"Skipping {chain_name}: {exc}",
            phase="attack", action="check_code", status="warning",
        )

docs.title: 4. Store Important Addresses as Constants
docs.text:
VULNERABLE_CONTRACT = "0x..."
ATTACKER_ADDRESS = "0x..."
FUNCTION_SELECTOR = "0x..."

docs.title: 5. Document Fidelity Constraints
docs.text:
In evidence.yaml, be honest about what your plugin cannot reproduce:

reproduction_scope:
  excluded_components:
    - "Solana-side validators (non-EVM â€” cannot fork)"
    - "Off-chain MPC key generation"

docs.title: 1. Double 0x Prefix in Bytecode
docs.text:
When deploying contracts, Foundry .json artifacts already include the 0x prefix:

# WRONG â€” double 0x prefix
bytecode = "0x" + artifact["bytecode"]  # artifact["bytecode"] already starts with 0x

# RIGHT
bytecode = artifact["bytecode"]

docs.title: 2. EOA vs Contract Addresses
docs.text:
Always verify that target addresses are contracts, not EOAs:

code = client.get_code(address)
if not code or code == "0x":
    # This is an EOA, not a contract!
    raise ValueError(f"Expected contract at {address}, got EOA")

docs.title: 3. Archive RPC Limitations
docs.text: Polygon and BSC archive RPCs at old fork blocks may not serve state for newly created addresses. This affects anvil_setBalance when bridge plugins try to fund deployer addresses. Always wrap per-chain deployment in try/except.

docs.title: 4. Non-EVM Boundaries
docs.text: Crucible is EVM-only. Attacks involving Solana, Cosmos, Harmony ONE, or Substrate chains must be documented in excluded_components â€” they cannot be reproduced on-chain.

docs.title: 1. Validate the Plugin Loads
docs.text:
crucible plugin validate attacks/my_exploit

This checks: - Plugin manifest (plugin.yaml) is well-formed - Entry point file exists - Class can be imported and instantiated - Evidence (evidence.yaml) has all required sections

docs.title: 2. Check Evidence Against Live Chain
docs.text:
crucible evidence check my_exploit --fork

This forks the chain at the configured block and verifies: - Fork block exists via eth_getBlockByNumber - Declared contracts have non-empty code via eth_getCode

docs.title: 3. Run the Experiment
docs.text:
crucible fork --topology single
crucible experiment run experiments/my_exploit.yaml

docs.title: 4. Verify Determinism
docs.text:
# Run twice
crucible experiment run experiments/my_exploit.yaml --name run-a
crucible experiment run experiments/my_exploit.yaml --name run-b

# Compare
crucible diff <run-a-id> <run-b-id>

All four metrics must be true: - storage_equal - events_equal - gas_equal - balances_equal

docs.title: 5. Export for Publication
docs.text:
# Export bundle
crucible export <run-id>

# Verify bundle integrity
crucible verify datasets/<run-id>

docs.title: Plugin Reference
docs.text: Crucible's architecture relies on an extensible plugin system. Below is the comprehensive reference of all 15 plugins included in the testbed out-of-the-box.

docs.title: Attack Plugins
docs.text: Attack plugins encode the mechanics for historical benchmarks, demonstrations, and validation tests.

• Type: Attack
  • Version: 1.2.0
  • Class: BnbTokenHubProof
  • Chains: bsc

Incident: BNB Bridge token hub proof verification bypass

Fidelity Level: L2

docs.title: demo_attack
docs.text:
• Type: Attack
  • Version: 1.0.0
  • Class: DemoAttack
  • Chains: ethereum
  • Topology: single

Incident: Demo attack

Fidelity Level: L0

docs.title: horizon_multisig_drain
docs.text:
• Type: Attack
  • Version: 1.0.0
  • Class: HorizonMultisigDrain
  • Chains: ethereum
  • Topology: single

Incident: Harmony Horizon multisig validator drain

The Harmony Horizon bridge used a 2-of-5 multisig wallet to authorize cross-chain transfers. The attacker compromised 2 validator keys (via social engineering attributed to Lazarus Group) and used the submitTransaction/confirmTransaction flow to drain ~$100M in bridged assets from the Ethereum-side bridge contracts. This was an infrastructure compromise, not a smart contract vulnerability — the contracts behaved exactly as designed.

Fidelity Level: L2

docs.title: l4_full_validation
docs.text:
• Type: Attack
  • Version: 1.0.0
  • Class: L4FullValidation
  • Chains: ethereum, polygon
  • Topology: nomad_bridge

Incident: L4 Full Testbed Validation — multi-chain bridge capability test

Comprehensive L4 testbed validation experiment. Exercises the full multi-chain bridge pipeline: blank-fork both chains, deploy NomadReplica contracts via the nomad_bridge plugin, send forged cross-chain messages bidirectionally, verify message acceptance, and capture all instrumentation artifacts. No historical state required — uses blank Anvil forks to eliminate RPC dependency and ensure deterministic, offline-capable testing.

Fidelity Level: L4

docs.title: multichain_router_permit
docs.text:
• Type: Attack
  • Version: 1.0.0
  • Class: MultichainRouterPermit
  • Topology: single

Incident: Multichain MPC node compromise

Fidelity Level: L1

docs.title: nomad_bridge_demo
docs.text:
• Type: Attack
  • Version: 1.0.0
  • Class: NomadBridgeDemo
  • Chains: ethereum, polygon
  • Topology: nomad_bridge

Incident: Nomad Bridge L4 Validation — multi-chain attack demo

L4 fidelity validation experiment demonstrating cross-chain message forgery using the Nomad bridge plugin. Deploys NomadReplica contracts on both chains, sends a forged message via process(), and verifies acceptance.

Fidelity Level: L4

docs.title: nomad_replica_fraud
docs.text:
• Type: Attack
  • Version: 1.0.0
  • Class: NomadReplicaFraud
  • Chains: ethereum
  • Topology: bridge

Incident: Nomad Bridge Replica Fraud — message verification bypass

The Nomad bridge Replica contract on Ethereum was deployed with its committedRoot storage variable initialized to 0x00. The contract's process() function treated this zero root as a valid proof, allowing anyone to forge arbitrary cross-chain messages and drain bridge funds.

Fidelity Level: L2

docs.title: poly_network_privkey
docs.text:
• Type: Attack
  • Version: 1.0.0
  • Class: PolyNetworkPrivkey
  • Chains: ethereum, polygon, bsc
  • Topology: poly_network_full

Incident: Poly Network cross-chain privilege escalation

Attacker exploited a hash collision in EthCrossChainManager's verifyHeaderAndExecuteTx to call putCurEpochConPubKeyBytes via _executeCrossChainTx, replacing the keeper public key and authorizing unauthorized cross-chain transfers. Funds stolen from Ethereum, BSC, and Polygon (~$611M total). Fork block: 12,996,693 (block before exploit tx at 12,996,694). Exploit tx: 0x5a8b2152... (verifyHeaderAndExecuteTx call on Manager).

Fidelity Level: L2

docs.title: wormhole_guardian_bypass
docs.text:
• Type: Attack
  • Version: 0.2.0
  • Class: WormholeGuardianBypass
  • Chains: ethereum
  • Topology: bridge

Incident: Wormhole Guardian Signature Bypass — forged VAA token mint

The Wormhole bridge was exploited when an attacker bypassed the Guardian signature verification on Solana, forging a Validated Action Approval (VAA) that instructed the Ethereum TokenBridge contract to mint 120,000 wETH (~$326M). The vulnerability was in Solana's use of a deprecated sysvar account validation, NOT in the EVM-side contracts — the Ethereum bridge processed the forged VAA as if it were legitimate. This reproduction focuses on the EVM-side effects: demonstrating the TokenBridge's completeTransfer() function and its VAA verification path.

Fidelity Level: L2

docs.title: Crucible Quick Reference
docs.text: One-page cheat sheet for daily Crucible tasks. See also: CLI Reference (full), Simple Operations (detailed walkthroughs)

docs.title: Setup & Environment
docs.text:
# Install
uv sync                              # Install Python dependencies

# Validate environment
crucible doctor                       # Check Python, Foundry, config, RPCs
crucible doctor --fix                 # Auto-fix common issues

# Initialize workspace
crucible init                         # Create runtime directories

# Configure RPC in .env
echo 'ETH_RPC_URL=https://rpc.ankr.com/eth/YOUR_KEY' >> .env

docs.title: Forking Chains
docs.text:
# Single chain (Ethereum, port 8545)
crucible fork --topology single

# Multi-chain (Ethereum + Polygon, ports 8545 + 8546)
crucible fork --topology dual

# Bridge topology (Ethereum + Polygon + bridge plugin)
crucible fork --topology bridge

# With longer timeout for slow RPCs
crucible fork --topology dual --fork-timeout 600

# Blank fork (no archive RPC needed — standalone EVM)
# First set fork_block: null in testbed.local.yaml, then:
crucible fork --topology single

# See what's running
crucible status
crucible status --disk               # Include disk usage

docs.title: Running Experiments
docs.text:
# Run with matching topology
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --no-baseline

# Skip baseline verification (first run on a machine)
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --no-baseline

# Skip fidelity enforcement (development only)
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --no-baseline --no-fidelity

# Name your run for easy identification later
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --name "my-nomad-test"

docs.title: Common Experiments & Their Topologies
docs.text:
Experiment Topology Chains Needed demo_experiment.yaml single Ethereum nomad_replay.yaml bridge Ethereum + Polygon wormhole_replay.yaml bridge Ethereum + Polygon poly_network_replay.yaml bridge Ethereum + Polygon + BSC horizon_replay.yaml single Ethereum bnb_token_hub_replay.yaml single_bsc BSC

Topology mismatch: If you fork with --topology single but the experiment expects bridge, the attack executes but the L2 fidelity check will fail. Either match the topology, or use --no-fidelity for development.

docs.title: Ad-Hoc Chain Interaction (cast)
docs.text:
export RPC=http://127.0.0.1:8545

# Query
cast balance 0xAddress --rpc-url $RPC
cast code 0xContract --rpc-url $RPC                # "0x" = EOA, bytecode = contract
cast storage 0xContract 0x0 --rpc-url $RPC
cast call 0xToken "totalSupply()(uint256)" --rpc-url $RPC
cast block-number --rpc-url $RPC

# Send transactions (auto-impersonate enabled)
cast rpc anvil_setBalance 0xAddress $(cast to-wei 100 ether) --rpc-url $RPC
cast send 0xToken "transfer(address,uint256)" 0xTo 1000 \
  --unlocked --from 0xOwner --rpc-url $RPC

# EVM manipulation
cast rpc evm_increaseTime 86400 --rpc-url $RPC     # +1 day
cast rpc evm_mine --rpc-url $RPC                    # mine block
cast rpc anvil_setStorageAt 0xContract 0xSlot 0xValue --rpc-url $RPC

# Impersonation
cast rpc anvil_impersonateAccount 0xAddress --rpc-url $RPC
cast send --from 0xAddress --unlocked --to 0xContract "fn()" --rpc-url $RPC
cast rpc anvil_stopImpersonatingAccount 0xAddress --rpc-url $RPC

docs.title: Managing State
docs.text:
# Named snapshots
crucible snapshot create my_state
crucible snapshot restore my_state --chain ethereum

# Three-tier reset (fastest → slowest)
crucible reset --scenario single --mode soft        # <5s  — reload saved state
crucible reset --scenario single --mode medium      # <30s — restart Anvil + restore
crucible reset --scenario single --mode hard        # <5min — re-fork from RPC

docs.title: Artifacts & Results
docs.text:
# List completed runs
crucible history
crucible history --outcome success                  # Filter by outcome
crucible history --tag nomad --limit 5              # Filter by tag, limit

# View artifacts
cat logs/<experiment>/<run_id>/report.md
cat logs/<experiment>/<run_id>/run_manifest.json | jq .provenance

# Compare two runs (determinism check)
crucible diff DS-2026-00182 DS-2026-00183
# All 4 must be true: storage_equal, events_equal, gas_equal, balances_equal

# Export for publication
crucible export DS-2026-00182

# Verify exported bundle
crucible verify datasets/DS-2026-00182/

docs.title: Cleaning Up
docs.text:
crucible clean                           # Stop chains, remove meta files
crucible clean --all                     # + remove cache, checkpoints, snapshots
crucible clean --cache                   # Remove fork cache only
crucible clean --logs                    # Remove old log directories
crucible clean --logs-older-than 7       # Remove logs older than 7 days
crucible clean --states                  # Remove non-baseline snapshots
crucible clean --checkpoints             # Remove experiment checkpoints

docs.title: Common Pitfalls
docs.text: Symptom Cause Fix insufficient funds Address not funded anvil_setBalance first Connection refused No Anvil running, or wrong port Run crucible fork first, check port Fidelity check fails on bridge experiment --topology single but experiment needs bridge Match topology (see topologies) or add --no-fidelity assertions failed Attack didn't produce expected state Run with --no-fidelity to isolate baseline manifest not found First run on this machine Add --no-baseline Port 8545 already in use Previous process still running crucible clean or pkill -9 -f anvil Cold fork too slow First time forking this block Use --fork-timeout 600, subsequent runs use cache No module named 'pydantic' .venv not synced uv sync

docs.title: Docker
docs.text:
# Build
docker compose build orchestrator

# Fork + run experiment in one container
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  rm -f states/*.meta.json && \
  uv run crucible fork --topology single && \
  uv run crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --no-baseline
"

# Run tests in container
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  cd /workspace && uv run pytest tests/unit/ -q --tb=line
"

# Launch dashboard
# Launch dashboard (Docker)
docker compose up -d dashboard

# Launch dashboard (local)
crucible dashboard

# Launch Jupyter for artifact analysis
crucible notebook

docs.title: Topology Profiles Available
docs.text: Profile File Chains Bridge single topologies/single.yaml Ethereum None dual topologies/dual.yaml Ethereum + Polygon None bridge topologies/bridge.yaml Ethereum + Polygon demo_bridge single_bsc topologies/single_bsc.yaml BSC None nomad_bridge topologies/nomad_bridge.yaml Ethereum + Polygon nomad_bridge wormhole_bridge topologies/wormhole_bridge.yaml Ethereum + Polygon wormhole_bridge poly_network_bridge topologies/poly_network_bridge.yaml Ethereum + Polygon + BSC poly_network_bridge harmony_bridge topologies/harmony_bridge.yaml Ethereum + Harmony bridge poly_network_full topologies/poly_network_full.yaml Ethereum + Polygon + BSC None

docs.title: Key Files & Directories
docs.text:
Path Purpose testbed.yaml Main configuration (fork blocks, RPCs, chains) testbed.local.yaml Per-user overrides (gitignored) .env RPC API keys (gitignored) states/ Chain snapshots, meta files, baselines logs/<experiment>/<run_id>/ Experiment artifacts (JSONL, report, manifest) .fork_cache/ Anvil RPC response cache (gitignored) topologies/*.yaml Topology profile definitions experiments/*.yaml Experiment definitions

Need more detail? See Simple Operations for step-by-step ad-hoc workflows, CLI Reference for all commands and flags, or Troubleshooting for error recovery.

docs.title: Reproducibility Guide
docs.text: Reproducibility is integral to the Crucible testbed's architectural design. The Technical Overview explains the philosophy behind determinism-by-default, cache fingerprinting, and fidelity enforcement. For the design rationale behind each reproducibility mechanism, see the Design Decisions document.

docs.title: What Makes A Crucible Run Reproducible
docs.text:
Every crucible experiment run pins the following in run_manifest.json:

Field Source Purpose git_commit git rev-parse HEAD Code version anchor git_dirty git status --porcelain Detect uncommitted changes foundry_version forge --version Anvil/forge binary version python_version sys.version Python version platform sys.platform OS identifier config_fingerprint SHA-256 of testbed.yaml Detect config drift docker_mode Detected from env Container vs native cache_mode CacheManager.mode() Cold/warm cache state cache_fingerprint SHA-256 of all cache manifests Cache identity experiment_file_hash SHA-256 of experiment YAML Experiment definition pin evidence_hash SHA-256 of evidence.yaml Attack definition pin scenario_hash SHA-256 of scenario.yaml Environment definition pin run_id DS-YYYY-NNNNN format Monotonically increasing, year-resetting counter timestamps UTC ISO-8601 Chronological anchor

docs.title: Reproducibility Modes
docs.text: Mode Description Requirements Docker (preferred) Containerized environment with pinned dependencies Docker, git Local Native Python + Foundry Python 3.11+, Foundry, archive RPC Cached fork Reuses previously fetched RPC cache Docker or local + existing .fork_cache Artifact replay Inspect exported bundle without re-execution Only the datasets/ export bundle

docs.title: Provenance Verification
docs.text:
After any experiment run, verify reproducibility by checking:

# 1. Check the run manifest
cat logs/*/<run_id>/run_manifest.json | jq '.provenance'

# 1b. Verify config fingerprint manually
# The config fingerprint is computed via get_config_fingerprint() in crucible.provenance
# It covers testbed.yaml and active topology settings.

# 2. Check log integrity
cat logs/*/<run_id>/log_integrity.json

# 3. Run twice and diff
crucible diff <run_a> <run_b>

# 4. Export and verify bundle
crucible export <run_id>
crucible verify datasets/<run_id>

docs.title: Cache Policy & Determinism
docs.text:
Cache state impacts execution latency but does not alter deterministic outcomes. A strict policy ensures cache fingerprints align with the current configuration:

# testbed.yaml
fork_cache:
  enabled: true
  cache_policy: strict     # invalidates on provider/block/version mismatch
  # or
  cache_policy: permissive # warns but doesn't invalidate

The cache_mode in run_manifest.json records whether the run used cold cache (first fork), warm cache (subsequent forks), or budget cache (RPC budget-limited mode).

docs.title: Publishing A Run
docs.text:
# 1. Run with publication gates
crucible experiment run experiments/attack.yaml --publication-mode

# 2. Export the bundle
crucible export <run_id> --format tar-gz

# 3. Verify the export
crucible verify datasets/<run_id>
cat datasets/<run_id>/bundle_hash.txt

The export bundle is entirely self-contained; it may be transferred and verified independently of the original workspace.

docs.title: Export Bundle Structure
docs.text: The exported bundle (.tar.gz or uncompressed directory) built by crucible.export.ExportBundle contains: - artifact_bundle_manifest.json: Hashes of every file in the bundle. - run_manifest.json: Full provenance and execution metadata. - evidence.yaml: The attacker's claims and fidelity level. - plugin.yaml: Attack plugin configuration. - scenario.yaml: Environment preconditions. - log_integrity.json: Original runtime hashes for sub-artifacts. - All JSONL streams (events.jsonl, storage_diffs.jsonl, gas_profile.jsonl, run.jsonl, etc.) - (Optional) .protected marker indicating publication status.

docs.title: Common Reproducibility Pitfalls
docs.text: When reproducing historical exploits or authoring new attack packs, researchers commonly encounter the following issues that break determinism or fidelity.

docs.title: Infrastructure & Environment Issues
docs.text: Pitfall Symptom Solution Stale meta files in Docker volume anvil_dumpState on wrong port Clean states/*.meta.json before each fork Daemon Anvil fork block mismatch Experiment uses wrong fork block Use single-container mode instead for precise control Port TIME_WAIT blocking restart New Anvil can't bind Crucible automatically polls ports before restarting, but manual resets may require waiting 10-30s Checkpoints snapshotting dead chains anvil_dumpState on stale meta file Crucible filters checkpoints to active topologies

docs.title: Research Data Errors
docs.text:
The most common cause of reproduction failure is incorrect research data in the evidence.yaml or plugin file.

Pitfall Symptom Solution Contract address is actually an EOA Contract has no code error during setup Verify the address on a block explorer. It must be a deployed contract, not the attacker's wallet. Wrong fork block Exploit tx reverts, state doesn't match Fork block must be exactly exploit_block - 1 Precompile/Deployer fails on archive node anvil_setBalance fails on new address Some archive providers don't support setting balances on addresses with no historical state. Wrap bridge deployments in try/except.

docs.title: Verification Checklist for New Attacks
docs.text: Before running an experiment: - [ ] Clean stale meta files: rm -f states/*.meta.json (if using Docker) - [ ] Set the correct fork block in testbed.yaml or testbed.local.yaml - [ ] Verify the RPC provider supports archive depth at your fork block - [ ] Run twice and diff: crucible diff <run_a> <run_b> - [ ] Check all 4 metrics (gas, storage, events, balances) are equal

docs.title: Baseline System
docs.text: Crucible uses a dual-verification baseline system that ensures reproducibility across runs:

docs.title: How Baselines Work
docs.text:
1. Capture â€” After forking and bootstrapping, crucible baseline capture <scenario>:
  2. Dumps the full EVM state for each chain via anvil_dumpState
  3. Canonicalizes and SHA-256 hashes the state
  4. Captures a live state hash from the running chain

  5. Writes a baseline manifest YAML with both hashes

  6. Verify â€” Before running an experiment, crucible baseline verify <scenario>:

  7. Re-computes the snapshot file hash and compares it to the manifest
  8. If the chain is running: also compares the live chain state against the captured state hash

  9. This dual check catches both file corruption AND environment drift

  10. Restore â€” crucible reset --mode soft loads the baseline snapshot back into the running chain via anvil_loadState (<5s)

docs.title: Baseline Storage
docs.text:
states/baseline/
â”œâ”€â”€ <scenario>.baseline.yaml      # Manifest with hashes and metadata
â”œâ”€â”€ <scenario>.<chain>.json       # Full EVM state snapshot
â””â”€â”€ <scenario>.<chain2>.json      # One per chain in topology

docs.title: When Baselines Go Stale
docs.text:
Situation Detection Fix Changed RPC provider Live state hash mismatch baseline verify catches it Changed fork block State completely different baseline verify catches it Changed topology (different chains) Missing chain snapshots Re-capture with baseline capture Snapshot file deleted/corrupted File hash mismatch Re-capture with baseline capture 
# Capture a new baseline after environment changes
crucible setup --topology single
crucible baseline capture my_scenario --topology single

docs.title: Cache System & Fingerprinting
docs.text: Crucible caches fork state from archive RPCs to accelerate repeated execution. The cache subsystem maintains its own provenance tracking:

docs.title: Cache Policy
docs.text:
Configured in testbed.yaml:

fork_cache:
  enabled: true
  path: ".fork_cache"
  cache_policy: permissive  # or "strict"
  • strict â€” Invalidates cache on ANY mismatch (provider, foundry version, config fingerprint). Ensures bit-identical forks but may re-fetch frequently.
  • permissive â€” Warns on mismatch but doesn't invalidate. Faster for development, uses cache even if config changed slightly.

docs.title: Cache Provenance
docs.text:
Each cache entry has a manifest tracking: - RPC provider URL (fingerprinted, not stored raw) - Fork block number - Chain ID - Foundry version at time of caching - Config fingerprint (SHA-256 of testbed.yaml)

# Inspect cache entries
crucible cache inspect

# Compute aggregate fingerprint (SHA-256 of all manifests)
crucible cache fingerprint

# Verify a specific entry
crucible cache verify --chain ethereum --fork-block 15259100

# Clear stale cache
crucible cache clean --chain ethereum

The cache_mode in run_manifest.json records whether the run used cold (first fork), warm (subsequent), or budget (RPC-limited) cache.

docs.title: Publication Gates
docs.text: Publication gates enforce research-quality standards before a run completes. They are optional but recommended for formal publications:

docs.title: Enabling Publication Gates
docs.text:
crucible experiment run experiments/attack.yaml --publication-mode

Or configure them to always run:

# testbed.yaml
publication:
  enabled: true
  require_foundry_pinned: true        # Foundry version must match testbed.yaml
  require_baseline_verified: true     # Baseline must pass verify() before run
  require_provenance_complete: true   # Git commit, config fingerprint must be available
  fidelity_enforcement: false         # When True, enforces fidelity at runtime (default check-based)

docs.title: What Each Gate Checks
docs.text:
Gate Checks Fails When Foundry pinned Installed Foundry version matches testbed.yaml Version mismatch, or required_version not set Baseline verified Latest baseline passes verify() Missing baseline, hash mismatch, live state drift Provenance complete Git commit available, config fingerprint computed Dirty working tree, missing .git, config parse failure Fidelity enforcement Runtime behavior matches declared fidelity level Missing trace artifacts, wrong chain topology, no exploit transactions

Note: Each gate evaluation returns a GateResult object containing passed (bool), reason (str), and fatal (bool).

docs.title: Run Tiers
docs.text:
Every experiment run is assigned a retention tier that controls cleanup behavior:

Tier Default For Auto-Cleaned Preserved By scratch All runs (default) After 7 days Nothing saved --tier saved flag Never Explicit flag published crucible publish Never .protected marker in dataset 
# Run and immediately save
crucible experiment run experiments/attack.yaml --tier saved

# Promote an existing run to published
crucible publish DS-2026-00155

# The run is now protected from all auto-cleanup

docs.title: Publication Lifecycle
docs.text:
1. Run experiment          â†’ crucible experiment run ... --tier saved
2. Verify artifacts        â†’ crucible verify logs/<experiment>/<run_id>
3. Promote to published    â†’ crucible publish <run_id>
4. Export publication bundle â†’ crucible export <run_id> --format tar-gz
5. Upload to Zenodo/archive â†’ Upload datasets/<run_id>.tar.gz

docs.title: Pre-Reproduction Workflow
docs.text:
Prior to executing any reproduction, the environment must be initialized:

# 1. Initialize workspace
crucible init

# 2. Validate environment
crucible doctor

# 3. Set up and capture baseline
crucible setup --topology <topology>
crucible baseline capture <scenario> --topology <topology>

# 4. Verify baseline
crucible baseline verify <scenario>
