docs.title: Formal Reproducibility Checklist
docs.text:
• Workspace initialized with crucible init
  • Environment validated with crucible doctor
  • Baseline captured and verified
  • Git commit pinned in run_manifest.json
  • Foundry version recorded
  • Baseline verified before run
  • Cache mode and fingerprint recorded
  • Log integrity hashes written
  • Experiment run twice with identical diffs
  • Export bundle verified with crucible verify
  • Cross-provider reproduction documented
  • Contract addresses verified on Etherscan (not EOAs)
  • Fork block verified against exploit transaction
  • RPC provider tested for archive depth
  • Stale meta files cleaned before forking
  • Determinism verified via crucible diff

docs.title: Reproduction Scope & Capabilities
docs.text: Purpose: Help researchers understand what Crucible can reproduce, at what fidelity, and what's in vs out of scope before planning a study. Last updated: June 2026

docs.title: What Crucible Can Reproduce
docs.text: Crucible is an EVM-only bridge security research testbed. It reproduces historical bridge exploits by forking blockchain state at a specific block using Foundry's Anvil and running attack plugins against the forked environment.

docs.title: Core Capability
docs.text:
Archive RPC → Fork EVM chain at block N → Run attack plugin → Capture artifacts
Capability Supported? Notes Fork any EVM chain [ok] Yes Ethereum, BSC, Polygon, Harmony — any chain with an archive RPC endpoint Fork at any historical block [ok] Yes Requires archive node access at that block depth Replay exploit transactions [ok] Yes L3: forge exact calldata and replay on fork Behavioral reproduction [ok] Yes L2: verify vulnerable state exists and exploit path works Multi-chain reproduction [ok] Supported via 3 bridge plugins Nomad (2 chains), Poly Network (3 chains), Wormhole (2 chains) — see bridge topology files Deploy fresh bridge contracts on blank chains [ok] Yes NomadBridge, PolyNetworkBridge, WormholeBridge plugins deploy real Solidity contracts to Anvil forks Forge cross-chain messages [ok] Yes bridge_plugin.send_message() with forged calldata — captured in cross_chain.jsonl Non-EVM chains (Solana, Cosmos) [fail] No Crucible is EVM-only by design Off-chain infrastructure [fail] No Relayers, keepers, MPC nodes, governance — must be modeled or excluded

docs.title: Fidelity Levels Supported
docs.text: Level What It Means Crucible Support Example L4 Full multi-chain + bridge simulation [ok] Supported via 3 bridge plugins — Nomad (2 chains), Poly Network (3 chains), Wormhole (2 chains). Requires paid archive RPCs for BSC/Polygon at 2021-era blocks Poly Network (all 3 chains), Nomad, Wormhole L3 Exact historical transaction replay [ok] Supported — forge calldata, replay on fork Nomad process() call L2 Fork-state behavioral reproduction [ok] Primary use case — verify vulnerability exists Wormhole guardian bypass L1 Conceptual / narrative reproduction [ok] Supported — deploy vulnerable contracts locally Multichain router permit (MPC compromise) L0 Description only [ok] Supported — no code required Documentation-only

docs.title: Complete (L2 — Research-Grade)
docs.text:
These attacks are fully reproducible with determinism proven:

Attack Chains Fork Block Fidelity Key Limitation Nomad Bridge (nomad_replica_fraud) Ethereum 15,259,100 L2 [ok] Doesn't replay exact historical tx (uses reconstructed forged message — proxy ABI resolution needed for exact replay) Wormhole Guardian Bypass (wormhole_guardian_bypass) Ethereum 14,125,553 L2 [ok] Root cause was Solana-side (deprecated sysvar); only EVM-side effects reproducible Poly Network PrivKey (poly_network_privkey) Ethereum + Polygon + BSC 12,996,693 L2 [ok] 3-chain topology available and determinism proven. BSC + Polygon at exploit blocks require paid archive RPCs for full multi-chain run Horizon Multisig Drain (horizon_multisig_drain) Ethereum 15,005,526 L2 [ok] Infrastructure compromise (not a contract bug) — replaying Ethereum tx succeeds but proves nothing about the vulnerability. Harmony chain (non-EVM) not forkable, blocking L4 BNB Token Hub Proof (bnb_token_hub_proof) BSC 22,166,659 L2 [ok] CrossChain contract (0x...2000) identified as the real handlePackage() target. Function selector corrected to 0x84013b6a. Requires paid BSC archive RPC (block 22M exceeds free tier)

docs.title: Complete (L1 — Narrative / Infrastructure)
docs.text:
These attacks are correctly classified at their maximum possible fidelity:

Attack Chains Fork Block Fidelity Key Limitation Multichain Router Permit (multichain_router_permit) Ethereum (narrative) — L1 📝 Maxed at L1. This was an MPC node infrastructure compromise, not a smart contract bug. No on-chain reproduction possible

docs.title: Demo & Reference
docs.text: Pack Fidelity Purpose demo_attack L0 Reference AttackPlugin implementation for learning (< 15 lines) demo_agent Agent LLM-driven autonomous agent demo demo_bridge Bridge Reference BridgePlugin implementation

docs.title: Chain Compatibility Matrix
docs.text: Chain Archive RPC Available? Fork Block Range Cost Ethereum [ok] Free (LlamaNodes, Ankr) 0–current Free Polygon [warn] Limited free ~40M+ (current only on free tier) Paid ($39/mo GetBlock) BSC [warn] Limited free ~28M+ (current only on free tier) Paid ($39/mo GetBlock) Harmony [ok] Free (Ankr) 0–current Free

docs.title: Recommended Provider
docs.text:
GetBlock Starter ($39/month) — covers BSC + Polygon archive. Ethereum works on free LlamaNodes.

See docs/RPC_PROVIDER_GUIDE.md for full comparison.

docs.title: Attack Types Crucible Can Reproduce
docs.text: Type Example Works? Smart contract logic bugs Nomad committedRoot = 0x00 [ok] Access control bypass Wormhole guardian signature verification [ok] Hash collision / selector confusion Poly Network verifyHeaderAndExecuteTx / putCurEpochConPubKeyBytes [ok] Message forgery (Merkle proof) Nomad, BNB TokenHub proof bypass [ok] Compromised key / privilege abuse Horizon multisig drain [warn] (infrastructure, not contract bug) Re-entrancy on bridge contracts Standard EVM re-entrancy [ok] Flash loan + price manipulation On forked chain with sufficient state [ok]

docs.title: Chains Crucible Can Fork
docs.text:
• Any EVM-compatible chain with an archive-capable RPC endpoint
  • Tested: Ethereum (mainnet), BSC, Polygon, Harmony
  • Multi-chain experiments supported via bridge topology

docs.title: What's Out of Scope
docs.text: Category Why Non-EVM chains (Solana, Cosmos, Near, etc.) Crucible's ChainManager launches Anvil (EVM-only). Non-EVM reproduction requires separate tooling Off-chain infrastructure Relayers, keepers, MPC nodes, governance processes — Crucible can't simulate these. They must be declared in excluded_components in evidence.yaml Live mainnet interaction Crucible forks are isolated — no transactions ever reach the real network. By design, not a limitation Real-time mempool monitoring Crucible is a replay tool, not a monitoring tool Social engineering / phishing Out of scope for a technical testbed

docs.title: Step 1: Identify Your Target
docs.text:
# Example: You want to study bridge message verification vulnerabilities
# Which attacks are relevant?
- Nomad Bridge (forged Merkle proof) — [ok] L2 complete
- Wormhole (guardian signature bypass) — [ok] L2 complete  
- BNB TokenHub (IAVL proof bypass) — 🚧 scaffold, needs research fill

docs.title: Step 2: Check Chain Requirements
docs.text: If your target is on... You need... Ethereum only Free RPC (LlamaNodes) Ethereum + Polygon Paid RPC for Polygon archive BSC only Paid RPC for BSC archive Harmony Free RPC (Ankr)

docs.title: Step 3: Choose Fidelity Level
docs.text: If you want to... Target fidelity Prove a vulnerability exists L2 Show exactly how the exploit executed L3 Demonstrate cross-chain impact L4

docs.title: Step 4: Find or Create the Attack Pack
docs.text:
# Check if it exists
ls attacks/
crucible plugin list --type attack

# If it's a scaffold, fill in:
crucible plugin validate attacks/<name>  # check what's missing
# Then update evidence.yaml, scenario.yaml, and plugin code

See docs/ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md for the complete guide.

docs.title: Step 5: Run With Publication Gates
docs.text:
# Run with publication mode for research-grade gates
crucible experiment run experiments/<name>.yaml --publication-mode

docs.title: Reproducibility Guarantees
docs.text: Claim Detail Bit-for-bit identical runs Same topology, same fork block, same attack → same gas, events, storage, balances [ok] Cross-provider reproduction Same results across different archive RPC providers (LlamaNodes vs Alchemy) [ok] Cross-environment Same results in Docker and native local [ok] Cross-machine Same results on different machines with same git commit [ok]

docs.title: What's NOT guaranteed identical
docs.text:
• Cache timing (cold vs warm fork)
  • RPC provider response ordering (doesn't affect on-chain state)
  • Cross-chain relay timing (multi-chain experiments)
  • Artifact file ordering (mitigated by canonical sort in diff)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers docs/BENCHMARKS.md Per-attack fidelity assessment and completion status docs/RPC_PROVIDER_GUIDE.md Archive RPC provider comparison and costs docs/ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md Step-by-step guide for creating attack packs docs/RESEARCH_WORKFLOW.md Academic research workflow docs/REPRODUCIBILITY.md Provenance and determinism guarantees docs/CLI_REFERENCE.md All CLI commands and options docs/internal/BRIDGE_PLUGIN_LIMITATIONS.md Bridge plugin coverage, limitations, and future scope

docs.title: 1. Reproduction Standard
docs.text:
A benchmark is typically classified as "historically reproduced" once the corresponding attack pack exhibits:

  1. A declared fidelity level.
  2. A complete evidence.yaml manifest.
  3. Explicit fork blocks.
  4. Affected contract addresses.
  5. Historical exploit transactions (if the target fidelity is L3 or L4).
  6. Expected state, balance, and event assertions.
  7. A deterministic rerun comparison.
  8. An exported artifact bundle.

While Crucible is capable of executing scaffold packs prior to the completion of these fields, such executions constitute engineering validation rather than rigorous research evidence.

Fidelity Note: Crucible enforces strict adherence to a 5-level fidelity system (L0-L4). Please refer to the Fidelity Guide for detailed definitions of each level and the Capabilities Matrix for supported topologies and bridge plugins. Fidelity enforcement is enabled by default. The --no-fidelity flag may be utilized to bypass this check during early development.

docs.title: 2. Attack Pack Lifecycle
docs.text: The development of a robust reproduction follows a sequential lifecycle. For comprehensive instructions regarding research, evidence attribution, plugin development, and validation, refer to the Attack Pack Guide.

docs.title: Phase A: Scaffold Generation
docs.text:
Accelerated Generation (via Transaction Hash):

crucible attack extract <tx_hash> --name my_attack --chain ethereum

This procedure automatically generates the complete scaffold structure (plugin.yaml, evidence.yaml, attack.py, scenario.yaml, ABI mapping).

Manual Generation:

attacks/<attack>/
  plugin.yaml
  evidence.yaml
  <attack>.py

Validate the structural integrity of the scaffold:

crucible plugin validate attacks/<attack>

docs.title: Phase B: Evidence Aggregation
docs.text:
Evidence aggregation is a strictly manual intelligence-gathering process. Researchers typically conduct off-chain analysis to ascertain the exact parameters of the historical exploit.

This verified data is then manually incorporated into evidence.yaml, which generally includes:

  • Official Postmortems: Links to Rekt News, BlockSec, or protocol postmortems.
  • Block Explorer References: Etherscan (or equivalent) links to the attacker's wallet and the vulnerable contracts.
  • Contract Addresses: The exact deployment addresses of the exploited contracts.
  • Vulnerable Function Selectors: The specific function signatures utilized to trigger the exploit.
  • Fork Block Numbers: The specific block number immediately preceding the attack transaction (used by Anvil to initialize the fork state).
  • Exploit Transaction Hashes: The authoritative transaction(s) that executed the attack.
  • State and Balance Expectations: The anticipated financial impact (e.g., token drain amounts).

docs.title: Phase C: Scenario Definition
docs.text:
Populate scenarios/<scenario>/scenario.yaml with concrete state assertions.

A comprehensive reproduction typically demonstrates:

  • The vulnerable execution path succeeds.
  • The attacker's on-chain position improves.
  • Protected state (e.g., bridge vaults) is materially altered.

docs.title: Phase D: Baseline Capture
docs.text:
Initialize the topology (the setup command may be used to execute the fork and bootstrap phases concurrently):

crucible setup --topology bridge
# OR manually:
crucible fork --topology bridge

Capture and verify the environmental baseline:

crucible baseline capture <scenario> --topology bridge
crucible baseline verify <scenario>

docs.title: Phase E: Formal Execution
docs.text:
Execute the experiment against the deterministic baseline:

crucible experiment run experiments/<attack>_replay.yaml
crucible history --rebuild
crucible logs list

docs.title: Phase F: Export & Verification
docs.text:
Export the resulting telemetry and artifacts for publication:

crucible export <run_id>                          # Export as directory
crucible export <run_id> --format tar-gz          # Export as compressed archive
crucible verify datasets/<run_id>                 # Verify bundle integrity

docs.title: Determinism Verification
docs.text:
Execute the identical experiment twice following the restoration of the exact baseline:

crucible reset --scenario <scenario>
crucible experiment run experiments/<attack>_replay.yaml

crucible reset --scenario <scenario>
crucible experiment run experiments/<attack>_replay.yaml

crucible diff <run_a> <run_b>

The diff result should ideally display:

  • storage_equal: true
  • events_equal: true
  • gas_equal: true (unless the experiment explicitly declares a gas tolerance)

docs.title: Patch Validation Workflow
docs.text:
If evaluating a patch, validation necessitates two parallel experiments:

  1. Vulnerable Replay: The historical exploit succeeds.
  2. Patched Replay: The exploit fails, but standard protocol operations remain functional.

The patch artifact should be structured as follows:

patches/<name>/
  patch.yaml
  validation.yaml
  contracts-or-scripts/

A complete patch evaluation generally includes both positive assertions (normal behavior succeeds) and negative assertions (exploit behavior fails).

docs.title: RPC Provider Guide
docs.text: Crucible reproduces historical blockchain exploits by forking the chain at a specific block using Foundry's anvil. To accomplish this, anvil necessitates an archive node (or "full archive node") capable of serving historical state data.

docs.title: 1. Archive vs Full Node
docs.text:
Full nodes aggressively prune state data. Archive nodes retain the entire state trie since genesis.

Feature Full Node Archive Node Latest block state Supported Supported Historical state (eth_getBalance at old block) Fails (pruned after ~128 blocks) Supported (stores all state since genesis) eth_getBlockByNumber (old blocks) Supported (block headers kept) Supported eth_getCode / eth_call (old blocks) Fails Supported Disk space (Ethereum) ~1 TB ~12+ TB Disk space (BSC) ~1 TB ~10+ TB

Crucial Requirement: For multi-chain exploits involving BSC or Polygon at 2021-era blocks, paid archive providers are almost always required as free providers aggressively prune non-Ethereum chains.

docs.title: Ethereum (Free Archive Available)
docs.text: Provider Archive Depth Rate Limit Cost Practicality for Testbed Ankr (rpc.ankr.com/eth) Full archive (with API key) 300M CU/month Free tier Best free option — sign up at app.ankr.com LlamaNodes (eth.llamarpc.com) Full archive 25 req/s Free Usable, but frequently rate-limits Anvil PublicNode Limited (~128 blocks) 10 req/s Free Fails for deep historical blocks Alchemy Full archive Varies Free tier + paid Excellent QuickNode Full archive Varies Paid Excellent

docs.title: BSC & Polygon (Paid Archive Recommended)
docs.text:
Free tiers for BSC and Polygon routinely fail to serve complete state queries (e.g., anvil_setBalance) at older blocks.

Provider Archive Depth Cost Practicality for Testbed GetBlock (go.getblock.io) Full archive ~$39/mo Highly Recommended (Tested) QuickNode Full archive Varies Excellent (Per-chain pricing) Chainstack Full archive Varies Excellent Ankr Full archive Varies Good (Requires Premium tier for L2s) LlamaNodes / PublicNode Pruned Free Fails for historical exploit reproduction

docs.title: 3. Recommended Provider: GetBlock
docs.text:
Based on extensive operational testing, GetBlock (Starter Plan) is the most practical option for multi-chain research:

  • Coverage: Full Archive for BSC, Polygon, and Ethereum.
  • Rate Limit: 20 RPS (sufficient for Anvil's throttled fetching).
  • Cost: ~$39/mo (supports 2 endpoints simultaneously, covering BSC + Polygon).

docs.title: Step 1: Set Environment Variables
docs.text:
Configure your provider credentials in the .env file at the root of the testbed:

# Ethereum archive — ANKR free tier (sign up at https://app.ankr.com)
ETH_RPC_URL=https://rpc.ankr.com/eth/YOUR_ANKR_API_KEY

# BSC Archive (GetBlock or your premium provider)
BSC_RPC_URL=https://go.getblock.io/YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN

# Polygon Archive (GetBlock or your premium provider)
POLYGON_RPC_URL=https://go.getblock.io/YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Rate Limiting
docs.text:
Crucible employs a robust TokenBucket algorithm to ensure Anvil's heavy parallel fetching does not exceed your provider's rate limits (mitigating HTTP 429 Too Many Requests errors).

Configure these limits per-provider within testbed.yaml:

rpc_providers:
  ethereum:
    - name: ankr_free
      url: "${ETH_RPC_URL}"
      rate_limit: 30       # 30 requests per second
      burst_limit: 50      # Allow temporary bursts up to 50

Fallback Providers: You may define multiple providers per chain. If a provider reaches its rate limit or encounters an outage, the testbed will automatically failover to the next configured endpoint.

docs.title: 1. Agent Lifecycle
docs.text:
The autonomous evaluation loop consists of six distinct phases:

  1. Prepare — The agent initializes configuration parameters from the run: block of the experiment YAML, defining the LLM provider, inference model, capability scope (privilege), and primary objective. The provider is dynamically instantiated via the from_run_config() factory.
  2. Observe — The agent systematically reads the environment state, assessing the execution history, available tool sets, and outputs of recent operations.
  3. Think — The agent transmits the environment state and execution history to the LLM provider. The model evaluates the state and yields the subsequent operational directive.
  4. Act — The agent executes the prescribed tool via the Crucible RPC proxy layer (facilitating operations such as impersonation, balance assertions, and storage queries).
  5. Repeat — Iterations of steps 2-4 continue until the is_done() constraint returns True, or the agent explicitly signals objective completion.
  6. Verify — A final verification pass is executed against the outcome constraints.

docs.title: 2. Configuration Specification
docs.text:
Experiment YAML files require the following structural declarations for agent deployment:

name: my_agent_experiment
scenario: demo_scenario          # Any scenario supporting a single-chain topology
attack: demo_agent               # Target plugin directory: attacks/<name>/plugin.yaml
topology: single

run:
  # ── Provider Parameters ───────────────────────────────────────
  agent_provider: openai         # Supported variants: openai | anthropic | ollama | local | custom
  agent_model: gpt-4o            # Model identifier supported by the declared provider
  # agent_base_url: ...          # Optional: Override the default provider endpoint URL

  # ── Runtime Constraints ───────────────────────────────────────
  agent_privilege: medium        # Privilege boundary: low | medium | high
  agent_temperature: 0.3         # Determinism parameter (Default: 0.7; Lower represents stricter determinism)
  agent_max_iterations: 30       # Iteration boundary preventing runaway evaluation (Default: 50)
  agent_llm_budget: 10000        # Maximum cumulative token consumption limit
  agent_task: "Explore the forked chain and report findings"
  agent_objective: "Use available tools to check chain ID, balances, and contract code"
  agent_allowed_accounts: []     # Allowed Ethereum addresses (Empty evaluates to unconstrained)

  # ── Deterministic Recording ───────────────────────────────────
  # record_path: tests/fixtures/my_agent.jsonl  # Path to capture RPC telemetry

  # ── Base Experiment Parameters ────────────────────────────────
  traces: false
  storage_diffs: true
  gas_profiling: true

tags:
  - agent

docs.title: 1.1. Supported Providers
docs.text: Type Default Model Authentication Required Notes openai gpt-4o OPENAI_API_KEY (.env) Compatible with Azure and vLLM proxying via agent_base_url. anthropic claude-sonnet-4-20250514 ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (.env) Connects via the Anthropic Messages API. ollama llama3.1 No Automatically establishes connection to http://localhost:11434/v1. local local-model Optional Mandates explicit agent_base_url configuration. custom local-model Optional Mandates explicit agent_base_url configuration.

docs.title: 1.2. Privilege Capabilities
docs.text: Boundary Level Capabilities Tools Granted low Read-only chain_id, get_balance, get_code, get_storage, call_contract, get_logs medium Execution Base tools + impersonate, send_transaction high Manipulation Base and Execution tools + advance_time

docs.title: 1.3. Advanced Constraint Management
docs.text: Key Default Description agent_temperature 0.7 Model sampling parameter. Configurations between 0.1-0.3 enforce strict deterministic progression. agent_max_iterations 50 Failsafe mechanism bounding maximum sequential tool calls before strict termination. agent_llm_budget None Caps cumulative token consumption to prevent runaway costs. Yields a warning at the 90% threshold and enforces a hard stop upon breach. agent_allowed_accounts [] White-list of Ethereum addresses permitted for agent interaction. record_path None JSONL path to persist all JSON-RPC calls for subsequent hermetic evaluation.

docs.title: 3. Provider Auto-Configuration
docs.text:
During execution, crucible experiment run evaluates the agent_provider parameter within the YAML configuration and automatically leverages the from_run_config() factory defined on LLMAgentPlugin.

This architectural pattern externalizes provider orchestration entirely to the YAML schema, removing the necessity to alter Python source code when substituting models:

# Provider Substitution: OpenAI
agent_provider: openai
agent_model: gpt-4o

# Provider Substitution: Anthropic
agent_provider: anthropic
agent_model: claude-sonnet-4-20250514

Redundant provider re-initialization is prevented within LLMAgentPlugin.prepare() via the internal _configured_from_run_config flag.

docs.title: 4. Built-In Agent Packs
docs.text: Crucible provides primary agent reference implementations within the local attacks/ directory:

docs.title: 3.1. Demo Agent (attacks/demo_agent/)
docs.text:
A foundational reference implementation designed for environment traversal. The agent verifies block attributes, contract initialization, and account configurations.

crucible experiment run experiments/demo_agent.yaml

docs.title: 3.2. Nomad Exploit Agent (attacks/llm_exploit_agent/)
docs.text:
An advanced adversarial implementation explicitly targeting the Nomad bridge Replica contract (0xB923...). Operational sequence:

  1. Verification of Replica contract code availability.
  2. Retrieval of the internal committedRoot mapping.
  3. Forgery of a process(bytes,bytes) function call manipulating selector 0x61bd0e46.
  4. Transaction transmission exploiting the vulnerability bypass.
  5. Assertion verification on post-exploit root transitions.

docs.title: 5. Developing Custom Agents
docs.text:
Custom agent implementations necessitate subclassing LLMAgentPlugin and implementing necessary interface overrides:

from crucible.agent_loop import LLMAgentPlugin
from crucible.agent_tools import AgentContext

class MyAgent(LLMAgentPlugin):
    def metadata(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "name": "my_agent",
            "version": "1.0.0",
            "type": "agent",
        }

    def build_objective(self, agent_ctx: AgentContext) -> str:
        """Formalize the objective constraint for the LLM."""
        return "Your custom objective here."

Configuration requires explicit plugin registration within attacks/<name>/plugin.yaml.

docs.title: 5.1. Reasoning Telemetry
docs.text: Agent operations produce an agent_reasoning.jsonl log. Each recorded transaction preserves the execution context, tool parameterization, and conversational history facilitating post-run review.

docs.title: 5.2. Hermetic RPC Recording
docs.text:
For deterministic reproduction without external RPC dependencies, agents can record local network interactions:

crucible experiment run experiments/llm_exploit_agent.yaml \
  --record tests/fixtures/llm_exploit_agent.jsonl

docs.title: 5.3. Isolated Testing Validation
docs.text:
To perform isolated lifecycle validation without external LLM dependencies, standard classes may override the think() evaluation routine or pass a mock provider architecture natively during configuration.

Note: For diagnostic parameters and troubleshooting workflows pertaining to LLM agents (e.g., token limits, Ollama connectivity, model segfaults), please refer directly to the centralized Crucible Troubleshooting Guide.

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text:
A scenario defines the deterministic blockchain environment prior to the execution of an experiment. It provides the execution context for the attack plugin by specifying:

  1. Target Chains: The EVM networks to be forked.
  2. State Verification: Pre-execution assertions on storage slots or contract existence.
  3. Preconditions: Setup operations (e.g., helper contract deployment, account funding).
  4. Postconditions: Assertions validating the outcome of the experiment.

docs.title: Architectural Separation
docs.text:
The testbed decouples environment setup from exploit execution to maximize reusability:

Component Purpose Format Scenario (scenarios/<name>/scenario.yaml) Environment initialization, state preparation, and precondition enforcement. Declarative YAML Attack Plugin (attacks/<name>/<name>.py) Exploit execution (calldata forging, transaction submission). Python Experiment (experiments/<name>_replay.yaml) Orchestration layer binding the scenario, attack plugin, and topology. Declarative YAML

This decoupling allows researchers to: - Test the same attack plugin against multiple scenarios (e.g., different fork blocks or state variations). - Apply different attack variations within the same predefined scenario. - Swap underlying network topologies without modifying the attack implementation.

docs.title: Scenario Definition Structure
docs.text:
Scenarios are defined in YAML. A standard scenario organizes operations into ordered phases.

name: my_attack_pre_exploit
description: >-
  Initializes the Ethereum fork at block 15259100.
  Verifies vulnerable contract state prior to exploit execution.
version: "1.0.0"
chains:
  - ethereum

phases:
  - name: setup
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Initializing Ethereum fork at block 15259100"
  - name: attack
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Yielding execution to attack plugin"
  - name: verify
    steps:
      - type: assert
        name: exploit_succeeds
        expected: true
        actual: true

docs.title: Unstructured Execution (Simple Format)
docs.text:
For scenarios that do not require explicit setup, attack, and verify phases, Crucible supports a flat execution model using the steps: array. In this format, all scenario steps execute sequentially prior to the attack plugin.

name: simple_scenario
chains: [ethereum]
steps:
  - type: log
    message: "Executing preconditions"
  - type: impersonate
    chain: ethereum
    account: "0xAttacker"

docs.title: Directory Hierarchy
docs.text:
scenarios/
  my_attack_pre_exploit/
    scenario.yaml          # Scenario definition manifest
    steps.py               # Optional Python extensions (for python_script steps)

docs.title: Phase Execution and Checkpointing
docs.text:
Scenarios execute in ordered phases. The attack plugin's execution is automatically interleaved between the scenario's phases by the ExperimentRunner.

flowchart LR
    P1["Phase 1: setup<br>Precondition steps executed"]
    P2["Phase 2: attack<br>Attack plugin execute() method invoked"]
    P3["Phase 3: verify<br>Postcondition steps executed"]

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

docs.title: Phase Checkpoints
docs.text:
The CheckpointManager captures a deterministic EVM state snapshot upon the completion of each phase.

Artifact Structure: 

states/checkpoints/
  <run_id>/
    checkpoint.json             # Execution manifest (phases, timestamps)
    integrity.json              # SHA-256 integrity hashes
    setup.ethereum.json         # EVM state snapshot post-setup
    attack.ethereum.json        # EVM state snapshot post-attack
    verify.ethereum.json        # EVM state snapshot post-verify

Mechanics: 1. Upon phase completion, anvil_dumpState captures the EVM state for all chains in the active topology. 2. Snapshots are structurally validated and hashed (SHA-256). 3. integrity.json maintains the authoritative hash record to prevent silent data corruption. 4. If execution is interrupted, ExperimentRunner.resume() restores the latest valid snapshot via anvil_loadState.

# Resume an interrupted experiment from the latest checkpoint
crucible experiment resume DS-2026-00155

# Enumerate available checkpoints
crucible experiment checkpoints

Note: Checkpoints are scoped to the active topology. Stale .meta.json artifacts from previous topologies are automatically excluded.

docs.title: Scenario Step Reference
docs.text: The following step types are supported within scenario phases.

docs.title: 1. log
docs.text:
Records an informational message to the experiment execution log (run.jsonl).

- type: log
  message: "Contract state verified at fork block"

docs.title: 2. assert
docs.text:
Evaluates a boolean condition. Execution halts and the experiment is marked as a failure if the condition is false.

- type: assert
  name: vulnerability_exists
  expected: true
  actual: true

docs.title: 3. impersonate
docs.text:
Instructs the Anvil node to bypass private key validation for a specific address.

- type: impersonate
  chain: ethereum
  account: "0xRelayerAddress"

docs.title: 4. send_tx
docs.text:
Dispatches a transaction to the specified chain. Supports symbolic address resolution.

- type: send_tx
  chain: ethereum
  from: "0xAttackerAddress"
  to: "bridge.ethereum"
  value: 1000000000000000000
  data: "0x123456"

docs.title: 5. advance_time
docs.text:
Advances the EVM timestamp and forces block generation.

- type: advance_time
  chain: polygon
  seconds: 3600

docs.title: 6. assert_balance
docs.text:
Verifies that the native token balance of an address meets specific constraints.

- type: assert_balance
  chain: ethereum
  account: "0xVaultAddress"
  min_value: 1000000000000000000  # Optional: Minimum balance in wei
  exact_value: 0                  # Optional: Exact expected balance

docs.title: 7. forge_script
docs.text:
Executes a Foundry Solidity script via subprocess, injecting the current RPC URL. The script must reside in scenarios/<name>/ or scripts/.

- type: forge_script
  chain: ethereum
  script: "Exploit.s.sol:ExploitScript"
  extra_args:
    - "--sender"
    - "0xAttackerAddress"

docs.title: 8. python_script
docs.text:
Dynamically loads and executes a Python script. By default, it searches for steps.py within the scenario directory, though a custom path may be specified to execute shared scripts.

- type: python_script
  script: "scripts/shared_setup.py"  # Optional: Defaults to "steps.py"

The script path is resolved in the following order: 1. Relative to the workspace root (e.g., for shared scripts). 2. Relative to the scenario directory (scenarios/<name>/).

The script must implement a run(context: ExperimentContext) entrypoint:

from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient
from crucible.sdk import ExperimentContext

def run(context: ExperimentContext):
    rpc_url = context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url
    client = JsonRpcClient(rpc_url)
    # Implementation logic

docs.title: 9. verify_tracing
docs.text:
Validates that the instrumentation proxy has successfully captured transaction traces for the specified chains. Minimum threshold constraints may be enforced to ensure sufficient artifact generation.

- type: verify_tracing
  chains:
    - ethereum
    - polygon
  min_events: 1               # Optional: Require at least 1 event emitted
  min_gas_entries: 1          # Optional: Require at least 1 gas profile entry
  min_storage_diffs: 0        # Optional: Require minimum storage slots changed
  min_balance_diffs: 0        # Optional: Require minimum balance changes
  check_consistency: true     # Optional: Ensure gas entries exist for every event tx

docs.title: 10. bridge_action
docs.text:
Executes a protocol-specific action via the active bridge plugin configured in the topology. Supported actions include: send_message, relay_messages, check_delivery, and verify_state.

- type: bridge_action
  action: send_message
  source_chain: ethereum
  dest_chain: polygon
  data: "0x..."
  correlation_id: "msg-001"   # Optional: Tag for cross-chain correlation

For checking delivery: 

- type: bridge_action
  action: check_delivery
  correlation_id: "msg-001"
  source_tx_hash: "0x..."     # Match against the source transaction hash

docs.title: Experiment Orchestration
docs.text:
The experiment YAML definition binds the scenario, attack plugin, and topology.

# experiments/my_attack_replay.yaml
name: my_attack_replay
scenario: my_attack_pre_exploit
attack: my_attack
topology: single
run:
  traces: true
  storage_diffs: true
  gas_profiling: true
tags: [my_attack, l2]

Execution Pipeline:

sequenceDiagram
    participant CLI as crucible CLI
    participant Config as testbed.yaml & evidence.yaml
    participant Runner as ExperimentRunner
    participant Anvil as Anvil Forks
    participant Scenario as Scenario Phases
    participant Attack as Attack Plugin

    CLI->>Runner: Request experiment execution
    Runner->>Config: Merge fork_blocks from evidence.yaml
    Runner->>Anvil: Spawn forks at target block heights
    Runner->>Scenario: Execute `setup` phase steps
    Runner->>Runner: Initialize artifact capture (tracing)
    Runner->>Attack: Invoke `execute()` method (Exploit runs)
    Runner->>Attack: Invoke `verify()` method
    Runner->>Scenario: Execute `verify` phase steps
    Runner->>Runner: Finalize and hash artifacts (run.jsonl, etc.)
    Runner->>CLI: Return execution report
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

Step-by-Step Flow: 1. The fork_blocks configuration is automatically merged from attacks/<name>/evidence.yaml. 2. Target chains are forked at the merged block heights. 3. Scenario setup phase steps are executed. 4. The instrumentation proxy initiates trace capture. 5. The attack plugin's execute() method is invoked. 6. The attack plugin's verify() method is invoked. 7. Scenario verify phase steps are executed. 8. Artifacts (events.jsonl, run.jsonl, etc.) are finalized and hashed.

Note: To bypass fork block auto-merging from evidence.yaml, use the --no-auto-merge CLI flag.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Error Condition Root Cause Remediation Scenario not found Directory structure mismatch Ensure scenarios/<name>/scenario.yaml exists Unknown step type Invalid step declaration Refer to the Step Reference section python_script missing steps.py Missing Python extension file Ensure steps.py exists alongside scenario.yaml Chain X not in topology Scenario requests an unconfigured chain Update the scenario chains array or topology definition Fork block mismatch testbed.yaml out of sync Update attacks/<name>/evidence.yaml (auto-merged at runtime)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Reference Purpose docs/ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md Attack plugin development guidelines docs/CLI_REFERENCE.md Command-line interface documentation docs/REPRODUCIBILITY.md Determinism and provenance architectures

docs.title: Simple Operations & Ad-Hoc Testing
docs.text: Purpose: Crucible isn't just for automated exploit reproduction. It's also a managed Anvil orchestrator for day-to-day development, manual testing, contract debugging, and chain exploration. This guide covers granular operational procedures.

docs.title: Quick Start: Fork & Use in 30 Seconds
docs.text:
# 1. Fork a chain
crucible fork --topology single

# 2. See what's running
crucible status

# 3. Fund an address and start transacting
cast rpc anvil_setBalance 0xYourAddress \
  $(cast to-wei 1000 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

cast send 0xTargetContract "transfer(address,uint256)" \
  0xRecipient $(cast to-wei 10 ether) \
  --unlocked --from 0xYourAddress \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 4. Clean up when done
crucible clean

docs.title: Basic Fork
docs.text:
# Fork a single Ethereum chain (port 8545)
crucible fork --topology single

# Fork multiple chains (Ethereum on 8545, Polygon on 8546)
crucible fork --topology dual

# Fork with a longer timeout for cold-cache chains (e.g., Polygon)
crucible fork --topology dual --fork-timeout 600

Following instantiation, Anvil executes on localhost. Any compatible tool may interact with this endpoint: - cast, forge (Foundry) - curl (raw JSON-RPC) - web3.py, ethers.js, viem - Crucible's own JsonRpcClient

docs.title: With Baseline Setup
docs.text:
# Full setup: fork + bootstrap + capture golden baseline
crucible setup --topology single

# Now the environment may be reset to this state at any time
crucible reset --scenario my_scenario --mode soft

docs.title: Blank Fork (No Archive RPC Needed)
docs.text:
For testing contracts without forking from a historical block, set fork_block to null in your topology:

# testbed.local.yaml
chains:
  ethereum:
    fork_block: null   # blank fork â€” starts from genesis
    port: 8545

Then fork normally:

crucible fork --topology single   # clean Anvil, no remote dependency

docs.title: 2. Checking What's Running
docs.text:
# Show all running Anvil processes
crucible status

Output example:

â”Œâ”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”¬â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”¬â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”¬â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”¬â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”¬â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”¬â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”¬â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”
â”‚ Name     â”‚ PID  â”‚ Port â”‚ Block â”‚ Status â”‚ Gas Price â”‚ Base Fee â”‚ RPC                     â”‚
â”œâ”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”¼â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”¼â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”¼â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”¼â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”¼â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”¼â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”¼â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”¤
â”‚ ethereum â”‚ 1234 â”‚ 8545 â”‚ 12996693 â”‚ ALIVE â”‚ 0         â”‚ 1gwei    â”‚ http://127.0.0.1:8545   â”‚
â”‚ polygon  â”‚ 1235 â”‚ 8546 â”‚ 16332982 â”‚ ALIVE â”‚ 0         â”‚ 1gwei    â”‚ http://127.0.0.1:8546   â”‚

Also see disk usage:

crucible status --disk

docs.title: Named Snapshots
docs.text:
Snapshots save the full EVM state (all contracts, storage, balances) of a running chain.

# Create a snapshot
crucible snapshot create my_state

# Restore a snapshot (instant â€” loads saved state back into memory)
crucible snapshot restore my_state --chain ethereum

# Snapshot specific chains in multi-chain topologies
crucible snapshot create phase1 --chain ethereum
crucible snapshot create phase1 --chain polygon

Snapshots are stored in states/snapshots/ as JSON files.

docs.title: Three-Tier Reset
docs.text:
Reset to a clean baseline state without re-forking:

Mode Speed What Happens soft <5s anvil_loadState from baseline snapshot â€” no process restart medium <30s Kill Anvil, relaunch, then restore snapshot hard <5min Kill, re-fork from archive RPC, re-run bootstrap, restore snapshot 
# Fastest â€” just reload state
crucible reset --scenario single --mode soft

# Full clean restart
crucible reset --scenario single --mode hard

docs.title: Save and Restore Custom States
docs.text:
from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient

client = JsonRpcClient("http://127.0.0.1:8545")

# Save the entire EVM state
state = client.dump_state()  # returns huge dict
import json
with open("my_state.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(state, f)

# Later: restore it
with open("my_state.json") as f:
    saved = json.load(f)
client.load_state(saved)

docs.title: Account Model
docs.text: Crucible starts Anvil with --accounts 0 --balance 0. This means no pre-funded test accounts are created. This is intentional â€” some archive nodes fail when Anvil tries to prefetch balance for the default Foundry accounts at historical blocks.

docs.title: Fund Any Address
docs.text:
Given that --auto-impersonate is permanently enabled, any address may be utilized, provided it is sufficiently funded.

With cast: 

# Fund with 1000 ETH
cast rpc anvil_setBalance \
  0xYourWalletAddress \
  $(cast to-wei 1000 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# Fund with 10000 ETH (for contract deployment)
cast rpc anvil_setBalance \
  0xDeployerAddress \
  $(cast to-wei 10000 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

With Python: 

from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient

client = JsonRpcClient("http://127.0.0.1:8545")
client.set_balance("0xYourWalletAddress", hex(1000 * 10**18))

With curl: 

curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8545 -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
    "jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,
    "method":"anvil_setBalance",
    "params":["0xYourWalletAddress", "0x3635C9ADC5DEA00000"]
  }'

ðŸ’¡ Tip: Create a shell script fund.sh that funds your test accounts: 

#!/bin/bash
RPC="http://127.0.0.1:8545"
ETH1000=$(cast to-wei 1000 ether)
for addr in "$@"; do
  cast rpc anvil_setBalance "$addr" "$ETH1000" --rpc-url "$RPC"
done
Usage: ./fund.sh 0xAlice 0xBob 0xCharlie

docs.title: Check Balances
docs.text:
cast balance 0xYourWalletAddress --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545
cast balance 0xTargetContract --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: Use Auto-Impersonation (No Private Keys Needed)
docs.text:
The --auto-impersonate flag permits transaction dispatch from any address without cryptographic signatures:

# Send from a contract owner address (no private key needed!)
cast send 0xVulnerableContract "approve(address,uint256)" \
  0xSpender 1000000 \
  --unlocked --from 0xOwnerAddress \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

The --unlocked flag tells Anvil to skip signature verification because the sender is impersonated.

docs.title: Stop Impersonating
docs.text:
cast rpc anvil_stopImpersonatingAccount 0xOwnerAddress \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: With cast send
docs.text:
# Simple ETH transfer
cast send 0xRecipient \
  --value $(cast to-wei 1 ether) \
  --unlocked --from 0xYourAddress \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# Contract call
cast send 0xTokenContract \
  "transfer(address,uint256)" \
  0xRecipient $(cast to-wei 100 ether) \
  --unlocked --from 0xTokenOwner \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# With custom gas
cast send 0xContract "exploit()" \
  --gas-limit 500000 \
  --unlocked --from 0xAttacker \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: With Python (Crucible's built-in client)
docs.text:
from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient
from web3 import Web3

client = JsonRpcClient("http://127.0.0.1:8545")

# Fund the sender first
client.set_balance("0xSender", hex(10 * 10**18))

# Impersonate and send
client.impersonate_account("0xSender")
tx_hash = client.send_transaction({
    "from": "0xSender",
    "to": "0xRecipient",
    "value": hex(10**18),  # 1 ETH
    "gas": hex(21000),
    "gasPrice": hex(0),
})
print(f"Tx hash: {tx_hash}")

docs.title: With forge script
docs.text:
forge script DeployMyContract \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545 \
  --unlocked --broadcast

âš ï¸ Note: When using forge script against a Crucible fork, the deployer address (default: 0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266) needs to be funded first with anvil_setBalance since Crucible uses --accounts 0.

docs.title: With cast call
docs.text:
# Query any contract state
cast call 0xTokenContract "totalSupply()" \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

cast call 0xTokenContract "balanceOf(address)" \
  0xSomeAddress --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# With block specification
cast call 0xContract "owner()" \
  --block 12996693 --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: With Python
docs.text:
from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient

client = JsonRpcClient("http://127.0.0.1:8545")

# Raw eth_call
result = client.call("eth_call", [{
    "to": "0xTokenContract",
    "data": "0x18160ddd",  # totalSupply() selector
}, "latest"])

# Higher-level helpers
balance = client.get_balance("0xSomeAddress")  # returns int (wei)
code = client.get_code("0xContract")           # returns hex bytecode
storage = client.get_storage_at("0xContract", "0x0")  # slot 0

docs.title: Set Storage Slots
docs.text:
# Directly write to a contract's storage
cast rpc anvil_setStorageAt \
  0xContractAddress \
  0xStorageSlotHex \
  0xNewValueHex \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

Python: 

client.set_storage_at("0xContract", "0x0", "0xNewValue")

docs.title: Set Code
docs.text:
Replace a contract's bytecode at runtime (useful for testing upgrade patterns):

cast rpc anvil_setCode \
  0xContractAddress \
  0xNewBytecode \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

client.call("anvil_setCode", ["0xContractAddress", "0xNewBytecode"])

docs.title: Set Nonce
docs.text:
cast rpc anvil_setNonce \
  0xSomeAddress \
  0x10 \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: Advance Time
docs.text:
# Jump forward 1 day
cast rpc evm_increaseTime 86400 --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545
cast rpc evm_mine --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# Or use Crucible's helper
python -c "
from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient
c = JsonRpcClient('http://127.0.0.1:8545')
c.advance_time(86400)  # advances time + mines a block
"

docs.title: Mine Blocks
docs.text:
# Mine a single block
cast rpc evm_mine --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# Mine with specific timestamp
cast rpc evm_mine $(date +%s) --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# Mine N blocks
for i in {1..10}; do cast rpc evm_mine --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545; done

docs.title: Using forge create
docs.text:
# Fund the deployer first
cast rpc anvil_setBalance \
  0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266 \
  $(cast to-wei 100 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# Deploy
forge create src/MyContract.sol:MyContract \
  --constructor-args arg1 arg2 \
  --unlocked --from 0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266 \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: Using forge script
docs.text:
# 1. Fund the default deployer
cast rpc anvil_setBalance \
  0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266 \
  $(cast to-wei 100 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 2. Deploy with forge script
forge script script/Deploy.s.sol:DeployScript \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545 \
  --unlocked --broadcast --slow

docs.title: Using Python (Crucible SDK)
docs.text:
from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient

client = JsonRpcClient("http://127.0.0.1:8545")
client.set_balance("0xDeployer", hex(100 * 10**18))
client.impersonate_account("0xDeployer")

# Deploy by sending to null address with init code
tx_hash = client.send_transaction({
    "from": "0xDeployer",
    "data": "0x" + bytecode_hex,  # contract init code
    "gas": hex(2000000),
    "gasPrice": hex(0),
})

docs.title: 9. Working With Artifacts (Free Instrumentation)
docs.text:
Even during ad-hoc manual testing, Crucible's instrumentation proxy captures everything automatically if you use crucible setup instead of crucible fork:

crucible setup --topology single   # fork + bootstrap + instrumentation

Now every transaction â€” whether sent via cast, curl, Python, or forge script â€” produces artifact streams:

# See captured events
cat logs/setup/bootstrap/events.jsonl | head -5

# See gas used per transaction
cat logs/setup/bootstrap/gas_profile.jsonl

# See storage changes
cat logs/setup/bootstrap/storage_diffs.jsonl

# See balance changes
cat logs/setup/bootstrap/balance_diffs.jsonl

Each JSONL line is structured and queryable:

# Find all Transfer events
grep '"event_name":"Transfer"' logs/setup/bootstrap/events.jsonl

# Find storage changes for a specific contract
grep "0xTargetContract" logs/setup/bootstrap/storage_diffs.jsonl

To use the proxy URL instead of the raw Anvil URL â€” the proxy port is dynamically assigned and printed in the setup output:

# After crucible setup, the proxy URL is shown in the CLI output.
# Example output: "started instrumented proxy for ethereum on http://127.0.0.1:34567"
# Use that URL for all your cast calls to get automatic artifact capture:
cast call 0xContract "owner()" --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:34567

Note: The proxy URL changes on every crucible setup since the OS assigns an available port. Always get the current URL from the setup output or crucible status. To find the proxy URL programmatically, check the chain's meta file in states/*.meta.json for the proxied rpc_url field.

docs.title: Cross-Chain Testing (Dual Fork)
docs.text:
# Fork Ethereum + Polygon simultaneously
crucible fork --topology dual

# Ethereum on :8545, Polygon on :8546
cast block-number --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545
cast block-number --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8546

# Fund addresses on both chains
cast rpc anvil_setBalance 0xAddr $(cast to-wei 100 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545
cast rpc anvil_setBalance 0xAddr $(cast to-wei 100 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8546

docs.title: Interactive Jupyter Analysis
docs.text:
# Launch Jupyter with Crucible analysis helpers
crucible notebook

# In a notebook cell:
from crucible.analysis import load_run, list_runs
runs = list_runs()                     # all experiment runs
events = load_run(runs[0], "events")   # load events as DataFrame

docs.title: Record and Replay RPC Fixtures
docs.text:
Record all RPC traffic during a session for later replay:

# Run with record mode
crucible experiment run experiments/attack.yaml \
  --record tests/fixtures/my_session.jsonl

Then replay in CI without needing a live archive node:

from crucible.recording import ReplayServer
server = ReplayServer("tests/fixtures/my_session.jsonl")
server.start()
# Now ethereum RPC is served from the fixture, not a real node

docs.title: 11. Cleaning Up
docs.text: For the full list of clean commands â€” crucible clean, --all, --cache, --logs, --states, --checkpoints, --datasets, and --logs-older-than â€” see the Quick Reference.

docs.title: Fresh Start
docs.text:
To completely reset the workspace (stop chains, remove all artifacts, cache, and logs):

crucible clean --all --logs --datasets --confirm
crucible init  # recreate runtime directories

docs.title: Topology Mismatch â€” Running Bridge Experiments on Single Topology
docs.text:
Problem: You run a bridge experiment (e.g., experiments/nomad_replay.yaml which declares topology: bridge) but forked with --topology single. The experiment runs and produces artifacts, but the fidelity check fails with:

Runtime Fidelity: FAILED
Fidelity level L2 requires complete trace artifacts, but 1 transaction(s) failed auto-instrumentation.

Cause: Experiment YAML files declare a topology field that tells Crucible which chain setup they expect. When you fork with --topology single but the experiment expects bridge, Crucible's instrumentation proxy expects trace artifacts from a multi-chain setup. On a single chain, the bridge-specific instrumentation steps can't complete, causing the fidelity check to fail.

Fix: Match the fork topology to what the experiment expects:

# Check what topology the experiment needs first:
head -10 experiments/nomad_replay.yaml
# Look for: topology: bridge

# Fork with the correct topology:
crucible fork --topology bridge
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --no-baseline

Common experiment topologies:

Experiment Expected Topology Notes nomad_replay.yaml bridge Needs Ethereum + Polygon fork wormhole_replay.yaml bridge Needs Ethereum + Polygon fork poly_network_replay.yaml bridge Needs Ethereum + Polygon + BSC demo_experiment.yaml single Ethereum only â€” works with single topology horizon_replay.yaml single Ethereum only bnb_token_hub_replay.yaml single_bsc BSC only

In the absence of multi-chain RPC endpoints, attack logic may still be validated by utilizing --no-fidelity:

crucible fork --topology single
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --no-baseline --no-fidelity

This skips the fidelity check. The attack plugin still executes and produces results. Important: Runs with --no-fidelity bypass the publication gates â€” exported bundles will not pass L2 verification. Use this for development only; publications require actual multi-chain trace artifacts.

# 1. Fork a clean chain
crucible fork --topology single

# 2. Fund the default Forge deployer
cast rpc anvil_setBalance \
  0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266 \
  $(cast to-wei 10000 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 3. Deploy and test iteratively
forge script Deploy --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545 --unlocked --broadcast
forge test --fork-url http://127.0.0.1:8545 --match-test testFeature

# 4. Quick state check
cast call 0xDeployed "balanceOf(address)" 0xAddr --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545
cast rpc anvil_setStorageAt 0xDeployed 0xSlot 0xValue --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 5. Snapshot before risky experiments
crucible snapshot create before_hack

# 6. If something breaks â€” restore instantly
crucible snapshot restore before_hack --chain ethereum

docs.title: Workflow: Historical State Exploration
docs.text:
# 1. Fork at a specific block
crucible fork --topology single

# 2. Check what state looked like at that block
cast balance 0xOldContract --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545
cast call 0xOldContract "owner()" --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 3. Try to exploit a known vulnerability
cast send 0xOldContract "vulnerableFunction(bytes)" \
  $(cast calldata "exploit()") \
  --unlocked --from 0xAttacker \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 4. Did it work? Check storage
cast storage 0xOldContract 0xKeySlot --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 5. Reset and try a different approach
crucible reset --scenario single --mode soft

docs.title: Workflow: Gas Optimization Testing
docs.text:
# 1. Fork and setup instrumentation
crucible setup --topology single

# 2. Fund deployer
cast rpc anvil_setBalance 0xDeployer $(cast to-wei 100 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 3. Deploy optimized contract
forge create --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:<proxy-port> \  # use proxy URL from crucible setup output
  --unlocked --from 0xDeployer \
  src/Optimized.sol:Optimized

# 4. Check gas profile
grep "Deploy" logs/setup/bootstrap/gas_profile.jsonl

# 5. Snapshot, deploy old version, compare
crucible snapshot create optimized
# ... deploy old version ...
grep "Deploy" logs/setup/bootstrap/gas_profile.jsonl
crucible snapshot restore optimized --chain ethereum

docs.title: Address Has No Balance
docs.text:
Problem: cast send fails with "insufficient funds" or "sender doesn't have enough funds".

Fix: Fund the address before using it: 

cast rpc anvil_setBalance 0xYourAddress $(cast to-wei 100 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: "No matching key" When Using --unlocked
docs.text:
Problem: cast send --unlocked fails because the account isn't impersonated.

Fix: --auto-impersonate is enabled by default in Crucible, but cast may need the --from flag explicitly: 

cast send 0xContract "method()" \
  --unlocked --from 0xYourAddress \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: Forge Script Default Deployer Not Working
docs.text:
Problem: forge script fails with "insufficient funds" for the default deployer.

Fix: The default Foundry deployer (0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266) is not pre-funded in Crucible (because of --accounts 0). Fund it: 

cast rpc anvil_setBalance \
  0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266 \
  $(cast to-wei 10000 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: Port Already in Use
docs.text:
Problem: Can't fork because port 8545 is taken.

Fix: Check what's running and clean up: 

crucible clean   # stop all Crucible-managed chains
# Or manually: kill the old Anvil process
lsof -i :8545
kill <pid>

docs.title: Cold-Cache Fork Too Slow
docs.text:
Problem: Forking Polygon takes 3-5 minutes.

Fix: Increase timeout or use a faster archive RPC: 

crucible fork --topology dual --fork-timeout 600

The second fork will use the cache and be much faster (<30s).

docs.title: 14. All Available Anvil Cheatcodes
docs.text:
These are all forwarded through Crucible's instrumentation proxy:

Method Purpose Example anvil_setBalance Set ETH balance cast rpc anvil_setBalance 0xAddr 0xAmount anvil_impersonateAccount Impersonate any address anvil_impersonateAccount(addr) (auto-enabled) anvil_stopImpersonatingAccount Stop impersonating anvil_stopImpersonatingAccount(addr) anvil_setCode Replace contract bytecode anvil_setCode(addr, bytecode) anvil_setStorageAt Write storage slot anvil_setStorageAt(addr, slot, value) anvil_setNonce Set account nonce anvil_setNonce(addr, nonce) anvil_mine Mine blocks anvil_mine(blocks) anvil_setCoinbase Set coinbase address anvil_setCoinbase(addr) anvil_setChainId Set chain ID anvil_setChainId(id) anvil_dumpState Export full EVM state dump_state() â†’ JSON anvil_loadState Import full EVM state load_state(json) evm_increaseTime Jump forward increaseTime(seconds) evm_setNextBlockTimestamp Set next block time setNextBlockTimestamp(timestamp) evm_snapshot Create snapshot ID snapshot() â†’ id evm_revert Revert to snapshot revert(id)

All of these work through:

# cast (direct to Anvil port â€” all methods work)
cast rpc <method> <params> --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# Python (direct to Anvil port â€” all methods work)
client.call("<method>", [params])

# curl (direct to Anvil port â€” all methods work)
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8545 -d '{"method":"<method>","params":[...]}'

Proxy vs Direct: When using the instrumentation proxy URL, only a subset of Anvil methods are forwarded (see the proxy allowlist in Instrumentation Architecture). For unrestricted access to all cheatcodes, connect to the raw Anvil port directly (http://127.0.0.1:8545).

docs.title: 15. RPC Endpoints Reference
docs.text: Chain Default Port RPC URL Notes Ethereum 8545 http://127.0.0.1:8545 Default for --topology single Polygon 8546 http://127.0.0.1:8546 For --topology dual BSC 8547 http://127.0.0.1:8547 For custom topologies Harmony 8548 http://127.0.0.1:8548 For custom topologies Proxy (Ethereum) Dynamic http://127.0.0.1:<dynamic> OS-assigned, printed by crucible setup

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers Technical Overview System architecture, core concepts, design principles User Guide Full setup, Docker vs local, environment config CLI Reference All CLI commands and flags Plugin Development Guide Writing attack, bridge, and agent plugins Troubleshooting Guide Common errors and fixes Fidelity Guide Fidelity levels and enforcement

docs.title: Technical Overview
docs.text: Audience: Researchers and developers who want to understand how Crucible works, why it was built this way, and how the pieces fit together.

docs.title: Architectural Philosophy
docs.text: Crucible's architecture is guided by four core design principles:

docs.title: 1. Separation of Exploit Logic from Infrastructure
docs.text:
Crucible isolates artifact collection and chain management from the core exploit logic. Researchers authoring attack plugins can focus exclusively on exploit mechanics (e.g., crafting calldata, invoking contracts, and asserting state changes) without managing underlying infrastructure. The framework abstracts away:

  • Historical chain fork synchronization
  • Transaction instrumentation (event and storage diff capture)
  • Cross-run determinism verification
  • Artifact packaging for publication

docs.title: 2. Default Determinism
docs.text:
Blockchain state forks inherently exhibit non-deterministic properties due to RPC provider variations, transaction ordering, and fluctuating gas prices. Crucible enforces deterministic execution by default through the following mechanisms:

  • Fixed gas price (--gas-price 0) to eliminate gas-based variability.
  • FIFO transaction ordering (--order fifo) to bypass mempool sorting behavior.
  • Pinned fork blocks to ensure every run originates from an identical historical state.
  • Static fork-block timestamps to eliminate time-dependent execution divergence.
  • Auto-mine execution to resolve block timing variance.

These constraints ensure that unaltered execution paths yield identical artifacts across successive runs.

docs.title: 3. Transparent and Universal Instrumentation
docs.text:
A significant source of friction in blockchain security research is the overhead of instrumenting transaction logic to capture execution traces. Crucible utilizes an RPC proxy layer that intercepts communication between the attack execution and the local chain fork.

This proxy architecture enables universal artifact capture (gas profiles, event emissions, storage diffs, and balance changes) regardless of the attack's implementation language or tooling (e.g., Python, forge script, or cast), without requiring explicit instrumentation code within the attack logic.

docs.title: 4. Self-Validating Publication Artifacts
docs.text: Every experiment run generates a standardized, self-validating artifact bundle. These bundles employ SHA-256 hashing to ensure data integrity and include comprehensive run manifests that capture the execution provenance (git commit hash, Foundry version, and configuration fingerprint). Furthermore, automated fidelity enforcers verify that the empirical behavior of the run aligns with its declared reproducibility level.

docs.title: Architecture at a Glance
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    subgraph L4 ["CLI Layer"]
        A["crucible/commands/ (20 modules)<br>Thin wrappers, zero business logic"]
    end
    subgraph L3 ["Orchestration Layer"]
        B["ExperimentRunner, ScenarioEngine, BaselineManager<br>PublicationGate, HistoryIndex, ExportBundle<br>FidelityEnforcer, CheckpointManager"]
    end
    subgraph L2 ["Runtime Layer"]
        C["ChainManager, PluginRegistry, JsonRpcClient<br>RpcProxy, BridgeLifecycle, LLMAgentPlugin<br>RpcProviderManager"]
    end
    subgraph L1 ["Foundation Layer"]
        D["config.py, sdk.py, logging.py, errors.py<br>provenance.py, evidence.py, hashing.py, filesystem.py<br>env.py, ids.py, utils.py"]
    end

    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

docs.title: Layer Rules
docs.text: Layer Imports From Responsibility CLI (L4) All lower layers Command parsing, delegation â€” zero business logic Orchestration (L3) L2, L1 Coordinates multi-step workflows, manages state transitions Runtime (L2) L1 only Manages external processes (Anvil), I/O, plugin lifecycle Foundation (L1) stdlib only No upward imports, no external process dependencies

docs.title: Experiment
docs.text:
An experiment is a YAML file that wires together a topology, scenario, and attack plugin:

# experiments/nomad_replay.yaml
name: nomad_replay
scenario: nomad_pre_exploit
attack: nomad_replica_fraud
topology: single

Running crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml triggers the full experiment lifecycle: fork chains â†’ validate evidence â†’ deploy bridge â†’ run scenario steps â†’ start instrumentation â†’ execute attack â†’ verify fidelity â†’ capture artifacts.

docs.title: Topology
docs.text:
A topology declares which chains to fork and (optionally) which bridge plugin to deploy:

# topologies/bridge.yaml
name: bridge
chains: [ethereum, polygon]
bridge: nomad_bridge

Topologies are reusable â€” the same attack can run on different topologies without modification. Adding a new chain config doesn't affect existing experiments.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
A scenario defines the chain environment before the attack runs â€” contract state, impersonated accounts, initial conditions:

# scenarios/nomad_pre_exploit/scenario.yaml
phases:
  - name: setup
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Setting up Nomad Replica contract state"
      - type: impersonate
        chain: ethereum
        account: "0x..."
      - type: send_tx
        chain: ethereum
        to: "0xB92..."
        data: "0x..."

Scenarios use a registry-based step dispatcher â€” each step type (log, send_tx, impersonate, forge_script, assert_balance, etc.) is registered via a decorator. New step types can be added without modifying the engine.

docs.title: Attack Plugin
docs.text:
An attack plugin is a Python class that subclasses AttackPlugin and implements:

class MyExploit(AttackPlugin):
    def execute(self, context, phase=None):
        # Forge calldata, send transactions, check results
        return AttackResult(success=True, ...)

    def verify(self, context):
        # Check on-chain state matches expectations
        return VerificationResult(passed=True, ...)

The plugin receives an ExperimentContext through which it accesses chain state (context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum") â†’ JsonRpcClient), logs events, and reads configuration.

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Every attack plugin must include an evidence.yaml that documents:

  • Incident metadata â€” name, date, affected bridge, damage
  • Sources â€” advisories, postmortems, transaction explorers
  • Historical scope â€” chains, fork blocks, exploit transactions, affected contracts
  • Reproduction scope â€” fidelity level (L0â€“L4), modeled components, excluded components
  • Acceptance criteria â€” required assertions that must pass

Evidence is validated against live chain state before every run â€” fork blocks are checked via eth_getBlockByNumber, contract addresses via eth_getCode.

docs.title: Fidelity Levels
docs.text:
The L0â€“L4 fidelity system is enforced at runtime, not just documented. Each level has specific requirements:

Level Name Requirements Runtime Checks L0 Narrative Description None L1 Conceptual Plugin returns a result Result exists L2 Fork-State Chains + contracts verified Trace artifacts exist, chains match topology L3 Exact Replay Exploit transactions declared Transaction actions in run.jsonl L4 Multi-Chain 2+ chains, bridge, excluded_components Cross-chain artifacts, bridge plugin loaded

docs.title: Instrumentation Proxy
docs.text:
The instrumentation proxy is a transparent HTTP proxy that sits between the attack plugin and Anvil:

Attack Plugin â†’ Proxy (port random) â†’ Anvil (port 8545)

After every eth_sendTransaction, the proxy automatically:

  1. Polls eth_getTransactionReceipt to get the receipt (gas used, logs)
  2. Calls debug_traceTransaction with prestateTracer in diffMode to get storage diffs and balance changes
  3. Emits all data to JSONL artifact streams

The attack plugin never knows it's being instrumented. ChainManager.runtime_for() silently returns the proxy URL instead of the direct Anvil URL.

docs.title: Phase 1: Setup
docs.text:
CLI â†’ ExperimentRunner â†’ ScenarioEngine â†’ ChainManager
                                         â†˜ PluginRegistry
  1. Load config â€” testbed.yaml + testbed.local.yaml â†’ Pydantic-validated TestbedConfig
  2. Load experiment YAML â€” topology, scenario, attack plugin specified
  3. Auto-merge fork blocks â€” if evidence.yaml specifies a different fork block, update config automatically
  4. Verify Foundry version â€” check forge --version against required_version
  5. Run publication gates â€” if in publication mode, check evidence completeness, baseline existence, git provenance
  6. Execute scenario â€” chain forking starts here. The scenario engine executes YAML steps (log, impersonate, send_tx, forge_script, etc.)

docs.title: Phase 2: Instrumentation
docs.text:
ExperimentRunner â†’ ChainManager.start_instrumentation()
                 â†’ RpcProxy (per chain)
                     â†’ HTTPServer on random port
                     â†’ ThreadPoolExecutor (max 10 workers)

Each chain gets its own proxy instance with its own handler class (via _make_handler() closure). The proxy:

  • Forwards all JSON-RPC requests to Anvil
  • Detects eth_sendTransaction / eth_sendRawTransaction
  • Schedules background trace tasks via ThreadPoolExecutor

docs.title: Phase 3: Attack Execution
docs.text:
ExperimentRunner â†’ PluginRegistry.load("attack", name)
                 â†’ AttackPlugin.run(context)
                     â†’ AttackPlugin.execute(context)
                         â†’ eth_sendTransaction (via proxy URL)
                         â†’ Proxy captures: gas, events, storage diffs, balance diffs

The attack plugin interacts with the chain through context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url, which points to the proxy, not directly to Anvil. Every transaction is automatically instrumented.

docs.title: Phase 4: Teardown
docs.text:
ExperimentRunner â†’ ChainManager.stop_instrumentation()
                 â†’ Proxy.drain()     â€” wait for pending traces
                 â†’ Proxy.stop()      â€” shutdown HTTP server
                 â†’ LogManager.write_report()
                 â†’ LogManager.write_integrity()
                 â†’ FidelityEnforcer.check()
                 â†’ write run_manifest.json

The shutdown sequence is critical: drain() must be called before stop() and before log handles close. This ensures all async trace tasks complete before artifacts are finalized.

docs.title: Configuration Hierarchy
docs.text:
.env                              â† RPC URLs, secrets
  â†“ load_dotenv()
testbed.yaml                      â† Committed to git (base config)
  â†“
testbed.local.yaml                â† Gitignored (per-user overrides)
  â†“ deep_merge()
â†“ expand_env()
â†“ Pydantic model_validate()
â†’ TestbedConfig

docs.title: Key Config Models
docs.text:
class TestbedConfig:
    workspace: str
    active_topology: str
    foundry: FoundryConfig
    publication: PublicationConfig
    chains: dict[str, ChainConfig]     # Per-chain configuration
    rpc_providers: dict[str, list]     # Per-chain RPC failover list
    fork_cache: ForkCacheConfig
    logging: LoggingConfig
    cleanup: CleanupConfig
    rpc_budget: RpcBudgetConfig
    notifications: NotificationConfig

class ChainConfig:
    rpc_url: str
    fork_block: int | None             # None = blank fork (no remote)
    chain_id: int
    port: int
    host: str
    determinism: DeterminismConfig

class DeterminismConfig:
    gas_price: int = 0
    block_base_fee: int = 1_000_000_000
    timestamp: int | Literal["fork_block"] = "fork_block"
    auto_mine: bool = True
    order: str = "fifo"

docs.title: Built-In Validations
docs.text:
• Port uniqueness â€” no two chains can share a port
  • Chain ID uniqueness â€” no two chains can share a chain_id
  • Port range â€” must be 1â€“65535
  • Matrix detection â€” load_experiment() rejects matrix files with a helpful error message

docs.title: CLI-First Architecture
docs.text: Every action is available via crucible <command>. The Streamlit dashboard is a separate read-only viewer. This makes experiments scriptable, Docker-friendly, and automatable.

docs.title: Topology-Driven Composition
docs.text: Topology YAML files declare which chains to fork. The same attack can run on different topologies. Adding a new chain config doesn't affect existing experiments.

docs.title: Transparent Instrumentation
docs.text: The proxy intercepts all RPC traffic. Researchers never add logging code. Any attack method (Python, Forge, cast send) produces complete artifacts.

docs.title: Snapshot-Driven Reset
docs.text: Baselines are captured once after setup. Soft resets use anvil_loadState â€” no chain restart needed. This enables fast iteration during development.

docs.title: Determinism by Default
docs.text: Deterministic flags are opt-out, not opt-in. If a researcher does nothing, their experiments are deterministic.

docs.title: Plugins for Capabilities
docs.text: New functionality arrives as plugins, not core rewrites: - attack â€” exploit reproductions - bridge â€” bridge protocol simulation - agent â€” LLM-driven autonomous agents

docs.title: Fidelity Levels Are Enforced
docs.text: The L0â€“L4 fidelity system isn't just documentation â€” publication gates and runtime enforcers check requirements programmatically.

docs.title: Separation of Scenario and Experiment
docs.text: Scenarios define environments (chain state, setup). Experiments pair a scenario + attack plugin for formal runs. The same scenario can be used with different attacks, and the same attack can run against different scenarios.

docs.title: Three Plugin Types
docs.text: Type Base Class Purpose Lifecycle Attack AttackPlugin Exploit reproduction prepare() â†’ execute() â†’ verify() â†’ rollback() â†’ cleanup() Bridge BridgePlugin Cross-chain simulation deploy() â†’ configure() â†’ connect() â†’ send_message() â†’ reset() â†’ destroy() Agent AgentPlugin LLM-driven agents prepare() â†’ observe() â†’ think() â†’ respond() (loop) â†’ verify()

docs.title: Discovery
docs.text:
Plugins are discovered from attacks/, bridges/, and plugins/ directories. Each plugin must include a plugin.yaml manifest:

name: nomad_replica_fraud
version: 1.0.0
type: attack
entry: nomad_replica_fraud.py
class: NomadReplicaFraud

Attack plugins must also include evidence.yaml. The registry validates evidence structure during discovery.

docs.title: ExperimentContext
docs.text:
Every plugin method receives an ExperimentContext through which it accesses:

  • Chain state â€” context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum") â†’ JsonRpcClient
  • Logging â€” context.log.step("message"), context.log.event(...)
  • Configuration â€” context.config.chains["ethereum"].fork_block
  • Bridges â€” context.bridge_plugin.send_message(...) (if bridge plugin deployed)

docs.title: Anvil Subprocess Lifecycle
docs.text:
Configured â†’ Starting (Popen) â†’ WaitingReady (poll eth_blockNumber)
â†’ Running â†’ Instrumented (proxy attached)
â†’ Draining (flush traces) â†’ Running â†’ Stopped

docs.title: Blank Fork Mode
docs.text:
When fork_block: null, Anvil starts without --fork-url as a standalone local EVM. This enables:

  • Fully offline operation â€” no RPC provider needed
  • Instant startup â€” <1 second instead of 30sâ€“5min
  • Full determinism â€” no archive state variability
  • CI/hermetic testing â€” zero external dependencies

The instrumentation proxy works identically in both modes â€” no code changes needed.

docs.title: 3-Tier Reset
docs.text: Tier Method Speed Use Case Soft anvil_loadState snapshot <5s Matrix runs, parameter sweeps Medium Kill â†’ restart â†’ restore <30s After process corruption Hard Re-fork from archive RPC <5min After cache corruption, topology change

docs.title: 7 JSONL Streams
docs.text: Stream Content Source run.jsonl Experiment lifecycle events LogManager.step() events.jsonl Contract events (Transfer, Approval, etc.) Proxy via LogEmitter.event() storage_diffs.jsonl Storage slot changes (contract, slot, before, after) Proxy via LogEmitter.storage_diff() gas_profile.jsonl Gas used per transaction Proxy via LogEmitter.gas() balance_diffs.jsonl ETH/token balance changes Proxy via LogEmitter.balance_diff() cross_chain.jsonl Bridge message correlation IDs LogEmitter.cross_chain() agent_reasoning.jsonl LLM agent reasoning traces LLMAgentPlugin._log_reasoning_step()

docs.title: Log Directory Structure
docs.text:
logs/
  nomad_replay/
    DS-2026-00042/                    â† Run directory
      run.jsonl                       â† Lifecycle events
      run_manifest.json               â† Provenance + outcome
      log_integrity.json              â† SHA-256 hashes
      report.md                       â† Human-readable summary
      chain_ethereum/                 â† Per-chain artifacts
        events.jsonl
        gas_profile.jsonl
        storage_diffs.jsonl
        balance_diffs.jsonl
      chain_bsc/
        ...
      bridge/
        cross_chain.jsonl

docs.title: Integrity Verification
docs.text: Every run produces a log_integrity.json with SHA-256 hashes of all artifact files, and an artifact_bundle_manifest.json with hashes of all run files. These enable crucible verify <run-id> to detect tampering or data corruption.

docs.title: Fidelity Enforcement
docs.text:
Fidelity enforcement runs after the attack executes but before the run manifest is written. This means the manifest reflects the fidelity check outcome.

The FidelityEnforcer runs cumulative checks:

  1. L0 â€” Always passes (narrative only)
  2. L1 â€” Verifies the plugin returned a result
  3. L2 â€” Verifies chains match topology, trace artifacts exist, no auto-trace failures
  4. L3 â€” Verifies exploit transactions declared, transaction actions in run.jsonl
  5. L4 â€” Verifies 2+ chains, bridge plugin loaded, excluded_components declared

docs.title: Two Operational Modes
docs.text: Mode Command When to Use Single-container docker compose run orchestrator ... Most experiments â€” Anvil runs inside orchestrator Daemon + orchestrator docker compose up -d anvil-ethereum && docker compose run orchestrator ... Multi-session workflows

docs.title: Container Composition
docs.text:
orchestrator â€” Python 3.12 + Foundry + uv | Entrypoint: uv run crucible
dashboard    â€” Streamlit UI | Port 8501
anvil-ethereum â€” Foundry Anvil daemon | Port 8545
anvil-polygon  â€” Foundry Anvil daemon | Port 8546
anvil-bsc      â€” Foundry Anvil daemon | Port 8547
anvil-harmony  â€” Foundry Anvil daemon | Port 8548

Named volumes (states_data, logs_data) persist chain state and experiment artifacts across container restarts.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers Architecture Deep Dive Complete component map, all 39 modules Design Decisions Key tradeoffs and rationale Instrumentation Architecture RPC proxy design in detail Plugin Development Guide Writing attack, bridge, and agent plugins (in website/) Fidelity Guide L0â€“L4 fidelity levels and enforcement Reproducibility Guide Provenance, determinism, bug log

docs.title: Testbed Scope Guide — What Can Be Tested
docs.text: Determining whether an attack is reproducible on Crucible. Covers EVM forkability, archive RPC requirements, fidelity level gating, and practical edge cases learned from real validation sessions.

docs.title: 1. Quick Decision Tree
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is the attack on an<br>EVM-compatible chain?"}

    No1["❌ Cannot be reproduced.<br>Classify as L1 (narrative only).<br><i>Examples: Solana, Harmony, Cosmos, MPC</i>"]

    Q2{"Can the chain be forked<br>at the exploit block?"}

    No2["No (archive doesn't serve state)<br>Try a different RPC provider (paid).<br>Result: ❌ Limited to L1/L2 on available chains."]

    Q3{"What fidelity level is achievable?"}

    L1["L1 (narrative)<br>No runtime checks needed"]
    L2["L2 (fork-state)<br>Chains + contracts verified"]
