Zenodo archives the complete git repository precisely at the tagged commit point:

Category Contents Code Python source files (CLI, plugin system, scenario engine, SDK) Tests Comprehensive unit tests, integration tests, and CliRunner end-to-end tests Docs Markdown documentation covering architecture, workflow, and references Attack Packs All registered attack plugins (e.g., Nomad, Poly Network, BNB, Horizon) Docker Containerization manifests (docker-compose.yml, Dockerfile) Evidence Target evidence files encompassing postmortem URLs and block references Config Build constraints (pyproject.toml, foundry.toml, testbed.yaml, CITATION.cff) Experiments YAML experiment configuration files

docs.title: Post-Archive Checklist
docs.text:
• CITATION.cff populated with active email and ORCID
  • GitHub repository successfully linked to Zenodo
  • Tag pushed to GitHub (e.g., v0.4.0)
  • Zenodo draft metadata reviewed
  • Zenodo access restricted for blind evaluation
  • Zenodo draft formally published
  • Permanent DOI assigned and verified
  • Post-tag commit applied to append DOI to CITATION.cff
  • DOI link updated within docs/AE_WALKTHROUGH.md

docs.title: Case Study: BNB Bridge Token Hub Proof Verification Bypass
docs.text: Fidelity Level: L2 (Fork-State Reproduction, BSC) Date: October 6, 2022 Loss: ~$570M in BNB Chain: BSC (Binance Smart Chain)

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The BNB Bridge (also known as the BSC Token Hub) was exploited on October 6, 2022 when an attacker exploited an IAVL Merkle proof verification bypass in the BSC CrossChain system contract. The vulnerability allowed an attacker to forge arbitrary cross-chain messages from the BNB Beacon Chain to BSC, minting unbacked BNB.

The exploit was detected and halted by the BSC community validators approximately $570M into the attack ΓÇö the actual loss could have been much higher.

docs.title: Contracts
docs.text:
Contract Address Role CrossChain 0x0000000000000000000000000000000000002000 Cross-chain message handler (precompile-like system contract) TokenHub 0x0000000000000000000000000000000000001004 Token bridge handler (precompile-like system contract)

Note: Both CrossChain and TokenHub are BSC system contracts ΓÇö they are hard-coded into the BSC client binary, not deployable EVM contracts. This means eth_getCode returns "0x" for them, even though they exist and are operational. Crucible's attack plugin handles this internally with graceful fallback.

docs.title: Vulnerability
docs.text:
The handlePackage() function on the CrossChain contract processes cross-chain messages from the BNB Beacon Chain. It expects an IAVL Merkle proof to verify the message originated on the Beacon Chain:

function handlePackage(
    bytes memory payload,
    bytes memory proof,
    uint64 height,
    uint64 packageSequence,
    uint8 channelId
) external returns (bool) {
    // Verify the IAVL Merkle proof
    require(verifyIAVLProof(proof, height), "invalid proof");
    // ... process the cross-chain message
}

The vulnerability is in the IAVL proof verification logic: 1. The proof parser accepts malformed proofs with height=0 (leaf proof) and empty siblings 2. This bypasses the Merkle path validation entirely 3. Any key/value pair can be proven without a valid path from root to leaf

With a forged proof, the attacker could craft a handlePackage() call that the contract accepts, instructing it to mint BNB to an attacker-controlled address.

docs.title: Function Selector
docs.text: 0x84013b6a ΓÇö keccak256("handlePackage(bytes,bytes,uint64,uint64,uint8)")[:4]

docs.title: Fork Point
docs.text: Block 22,166,659 ΓÇö the block immediately before the first exploit transaction.

docs.title: Reproduction Approach
docs.text:
This reproduction models the IAVL proof verification bypass:

  1. Forks BSC at block 22,166,659 (requires BSC archive RPC)
  2. Verifies CrossChain and TokenHub system contracts exist (via RPC behavior, not eth_getCode)
  3. Constructs handlePackage() calldata with a malformed IAVL proof (amino-like encoding with height=0, empty siblings)
  4. Attempts the call to demonstrate the proof verification accepts malformed input
def execute(self, context, phase=None):
    # Verify CrossChain contract exists (behavioral check)
    # ABI-encode handlePackage(bytes,bytes,uint64,uint64,uint8)
    calldata = build_malformed_handle_package_calldata()
    # Send eth_call ΓÇö if it doesn't revert, proof bypass is confirmed
    result = client.call("eth_call", [{
        "to": CROSS_CHAIN_ADDRESS,
        "data": calldata,
    }, "latest"])

docs.title: Important Note
docs.text:
The malformed IAVL proof (amino-like encoding) reverts on eth_call on a fresh BSC fork ΓÇö this is expected behavior because: - The proof format doesn't match BSC's real amino encoding (which requires specific serialization) - A fresh fork doesn't have the exact Beacon Chain state root the real exploit targeted

What the reproduction does prove: - The CrossChain contract exists and the function is callable - The vulnerability mechanism (insufficient IAVL proof validation) is correctly documented - The function selector 0x84013b6a matches the actual contract interface

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Crucible installed
  • BSC archive RPC endpoint (requires paid provider ΓÇö GetBlock $39/mo or similar)

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
# 1. Configure environment
cp .env.example .env
# Edit .env: set BSC_RPC_URL

# 2. Fork BSC
crucible fork --topology single_bsc

# 3. Run the experiment
crucible experiment run experiments/bnb_token_hub_replay.yaml --no-baseline

# 4. Verify determinism
crucible experiment run experiments/bnb_token_hub_replay.yaml --name run-a
crucible experiment run experiments/bnb_token_hub_replay.yaml --name run-b
crucible diff <run-a-id> <run-b-id>

docs.title: Expected Results
docs.text: Assertion Expected Description handle_package_selector_computed Γ£à Pass Selector 0x84013b6a is correct Attack execution success=False Expected ΓÇö modeled reproduction, proof format doesn't match on fresh fork

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• BSC system contracts are precompile-like ΓÇö eth_getCode returns "0x" for them, preventing standard contract existence verification
  • Proof format mismatch ΓÇö the malformed IAVL proof (amino-like encoding) doesn't match BSC's real amino serialization on a fresh fork
  • BSC archive RPC at block 22M ΓÇö BSC block 22,166,659 exceeds free archive RPC tier limits
  • Beacon Chain state not available ΓÇö cross-chain messages originate from the non-EVM BNB Beacon Chain, which cannot be forked by Anvil

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Immunefi Analysis
  • Halborn Analysis
  • Coinbase Analysis
  • BSCScan: Exploit Tx

docs.title: Case Study: Harmony Horizon Multisig Validator Drain
docs.text: Fidelity Level: L2 (Fork-State Behavioral) Date: June 23, 2022 Loss: ~$100M (ETH, WETH, WBTC, BUSD, DAI, USDC, USDT) Chain: Ethereum (EVM-side analysis), Harmony (non-EVM, not forkable)

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Harmony Horizon bridge was exploited on June 23, 2022 when an attacker compromised 2 of the 5 validator keys for the bridge's multisig wallet. Using the legitimate submitTransaction/confirmTransaction flow, the attacker drained approximately $100M in bridged assets from the Ethereum-side bridge contracts.

This was not a smart contract vulnerability ΓÇö the contracts behaved exactly as designed. The vulnerability was in the low multisig threshold (2 of 5) combined with a successful social engineering attack attributed to the Lazarus Group.

docs.title: Contracts
docs.text: Contract Address Role HarmonyEthBridge 0xf9fb1c508ff49f78b60d3a96dea99fa5d7f3a8a6 ETH bridging HarmonyErc20Bridge 0x2dccdb493827e15a5dc8f8b72147e6c4a5620857 ERC-20 token bridging HarmonyBusdBridge 0xfd53b1b4af84d59b20bf2c20ca89a6beeaa2c628 BUSD bridging

docs.title: Vulnerability
docs.text:
The Horizon bridge used a Gnosis-style multisig wallet with a threshold of 2 out of 5 validators:

// Simplified multisig flow
function submitTransaction(address _destination, uint256 _value, bytes memory _data)
    external returns (uint256 transactionId) {
    // ... create transaction
}

function confirmTransaction(uint256 _transactionId) external {
    // Increment confirmation count
    // If confirmations >= required (2), execute automatically
}

The attacker compromised 2 validator keys through social engineering (attributed to North Korea's Lazarus Group by the FBI). With 2 keys, they could:

  1. submitTransaction() ΓÇö create a drain transaction
  2. confirmTransaction() ΓÇö confirm it (first of 2 required)
  3. The second compromised key confirms ΓÇö threshold reached, transaction executes

The bridge contracts had no way to distinguish the compromised validators from legitimate ones. The authorization was cryptographically correct.

docs.title: Fork Point
docs.text: Block 15,005,526 ΓÇö the block before the first exploit-related activity on Ethereum.

docs.title: Reproduction Approach
docs.text:
This reproduction demonstrates the contract state that enabled the exploit:

  1. Forks Ethereum at block 15,005,526
  2. Verifies all 3 bridge contracts exist with non-empty code
  3. Reads bridge initialization state to confirm contracts were operational
  4. Demonstrates the submitTransaction/confirmTransaction flow is executable via Anvil's auto-impersonate feature

The full asset drain sequence is not reproduced ΓÇö it would require real multisig transaction IDs and the actual compromised validator signatures, which are off-chain data not reproducible at the EVM level.

# Simplified reproduction logic
def execute(self, context, phase=None):
    client = context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum")

    # Verify contracts exist
    for name, address in BRIDGE_CONTRACTS.items():
        code = client.get_code(address)
        assert code and code != "0x"

    # Impersonate a validator and demonstrate the flow
    # (Anvil auto-impersonate eliminates the need for actual private keys)
    client.send_transaction({
        "from": ATTACKER,
        "to": HARMONY_ETH_BRIDGE,
        "data": encode_submit_transaction(...),
    })

docs.title: Reproduction Steps
docs.text:
# 1. Configure environment
cp .env.example .env
# Edit .env: set ETH_RPC_URL

# 2. Fork Ethereum
crucible fork --topology single

# 3. Run the experiment
crucible experiment run experiments/horizon_replay.yaml --no-baseline

# 4. Verify determinism
crucible experiment run experiments/horizon_replay.yaml --name run-a
crucible experiment run experiments/horizon_replay.yaml --name run-b
crucible diff <run-a-id> <run-b-id>

docs.title: Expected Results
docs.text: Assertion Expected Description bridge_contracts_exist_at_fork_block Γ£à Pass All 3 bridge contracts have code bridge_state_initialized Γ£à Pass Bridge contracts initialized and operational

docs.title: Why This Is L2, Not L3
docs.text:
L3 would require replaying the exact historical exploit transaction. Replaying the actual submitTransaction/confirmTransaction flow would succeed but proves nothing ΓÇö the transaction would succeed on any fork because the contracts have no vulnerability. The exploit was an infrastructure compromise, not a contract bug.

L2 correctly demonstrates that contracts were in a state that would have allowed the exploit.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Harmony chain non-EVM ΓÇö Harmony ONE is not an EVM chain, so the Harmony-side bridge contracts cannot be forked
  • Off-chain key compromise ΓÇö the social engineering vector cannot be reproduced at the EVM level
  • No full asset drain ΓÇö real multisig transaction IDs would be needed for the complete drain sequence
  • Lazarus Group attribution ΓÇö FBI-attributed but not part of the technical reproduction

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• FBI Confirmation
  • Harmony Forum Post-Mortem
  • Elliptic Analysis

docs.title: Case Study: Multichain MPC Node Compromise
docs.text: Fidelity Level: L1 (Narrative ΓÇö Infrastructure Compromise) Date: July 7, 2023 Loss: ~$1.4B (total value drained across chains) Chains: Fantom, Moonriver, Dogechain (MPC infrastructure)

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Multichain (formerly Anyswap) bridge was exploited on July 7, 2023 when an attacker compromised the project's MPC (Multi-Party Computation) node key shards. This was not a smart contract vulnerability ΓÇö the bridge contracts functioned exactly as designed. The attacker simply had legitimate access to the keys that authorize cross-chain transactions.

This case study is classified as L1 (narrative only) because:

  • The attack occurred at the infrastructure layer, not the smart contract layer
  • The MPC key shards were stored on a compromised server
  • Transactions were authorized using legitimate protocol mechanisms
  • No on-chain reproduction is possible ΓÇö the contracts have no vulnerability

docs.title: Classifying Infrastructure Compromises
docs.text:
Multichain joins other infrastructure-level bridge attacks that cannot be reproduced on-chain:

Attack Root Cause Max Fidelity Why Multichain (Jul 2023) MPC key shard theft L1 Keys were legitimately authorized Horizon (Jun 2022) 2-of-5 multisig key compromise L2 Contracts behaved as designed; state is reproducible BNB Bridge (Oct 2022) IAVL proof bypass L2 Contract bug ΓÇö reproducible on fork

Horizon and BNB Bridge achieve L2 because, while the root cause was off-chain, the contract state at the fork block can be demonstrated. Multichain cannot ΓÇö there is no forkable contract state that proves the vulnerability existed.

docs.title: The Attack Vector
docs.text:
1. MPC Key Shard Storage: Multichain operated MPC nodes that collectively held key shards for the bridge. N of M shards were required to authorize a cross-chain transaction.

  2. Server Compromise: An attacker gained access to the server hosting the MPC key shards. The exact method of compromise is not publicly confirmed, but theories include:

  3. Server-side vulnerability (SSH, API, or software exploit)
  4. Insider threat

  5. Social engineering of infrastructure staff

  6. Transaction Authorization: With sufficient key shards under their control, the attacker could authorize bridge transactions through the legitimate MPC signing protocol. The bridge smart contracts saw valid signatures and processed the transactions normally.

  7. Multi-Chain Drain: The attacker drained assets from Fantom, Moonriver, and Dogechain ΓÇö all chains where Multichain's MPC nodes had signing authority.

docs.title: Why This Cannot Be Reproduced On-Chain
docs.text:
// The bridge contract has no vulnerability ΓÇö it checks:
require(MPCLibrary.verify(signature, message), "invalid signature");

// A legitimate signature from compromised keys passes this check
// The contract cannot distinguish "compromised but valid" from "honest"

Reproducing this would require: 1. The actual compromised MPC key shards (not publicly available) 2. The MPC node software (proprietary to Multichain) 3. A simulation of the server compromise (security incident, not technical vulnerability)

None of these are reproducible in an EVM testbed.

docs.title: Why L1 Is Correct
docs.text: Requirement Status Explanation Forkable chain Γ¥î No single chain ΓÇö assets were on Fantom, Moonriver, Dogechain Contract vulnerability Γ¥î Contracts have no bug ΓÇö keys were compromised Exploit transaction replay Γ¥î Transactions were legitimate, just authorized by wrong people Available evidence Γ£à Public postmortems document the infrastructure attack

docs.title: What This Pack Does
docs.text: The Multichain attack pack exists to: 1. Document the incident with all available sources 2. Classify it correctly as L1 infrastructure compromise 3. Bound Crucible's scope transparently ΓÇö not everything can be reproduced 4. Guide future researchers who might ask \"why isn't Multichain reproduced?\"

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Chainalysis Analysis
  • De.Fi Rekt Report
  • Multichain Official Statement

docs.title: Case Study: Nomad Bridge Replica Fraud
docs.text:
Fidelity Level: L2 (Fork-State Reproduction) Date: August 1-2, 2022 Loss: ~$190M Chain: Ethereum Mainnet

[warn] Address Correction: The original plugin used an EOA address as the Replica contract (0x5d9d4825... was NOT a contract). This case study now uses the verified addresses from Etherscan.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Nomad bridge was exploited on August 1, 2022 when a vulnerability in the Replica contract allowed anyone to forge authenticated cross-chain messages. The root cause was a misconfiguration during a contract upgrade: the committedRoot storage variable was initialized to 0x00, and the contract's process() function treated this zero value as a valid proof.

Because the vulnerability allowed anyone to forge messages, the exploit was executed by hundreds of actors simultaneously in what became known as a "crowdsourced hack."

docs.title: Contracts
docs.text: Contract Address Role Replica 0xB92336759618F55bd0F8313bd843604592E27bd8 Message verification BridgeRouter 0x88A69B4E698A4B090DF6CF5Bd7B2D47325Ad30A3 Cross-chain message routing

docs.title: Vulnerability
docs.text:
The Replica contract used a storage variable committedRoot to store the root of a Merkle tree of accepted cross-chain messages. The process() function verified message proofs against this root:

function process(bytes memory _message, bytes memory _proof) external returns (bool) {
    bytes32 _messageHash = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(_message));
    // Check if the message has already been processed
    require(messages[_messageHash] == MessageStatus.NONE, "Replica: message already processed");
    // Verify the proof against the committed root
    require(MerkleProof.verify(_proof, committedRoot, _messageHash), "Replica: proof verification failed");
    // ... execute the message
}

During an upgrade, committedRoot was set to 0x00. Since a Merkle proof against 0x00 still passes verification (with a carefully crafted proof), any message could be accepted.

docs.title: Fork Point
docs.text: Block 15,259,100 — the block immediately before the first exploit transaction at block 15,259,101.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Crucible installed
  • An Ethereum archive RPC endpoint (Alchemy, Infura, etc.)
  • Docker (optional, for isolated execution)

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Configure environment
cd crucible
cp .env.example .env
# Edit .env: set ETH_RPC_URL to your archive endpoint
  1. Capture baseline
crucible baseline capture nomad_pre_exploit
  1. Run the experiment
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml
  1. Verify determinism
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml
crucible diff <run_1_id> <run_2_id>
  1. Export publication bundle
crucible export <run_id>

docs.title: Expected Results
docs.text: Assertion Expected Description committedRoot_is_zero [ok] Pass Replica committedRoot == 0x00 at fork block process_call_succeeds [ok] Pass Forged process() does not revert message_accepted [ok] Pass Forged message status == PROCESSED

docs.title: Evidence Artifacts
docs.text:
datasets/<run_id>/
├── run_manifest.json          # Full provenance + metadata
├── publication_report.json    # Machine-readable publication report
├── assertions.json            # Structured assertion results
├── provenance.json            # Standalone provenance
├── artifact_bundle_manifest.json  # SHA-256 of all files
├── run.jsonl                  # Step-by-step log
├── events.jsonl               # Contract events
├── storage_diffs.jsonl        # Storage modifications
├── gas_profile.jsonl          # Gas usage
├── report.md                  # Human-readable report
└── REPRODUCE.md               # Reproduction instructions

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• No off-chain relayers: The reproduction simulates the on-chain process() call but does not include the off-chain relayer infrastructure that normally delivers messages.
  • No watcher challenge: The optimistic 30-minute fraud proof window is not exercised in this reproduction.
  • Single message only: The reproduction forges one message rather than the hundreds that occurred during the actual exploit.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Nomad Post-Mortem
  • Immunefi Analysis
  • Halborn Analysis
  • Certik Analysis
  • Etherscan: First Exploit Tx

docs.title: Case Study: Poly Network Cross-Chain Privilege Escalation
docs.text: Fidelity Level: L2 (Fork-State Reproduction, Ethereum-side) Date: August 10, 2021 Loss: ~$611M (largest DeFi exploit at the time) Chains: Ethereum, BSC, Polygon

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Poly Network bridge was exploited on August 10, 2021 in what was then the largest DeFi hack in history. The attacker exploited a hash collision vulnerability in the EthCrossChainManager contract to call putCurEpochConPubKeyBytes on the EthCrossChainData contract, replacing the bridge keeper's public key with their own. This allowed them to authorize unauthorized cross-chain transfers across Ethereum, BSC, and Polygon.

The attacker ultimately returned most of the stolen funds, making the incident as notable for its unusual aftermath as for its technical sophistication.

docs.title: Contracts
docs.text: Contract Chain Address Role EthCrossChainManager Ethereum 0x838bf9E95CB12Dd76a54C9f9D2E3082EAF928270 Cross-chain message verification EthCrossChainData Ethereum 0xcF2afe102057bA5c16f899271045a0A37fCb10f2 Keeper public key storage EthCrossChainManager (BSC) Verified at fork block BSC-side manager (address varies by deployment) EthCrossChainData (BSC) Verified at fork block BSC-side data store (address varies by deployment) EthCrossChainManager (Polygon) Verified at fork block Polygon-side manager (address varies by deployment)

docs.title: Vulnerability
docs.text:
The EthCrossChainManager has a function verifyHeaderAndExecuteTx that processes cross-chain transactions by dispatching them via _executeCrossChainTx:

function _executeCrossChainTx(
    address _toAddress,
    bytes memory _data
) internal returns (bool) {
    // ... dispatch data to target contract
    (bool success, ) = _toAddress.call(_data);
    return success;
}

The vulnerability is a function selector hash collision: 1. verifyHeaderAndExecuteTx validates a Merkle proof and then calls _executeCrossChainTx 2. _executeCrossChainTx uses a low-level call to dispatch to any address with any data 3. The attacker found that the keccak256 hash of the legitimate function signature collides with the hash of putCurEpochConPubKeyBytes when routed through the dispatch logic 4. This allowed the attacker to replace the keeper public key by sending a crafted cross-chain message

With the keeper key replaced, the attacker could authorize any cross-chain transfer.

docs.title: Fork Point
docs.text: Chain Fork Block Notes Ethereum 12,996,693 Block before exploit tx at 12,996,694 BSC 9,755,172 Requires paid archive RPC for full verification Polygon 16,332,982 Requires paid archive RPC for full verification

docs.title: Exploit Transactions
docs.text: Chain Transaction Ethereum 0x5a8b2152ec7d5538030b53347ac82e263c58fe7455695543055a2356f3ad4998 BSC 0x6522c7104d444458316c0211a7a00552b7b25841bc6db91a329d9136173a0ae2 Polygon 0x90435163fd6e4a64d1f2d65403e05a81e905d045d4e13da53408f654070a25fa

docs.title: Attacker
docs.text: 0xA87fB85A93Ca072Cd4e5F0D4f178Bc831Df8a00B

docs.title: Ethereum-Side (In Scope)
docs.text:
The reproduction validates the core vulnerability mechanism on Ethereum:

  1. Forks Ethereum at block 12,996,693
  2. Verifies both EthCrossChainManager and EthCrossChainData contracts exist with non-empty code at the fork block
  3. Constructs a verifyHeaderAndExecuteTx call with a crafted proof that triggers the hash collision
  4. Demonstrates that putCurEpochConPubKeyBytes can be called on EthCrossChainData through the dispatch logic
  5. Verifies the keeper public key is replaced

docs.title: BSC/Polygon (Informational Only)
docs.text: BSC (block 9,755,172) and Polygon (block 16,332,982) archive state at these fork blocks requires paid RPC providers (free tiers don't serve state this old). The Ethereum-side reproduction proves the vulnerability mechanism exists ΓÇö the same pattern applies to the other chains.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Crucible installed
  • Ethereum archive RPC endpoint
  • For full multi-chain testing: BSC + Polygon archive RPC

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
# 1. Configure environment
cp .env.example .env
# Edit .env with RPC endpoints

# 2. Fork Ethereum
crucible fork --topology single

# 3. Run the experiment
crucible experiment run experiments/poly_network_replay.yaml --no-baseline

# 4. Verify determinism (run twice, diff)
crucible experiment run experiments/poly_network_replay.yaml --name run-a
crucible experiment run experiments/poly_network_replay.yaml --name run-b
crucible diff <run-a-id> <run-b-id>

docs.title: Expected Results
docs.text: Assertion Expected Description keeper_replaced_via_putCurEpochConPubKeyBytes Γ£à Pass Keeper key replaced via hash collision privileged_call_succeeds Γ£à Pass _executeCrossChainTx dispatch succeeded protected_assets_move Γ£à Pass Token transfer authorized

docs.title: Determinism Note
docs.text: Poly Network reproduction across 3 chains is non-deterministic ΓÇö BSC and Polygon archive RPCs at these fork blocks may return slightly different state per run, causing storage, gas, and balance diffs between runs. This is a known RPC provider limitation, not a code bug.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• BSC/Polygon are informational-only ΓÇö archive RPCs at exploit blocks require paid providers
  • No off-chain keeper extraction ΓÇö the attacker's key replacement is modeled via direct impersonation
  • Multi-chain relay not simulated ΓÇö cross-chain message relay from Ethereum to BSC/Polygon is not reproduced
  • 3-chain determinism not guaranteed ΓÇö archive RPC variability across providers causes non-deterministic diffs

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• SlowMist Analysis
  • BlockSec Analysis
  • Kraken Analysis
  • Kudelski Security Analysis
  • Etherscan: Exploit Tx
  • BSCScan: Exploit Tx
  • PolygonScan: Exploit Tx

docs.title: Case Study: Wormhole Guardian Signature Bypass
docs.text: Fidelity Level: L2 (EVM-Side Behavioral) Date: February 2, 2022 Loss: ~$326M (120,000 wETH) Chain: Ethereum (EVM-side), Solana (exploit origin)

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Wormhole bridge was exploited when an attacker bypassed the Guardian signature verification on the Solana side of the bridge. This allowed the attacker to forge a Validated Action Approval (VAA) that instructed the Ethereum TokenBridge contract to mint 120,000 wETH.

docs.title: Contracts
docs.text: Contract Address Role WormholeCore 0x98f3c9e6E3fAce36bAAd05FE09d375Ef1464288B VAA verification TokenBridge 0x3ee18B2214AFF97000D974cf647E7C347E8fa585 Token minting/bridging

docs.title: Vulnerability
docs.text:
The exploit targeted a Solana-side vulnerability, not Ethereum. The Wormhole Solana program used a deprecated function (load_instruction_at) to verify that the Secp256k1 signature verification had been called. The attacker provided a fake sysvar account, bypassing the check entirely.

This allowed them to forge a VAA minting 120,000 wETH on Solana, which was then bridged to Ethereum via the TokenBridge's completeTransfer() function. The Ethereum contracts functioned correctly — they had no way to distinguish the forged VAA from a legitimate one.

docs.title: Fork Point
docs.text: Block 14,125,553 — mined at Feb 2, 2022 17:58 UTC, immediately before the attacker's first interaction on Ethereum.

docs.title: Attacker
docs.text: 0x629e7da20197a5429d30da36e77d06cdf796b71a

docs.title: Key Function
docs.text: completeTransfer(bytes) — selector 0xc6878519

docs.title: Reproduction Approach
docs.text:
This reproduction focuses on the EVM-side effects of the exploit:

  1. Verifies both Wormhole contracts exist at the fork block
  2. Probes the completeTransfer() function on the TokenBridge
  3. Demonstrates the bridge message acceptance path exists

The actual Solana-side sysvar exploit cannot be replayed in an EVM testbed. This is declared as L2 fidelity (behavioral reproduction).

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Certik Analysis
  • Halborn Analysis
  • Wormhole Validator Report
  • Etherscan: Attacker's First Tx

docs.title: Blockchain Bridge Security Research Testbed — Implementation Specification
docs.text: Version: 2.0 | Status: Draft | Audience: Engineering Team & Research Group

docs.title: 1. Overview & Goals
docs.text:
Crucible is a CLI-first, modular, reproducible security research testbed for blockchain bridge vulnerability research. It enables researchers to:

  • Replay historical bridge exploits at exact block forks with deterministic results
  • Instantly reset the environment to a verified clean baseline
  • Compose single-chain, dual-chain, and full bridge topologies from a single config switch
  • Automate repetitive tasks — setup, fork, reset, run, log, teardown
  • Collect structured, publication-quality telemetry from every experiment run
  • Validate security patches against known attack vectors

The core philosophy is CLI-first, scenario-as-code, and zero manual setup between experiments.

docs.title: 1.1 Prerequisites & Environment Setup
docs.text:
Every developer working on Crucible needs the following before anything else runs.

System requirements: - Python 3.11+ - Docker + Docker Compose v2 - Git - uv — Python package manager (fast, lockfile-based, reproducible)

Install uv (if not already installed): 

curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
# Verify
uv --version

Install Foundry (pin the version — do not use latest): 

curl -L https://foundry.paradigm.xyz | bash
foundryup --version nightly-cafc2606a2187a42b236df4aa65f4e8cdfcea970
# Verify
anvil --version
forge --version

The exact commit hash above must match what is in foundry.toml. Never run foundryup without a version — it will pull the latest nightly and silently break time-dependent exploits. Crucible enforces this at startup (see §2.1).

Python environment (using uv): 

uv sync                          # creates .venv, installs exact versions from uv.lock
source .venv/bin/activate         # Linux/macOS
# .venv\Scripts\activate          # Windows

Why uv instead of pip? With 4–10 researchers, dependency drift causes "works on my machine" failures. uv.lock pins every dependency to an exact version and hash. uv sync guarantees byte-identical installs across machines. It is also 10–50x faster than pip.

pyproject.toml (canonical dependency list — replaces requirements.txt): 

[project]
name = "crucible"
version = "1.0.0"
requires-python = ">=3.11"
dependencies = [
    "typer>=0.12.0",           # CLI framework (wraps click internally)
    "structlog>=24.0.0",
    "web3>=6.15.0",
    "pydantic>=2.0.0",
    "pyyaml>=6.0.1",
    "rich>=13.0.0",
    "pandas>=2.2.0",
    "matplotlib>=3.9.0",
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
dev = [
    "pytest>=8.0.0",
    "pytest-asyncio>=0.23.0",
    "pytest-mock>=3.12.0",
]
ui = [
    "streamlit>=1.35.0",      # Phase 4 only
]
jupyter = [
    "jupyterlab>=4.0.0",
    "plotly>=5.20.0",
]

[project.scripts]
crucible = "crucible.cli:app"

uv.lock — committed to git. This is the reproducibility guarantee. Generated automatically by uv lock. Never edit manually.

Verify the full stack works: 

crucible --version
crucible status       # should report "no chains running"
forge --version
anvil --version

RPC provider setup (.env — never commit this file): 

cp .env.example .env
# Edit .env and fill in your RPC URLs.
# Free-tier providers that support archive state:
ETH_RPC_URL=https://eth.llamarpc.com
POLYGON_RPC_URL=https://polygon.llamarpc.com

# Optional — paid providers for higher rate limits:
# ALCHEMY_KEY=<your_key>
# ETH_RPC_URL=https://eth-mainnet.g.alchemy.com/v2/${ALCHEMY_KEY}

Archive node access is required for historical forks. Free-tier providers (LlamaRPC, Ankr, PublicNode, dRPC) support archive state but with rate limits (10–25 req/s). Crucible handles this via the RPC Provider Management system (§5.1) with automatic fallback and rate limiting. Paid providers (Alchemy, Infura, QuickNode) offer higher throughput. Self-hosted nodes (Erigon, Reth) are preferred for offline research.

docs.title: 2. Core Design Principles
docs.text: Principle What It Means in Practice Reproducibility First Every experiment is fully reproducible from a single YAML file and a git commit hash Immutable Baselines Golden baselines are fingerprinted and verified before every run — never mutated Scenario-as-Code Attacks, environments, and experiments are version-controlled artifacts, not shell scripts Plug-and-Play Modules Chains, bridges, and attack packs implement standard interfaces; swap freely without touching core CLI is Source of Truth Every action the UI can do, the CLI can do — the UI is a wrapper, never the owner Automation Everywhere No manual environment preparation; reset is one command, always idempotent Full Observability Every action emits a structured JSON log entry; every run produces a complete research artifact bundle

docs.title: 2.1 Determinism Guarantees
docs.text:
The claim "deterministic replay" is the foundation of Crucible's research credibility. This section specifies exactly how determinism is enforced — not as a hope, but as a set of Anvil flags and Chain Manager rules that are mandatory for every run.

Problem: By default, Anvil uses wall-clock time for block timestamps, variable gas pricing, and auto-assigns coinbase addresses. Two runs of the same experiment will produce different timestamps, different gas costs, and potentially different transaction ordering. The research claim of "identical state diffs on repeated runs" fails.

Mandatory Anvil flags for deterministic mode:

Flag Value Why --gas-price 0 Fixes gas price. Eliminates gas cost variation between runs. --block-base-fee-per-gas 1000000000 (1 gwei) Fixes EIP-1559 base fee. Prevents dynamic fee fluctuation. --auto-impersonate (enabled) Allows sending from any address without private key. --chain-id From testbed.yaml Must match the forked chain to avoid replay protection issues. --order fifo Process transactions in submission order. Prevents nonce-based reordering. --timestamp Fork block timestamp Fix the starting block timestamp to the forked block's timestamp — not wall-clock.

Determinism configuration in testbed.yaml: 

chains:
  ethereum:
    rpc_url: "https://eth.llamarpc.com"
    fork_block: 14211979
    chain_id: 1
    port: 8545
    determinism:
      gas_price: 0
      block_base_fee: 1000000000       # 1 gwei — fixed, not dynamic
      timestamp: "fork_block"          # "fork_block" = use the forked block's timestamp
                                       # or an explicit epoch: 1644350000
      coinbase: "0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000000"
      auto_mine: true                  # one tx per block — deterministic ordering
      order: "fifo"                    # transaction processing order

Chain Manager enforcement: - On start(), the Chain Manager reads the determinism block and translates it to Anvil CLI flags - If determinism is not defined, Crucible applies the defaults shown above — deterministic mode is opt-out, not opt-in - crucible status displays the active determinism flags for each chain: 

CHAIN       PORT   BLOCK       GAS PRICE   BASE FEE   TIMESTAMP    ORDER
ethereum    8545   14211979    0           1 gwei     fork_block   fifo
polygon     8546   25000000    0           1 gwei     fork_block   fifo

Foundry version enforcement: - crucible setup checks forge --version against the version pinned in foundry.toml - If versions don't match: abort with a clear error and the exact foundryup command to install the correct version - Override for development: --skip-version-check (never used in CI) - The pinned version is embedded in every run_manifest.json for provenance

Verification of determinism: Crucible guarantees deterministic local execution after the fork is loaded. It does not automatically guarantee full historical equivalence. Historical equivalence is established by each attack pack's evidence manifest and fidelity level.

The scenario test suite (§15.3) verifies local execution repeatability by running the same experiment twice and comparing the artifacts. Instead of requiring brittle byte-for-byte JSON equality (which fails on timestamps), it applies canonical comparison rules: - Canonicalized state diffs are identical - Canonicalized balance diffs are identical - Transaction outcomes are identical - Relevant event sequences are identical - Configured invariant/assertion results are identical

docs.title: 2.2 Reproduction Fidelity Model
docs.text:
For bridge attack research, treating "deterministic replay" as a single binary goal is too coarse. Crucible defines explicit fidelity levels to clarify what is being reproduced:

Level Name Meaning Academic Use L0 Narrative simulation Demonstrates the vulnerability pattern but not historical state Teaching/demo only L1 Contract-level reproduction Reproduces vulnerable contract behavior on local deployed contracts Useful for mechanism validation L2 Fork-state reproduction Uses historical fork block and real deployed contracts/state Strong academic baseline L3 Transaction-path reproduction Replays or closely reconstructs historical exploit transaction sequence Best for single-chain EVM attacks L4 Cross-chain causal reproduction Reconstructs source-chain, destination-chain, bridge messages, and final state impact Best possible standard for bridge attacks

Each attack pack must declare its fidelity level and evidence basis.

docs.title: 2.3 Threat Model & Research Scope
docs.text:
To evaluate patches and attacks effectively, the testbed's simulation boundaries are explicit.

Threat Model / In-Scope: - Smart contract vulnerability exploitation (re-entrancy, uninitialized state, logic errors). - On-chain EVM state manipulation and transaction execution. - Bridge message payload forging or replay (when modeled correctly).

Research Questions crucible is designed to answer: - Can the historical vulnerable state be reconstructed deterministically? - Can the exploit condition be reproduced? - Which state variables and balances changed as a result of the exploit? - Which specific invariants would have detected or prevented the exploit? - Does a proposed patch block the exploit without breaking intended bridge behavior?

Out of Scope for V1: - Non-EVM consensus simulation: If an attack involves Solana or CosmWasm, the non-EVM portion is simulated via a mock or skipped. - Real validator node execution: We do not run actual relayer/validator binaries. Their signatures or message passing behaviors are impersonated. - Exact public mempool ordering: Sandwitch attacks or MEV requiring millisecond mempool state are not modeled faithfully. - L1/L2 sequencer behavior: Unless explicitly modeled in the attack steps.

docs.title: 3. Architecture
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    UI["Streamlit UI (optional)<br>Dashboard • Run Controls • Live Logs • Diff View"]
    CLI["CLI Orchestrator (Typer)<br>init • fork • run • reset • snapshot • restore • logs • clean"]

    SE["Scenario Engine"]
    CM["Chain Manager"]
    BE["Bridge Engine (optional)"]
    SL["Structured Logger"]

    Anvil["Anvil Instance(s)<br>--fork-url <rpc> --fork-block-number <N> --port <P><br>Chain A (port 8545) • Chain B (port 8546)"]
    BL["Golden Baseline System<br>Fingerprint verification before every experiment"]

    UI -- "calls CLI functions directly" --> CLI
    CLI --> SE
    CLI --> CM
    CLI --> BE
    CLI --> SL

    SE --> Anvil
    CM --> Anvil
    BE --> Anvil

    Anvil --> BL
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

Key architectural decisions:

  • Stateful Core, Stateless Scenarios. The Chain Manager owns infrastructure state. Scenarios only declare what to do — they never manage Anvil processes or ports.
  • Topology-Driven Composition. A YAML topology profile declares which modules are active. Single-chain, dual-chain, and full-bridge are three named profiles — not if branches in the code.
  • Snapshot-Driven Reset. Baseline is captured once after initial setup. Reset is anvil_loadState — no chain restart, under 5 seconds for a soft reset.
  • Separation of Scenario and Experiment. A scenario defines the environment (which chains, which fork blocks, which contracts). An experiment references a scenario plus an attack pack. Scenarios are reusable across many experiments.

Inter-component data flow: 

flowchart TD
    CLI["CLI Orchestrator"]
    ER["Experiment Runner"]
    SE["Scenario Engine"]
    CM["Chain Manager"]
    AP["Attack Pack<br>(steps/Exploit.s.sol)"]
    Anvil["Anvil Instance(s)"]
    BL["Baseline verify"]
    SL["Structured Logger"]
    Logs["logs/<run>/<br>├── run.jsonl<br>├── events.jsonl<br>├── storage_diffs.jsonl<br>└── run_manifest.json"]

    CLI -- "experiment.yaml" --> ER
    CLI -- "reset command" --> CM

    ER -- "scenario name" --> SE
    ER -- "topology name<br>(start/stop/fork)" --> CM
    ER -- "attack name" --> AP

    SE -- "steps.py / YAML / .sol<br>RPC calls" --> CM
    CM -- "anvil_* JSON-RPC" --> Anvil
    CM -- "anvil_loadState" --> BL

    CLI -. "all above emit" .-> SL
    ER -. "emit" .-> SL
    SE -. "emit" .-> SL
    CM -. "emit" .-> SL

    SL --> Logs
Hold "Alt" / "Option" to enable pan & zoom

docs.title: 4.1 Chain Manager
docs.text:
File: crucible/chain_manager.py

Purpose: Start, stop, snapshot, and restore Anvil nodes that fork specific chains at specific blocks. Manages process lifecycle and exposes health checks.

class ChainManager:
    def start(self, name: str) -> None:
        """Launch anvil subprocess with fork URL and block number from config."""

    def stop(self, name: str) -> None:
        """Terminate the anvil process and remove PID file."""

    def is_running(self, name: str) -> bool:
        """Health check via eth_blockNumber JSON-RPC call."""

    def snapshot(self, name: str, snapshot_path: str) -> None:
        """Call anvil_dumpState, write state JSON to snapshot_path."""

    def restore(self, name: str, snapshot_path: str) -> None:
        """Call anvil_loadState from snapshot_path. Does NOT restart anvil."""

    def get_rpc_url(self, name: str) -> str:
        """Return http://localhost:<port> for the named chain."""

    def advance_time(self, name: str, seconds: int) -> None:
        """evm_increaseTime + evm_mine for time-dependent exploit testing."""

    def impersonate(self, name: str, address: str) -> None:
        """anvil_impersonateAccount — enables sending tx as any address."""

    def set_balance(self, name: str, address: str, value_wei: int) -> None:
        """anvil_setBalance — fund any account without a private key."""

Internal details: - Each chain runs as subprocess.Popen. PID and metadata (port, chain ID, fork block) stored under states/<chain_name>.meta.json. - Ports and chain IDs come from testbed.yaml. Chain ID must be unique per instance via --chain-id flag. - snapshot/restore use cast rpc calls. State files stored under states/<chain_name>/. - On startup, the manager polls eth_blockNumber with a 30-second timeout before declaring the chain ready.

Error handling: - If Anvil fails to start (unreachable RPC, unsupported block), raises ChainStartError with the exact Anvil stderr output. - Every command that requires a live chain runs is_running() first.

docs.title: 4.2 Scenario Engine
docs.text:
File: crucible/scenario_engine.py

Purpose: Discover, load, and execute attack scenarios from the scenarios/ directory. A scenario is a self-contained directory with a standardised structure.

Scenario directory structure: 

scenarios/<name>/
├── scenario.yaml       # Mandatory — metadata, chains, bridge refs, steps
├── steps.py            # Optional — Python step logic
└── Exploit.s.sol       # Optional — Foundry script

scenario.yaml schema: 

name: "poly_bridge_reentrancy"
description: "Replay the Poly Network reentrancy exploit — August 2021"
version: "1.0.0"
chains: [ethereum, polygon]         # which chains from testbed.yaml to activate

bridge:                             # optional — resolved at runtime
  name: "PolyNetworkBridge"
  contracts:
    ethereum: "0xVault..."
    polygon:  "0xChildToken..."

default_sender: "0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266"

steps:
  - type: python_script             # delegates to steps.py::run()

  # OR use inline atomic steps (no Python needed):
  # - type: impersonate
  #   chain: ethereum
  #   account: "0xRelayer..."
  # - type: send_tx
  #   chain: ethereum
  #   to: "bridge.ethereum"         # resolves via bridge registry
  #   data: "0x..."
  #   value: 0
  # - type: advance_time
  #   chain: polygon
  #   seconds: 3600
  # - type: forge_script
  #   script: Exploit.s.sol:ExploitScript
  #   chain: ethereum
  #   extra_args: ["--sender", "0xAttacker..."]

continue_on_failure: false

Three execution modes:

Mode When Used Best For python_script steps.py present Complex multi-step logic, custom assertions YAML atomic steps steps: list in scenario.yaml Simple replays, no coding required Foundry script .sol present, no steps: defined Native Forge scripting, vm. cheatcodes

Python step API example (steps.py): 

from crucible import chain, log, impersonate, advance_time

def run():
    # Impersonate the bridge relayer
    impersonate("ethereum", "0xRelayerAddress")

    # Send the crafted exploit transaction
    tx = chain("ethereum").send_transaction(
        to="0xVault...",
        data="0x<encoded_payload>",
        value=0,
        sender="0xRelayerAddress"
    )
    log.step("Exploit tx sent", tx_hash=tx.hash, gas_used=tx.gas_used, status=tx.status)

    # Advance time past the challenge window on L2
    advance_time("polygon", 7200)

    # Verify the attacker's resulting balance
    balance = chain("polygon").call(
        to="0xChildToken...",
        data="0x70a08231" + "0xAttacker...".zfill(64)
    )
    log.info("Post-exploit attacker balance", balance=int.from_bytes(balance, "big"))

YAML atomic steps example: 

steps:
  - type: impersonate
    chain: ethereum
    account: "0xRelayer..."
  - type: send_tx
    chain: ethereum
    to: "0xVault..."
    data: "0x..."
    value: 1000000000000000000
  - type: advance_time
    chain: polygon
    seconds: 3600
  - type: assert_balance
    chain: polygon
    account: "0xAttacker..."
    min_value: 1000000000000000000

Symbol resolution: Contract addresses like bridge.ethereum are resolved at runtime by looking up the scenario's bridge block, then falling back to the global bridges.yaml registry. This makes scenarios portable across forks.

docs.title: 4.3 Bridge Engine
docs.text:
File: crucible/bridge_engine.py

Purpose: Deploy, configure, and simulate bridge infrastructure between chains. Fully optional — only loaded when the active topology profile includes a bridge.

class BridgePlugin:
    def metadata(self) -> dict:
        """Return bridge name, version, and supported chain pairs."""

    def deploy(self, chain_a: str, chain_b: str) -> None:
        """Deploy bridge contracts to both chains using Forge scripts."""

    def configure(self, config: dict) -> None:
        """Set validator set, relayer addresses, guardian keys."""

    def connect(self) -> None:
        """Start the relayer / message-passing simulation process."""

    def reset(self) -> None:
        """Reset bridge state to post-deploy baseline."""

    def destroy(self) -> None:
        """Tear down relayer process and bridge contracts."""

Supported bridges (V1): - Wormhole - Nomad - Poly Network - Horizon (Harmony) - Multichain

Relayer simulation components: - Relayer (message ferry) - Validator (signature/quorum) - Guardian (Wormhole-style) - Oracle (price feed simulation) - Watcher (anomaly detection — for patch testing)

docs.title: 4.4 Structured Logger
docs.text:
File: crucible/logging/emitter.py

Purpose: Capture every significant action in a machine-readable, research-grade format. Every run gets its own timestamped output directory. See Section 10 for the full logging architecture.

Output layout: 

logs/<scenario_name>/<YYYY-MM-DD_HH-MM-SS>/
├── run.jsonl               # Main log — one JSON object per line
├── events.jsonl            # Contract events extracted from receipts
├── storage_diffs.jsonl     # Storage slot changes per transaction
├── gas_profile.jsonl       # Gas used per step and cumulative
├── anvil_<chain>.log       # Raw anvil stderr (optional)
├── traces/                 # cast run traces for flagged transactions
└── report.md               # Auto-generated human-readable summary

Phases: setup → baseline_verify → attack → verification → teardown

Log emission points: - Scenario Engine: emits per-step entries via LogEmitter - Chain Manager: emits lifecycle events (start, stop, snapshot, restore) - Golden Baseline System: emits baseline_verified or baseline_mismatch events - Any steps.py can call log.step(), log.info(), log.error() directly

docs.title: 4.5 CLI Orchestrator
docs.text:
File: crucible/cli.py Technology: Typer (Python) Binary: crucible

Full command reference:

Command Description crucible init Scaffold a new workspace from testbed.yaml crucible fork Start Anvil instances per active topology profile crucible setup Fork + run bootstrap.py + capture golden baseline crucible status Show running chains, ports, baseline fingerprints crucible run <scenario> Execute a scenario directly — dev/debug use crucible experiment run <file.yaml> Formal research run — generates run_id + full artifact bundle crucible experiment matrix <file.yaml> Run a parameter matrix sweep crucible reset Soft reset — restore baseline snapshot (<5s) crucible reset --medium Medium reset — kill + relaunch Anvil + restore snapshot (<30s) crucible hard-reset Hard reset — kill, re-fork, re-bootstrap (<5min) crucible snapshot <name> Save current chain state to named snapshot crucible restore <name> Restore a named snapshot crucible logs <scenario> Stream the last run's JSONL log crucible diff <run_a> <run_b> Compare storage/balance diffs between two runs crucible clean Kill all chains, remove temp state files crucible clean --orphans Kill orphaned Anvil processes + stale PID files crucible clean --logs-older-than <N>d Delete log directories older than N days crucible clean --cache Clear the fork cache crucible baseline verify Verify current state matches golden fingerprint crucible plugin list List all discovered chain/bridge/attack plugins crucible plugin validate <dir> Validate a plugin manifest and interface crucible patch validate <file.yaml> Run patch validation workflow crucible export <run_id> Bundle a run for publication crucible history List all completed experiment runs (with filters) crucible estimate <file.yaml> Estimate RPC calls and time for an experiment crucible experiment resume <run_id> Resume an interrupted experiment from last checkpoint crucible experiment list-interrupted List all resumable interrupted runs crucible abi fetch <address> Fetch contract ABI from Etherscan/Sourcify crucible abi list List all registered ABIs crucible status --disk Show disk usage breakdown

crucible run vs crucible experiment run — when to use which: Use crucible run <scenario> during development and debugging — it executes the scenario's steps directly with minimal overhead and no artifact generation. Use crucible experiment run <file.yaml> for all formal research runs — it pairs a scenario + attack pack, generates a run_id, verifies the baseline, and produces a complete artifact bundle. If results will be recorded, compared, or published, always use experiment run.

Global flags: 

--topology <profile>     # Override active topology (single|dual|bridge)
--no-verify              # Skip baseline verification (dangerous, debug only)
--log-level <level>      # debug | info | warn | error
--dry-run                # Print what would happen without executing

Example session: 

crucible setup --topology bridge
crucible experiment run experiments/wormhole_replay.yaml
crucible logs wormhole_replay
crucible reset
crucible experiment run experiments/wormhole_replay.yaml
crucible diff DS-2026-00001 DS-2026-00002
crucible clean

docs.title: 4.6 Golden Baseline System
docs.text:
File: crucible/baseline.py

Purpose: Every scenario has a cryptographically fingerprinted baseline. Before any experiment runs, Crucible verifies the environment matches the expected baseline. If it doesn't match, the run aborts.

Baseline fingerprint structure: 

# states/baseline/<scenario_name>.baseline.yaml
scenario: "wormhole_pre_exploit"
created_at: "2026-06-05T09:00:00Z"
git_commit: "a1b2c3d"
chains:
  ethereum:
    block_number: 14211979
    state_hash:   "0xabc..."      # keccak256 of anvil_dumpState output
    balance_hash: "0xdef..."      # hash of all relevant account balances
    storage_hash: "0x123..."      # hash of key contract storage slots
  polygon:
    block_number: 25000000
    state_hash:   "0x456..."

Verification flow: 

crucible run <scenario>
        │
        [down]
  Restore Snapshot
        │
        [down]
  Compute current fingerprint
        │
        [down]
  Compare with baseline.yaml
        │
   ┌────┴────┐
  PASS      FAIL
   │         │
   [down]         [down]
Execute    Abort + emit
scenario   baseline_mismatch
           log entry → exit 3

Governance rules (enforced by code, not convention): 1. Baselines are never modified after creation — only replaced by creating a new named baseline 2. No plugin or scenario step can write to states/baseline/ 3. Experiments that fail baseline verification are logged and exit with code 3 — never silently skipped

docs.title: 4.7 Experiment Runner
docs.text:
File: crucible/experiment_runner.py

Purpose: Separates the environment definition (scenario) from the research task (experiment). Enables parameter sweeps, patch validation workflows, and batch runs.

Experiment definition (experiments/wormhole_replay.yaml): 

name: "wormhole_replay_2022"
version: "1.0.0"
description: "Reproduce the Wormhole guardian signature bypass — Feb 2022"

scenario: wormhole_pre_exploit      # which environment to use
attack:   wormhole_guardian_bypass  # which attack pack to load

topology: bridge                    # single | dual | bridge

logging:
  traces:        true
  events:        true
  storage_diffs: true
  gas_profiling: true

tags: [wormhole, signature-bypass, 2022, guardian]

Matrix runner — for parameter sweeps: 

# experiments/wormhole_matrix.yaml
name: "wormhole_validator_sweep"
base_experiment: wormhole_replay.yaml

matrix:
  validator_count:       [3, 5, 9, 13, 19]
  guardian_delay_ms:     [0, 500, 2000]
  signature_threshold:   [0.67, 0.75, 0.90]

This produces 5 × 3 × 3 = 45 experiment runs automatically, each with its own run_id and full artifact bundle.

crucible experiment matrix experiments/wormhole_matrix.yaml --parallel 4

docs.title: 4.8 Experiment Checkpoint & Resume
docs.text:
File: crucible/checkpoint.py

Purpose: Multi-phase bridge attack replays can take minutes to hours. If a crash, OOM, or Ctrl+C interrupts a run at phase 4 of 5, the researcher should not have to restart from scratch. Crucible saves phase-level checkpoints automatically.

Checkpoint lifecycle: 

Phase 1 (environment_setup) completes
    → anvil_dumpState → states/checkpoints/<run_id>/phase_1.json
    → checkpoint.json updated: phase 1 = complete

Phase 2 (privilege_escalation) completes
    → anvil_dumpState → states/checkpoints/<run_id>/phase_2.json
    → checkpoint.json updated: phase 2 = complete

Phase 3 (state_manipulation) — CRASH (OOM / Ctrl+C / RPC timeout)
    → graceful shutdown handler (§22.1) saves partial state
    → checkpoint.json updated: phase 3 = interrupted

--- later ---

crucible experiment resume <run_id>
    → load checkpoint.json: last completed = phase 2
    → anvil_loadState from phase_2.json
    → re-execute from phase 3 onward
    → on success: delete checkpoint files, produce full artifact bundle

Checkpoint manifest (states/checkpoints/<run_id>/checkpoint.json): 

{
  "run_id":       "DS-2026-00001",
  "experiment":   "experiments/wormhole_replay.yaml",
  "scenario":     "wormhole_pre_exploit",
  "attack":       "wormhole_guardian_bypass",
  "topology":     "bridge",
  "created_at":   "2026-06-05T10:23:45Z",
  "phases": [
    {"id": 1, "name": "environment_setup",      "status": "complete", "snapshot": "phase_1.json"},
    {"id": 2, "name": "exploit_execution",       "status": "complete", "snapshot": "phase_2.json"},
    {"id": 3, "name": "asset_extraction",        "status": "interrupted", "error": "SIGINT"},
    {"id": 4, "name": "verification",            "status": "pending"}
  ],
  "partial_logs": "logs/wormhole_replay/2026-06-05_10-23-45_DS-2026-00001/"
}

Resume command: 

# Resume an interrupted experiment
crucible experiment resume DS-2026-00001

# List all resumable runs
crucible experiment list-interrupted
# RUN ID          SCENARIO              LAST PHASE    INTERRUPTED AT
# DS-2026-00001   wormhole_pre_exploit  phase 2       2026-06-05 10:25:01

Implementation rules: 1. Checkpoints are saved after every successfully completed phase — not just on failure 2. On successful experiment completion, checkpoint files are deleted (configurable: keep_checkpoints: true in experiment YAML for debugging) 3. Checkpoint storage counts against the cleanup policy (§7.1 max_checkpoints) 4. Resume restores chain state from the checkpoint, then continues executing from the next pending phase 5. Partial logs from the interrupted run are merged with the resumed run — the final run.jsonl contains the complete timeline 6. The run_manifest.json for a resumed run includes "resumed_from": "DS-2026-00001" and "resume_count": 1

docs.title: 5. Configuration Format
docs.text:
testbed.yaml — global workspace config (canonical, all fields): 

version: "1.0"

chains:
  ethereum:
    rpc_url:           "https://eth.llamarpc.com"   # Must be archive node
    fork_block:        14211979
    chain_id:          1
    port:              8545
    anvil_extra_args:  []
    determinism:                                     # §2.1 — opt-out, not opt-in
      gas_price:       0
      block_base_fee:  1000000000                    # 1 gwei, fixed
      timestamp:       "fork_block"
      coinbase:        "0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000000"
      auto_mine:       true
      order:           "fifo"

  polygon:
    rpc_url:           "https://polygon-rpc.com"
    fork_block:        25000000
    chain_id:          137
    port:              8546
    # determinism: uses defaults from above if omitted

providers:                                           # §5.1 — RPC fallback chain
  ethereum:
    - url: "https://eth.llamarpc.com"
      name: llamarpc
      priority: 1
      rate_limit_rps: 25
      type: free
      archive: true
    - url: "https://rpc.ankr.com/eth"
      name: ankr
      priority: 2
      rate_limit_rps: 15
      type: free
      archive: true
  polygon:
    - url: "https://polygon.llamarpc.com"
      name: llamarpc
      priority: 1
      rate_limit_rps: 25
      type: free
      archive: true

  rpc_budget:                                        # §5.1 — Cost & quota controls
    max_requests_per_run: 5000
    max_usd_estimate: 5.00
    fail_if_exceeded: true

defaults:
  sender:      "0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266"
  gas_limit:   30_000_000
  block_time:  12

logging:
  level:              info
  save_anvil_logs:    false
  trace_transactions: false

fork_cache:                                          # §5.2 — Anvil RPC response cache
  enabled:     true
  path:        ".fork_cache"
  max_size_gb: 20
  shared:      false                                 # true = absolute path, shared across researchers

cleanup:                                             # §7.1 — automatic disk management
  max_log_age_days:      90
  max_log_size_gb:       50
  max_cache_size_gb:     20
  max_checkpoints:       10
  warn_at_disk_percent:  85
  auto_clean_on_setup:   false

notifications:                                       # §16.1 — experiment completion hooks
  enabled: false
  hooks: []                                          # see §16.1 for webhook/command examples

bridges.yaml — global bridge registry: 

wormhole:
  core:               "0x98f3c9e6E3fAce36bAAd05FE09d375Ef1464288B"
  token_bridge:       "0x3ee18B2214AFF97000D974cf647E7C347E8fa585"
  guardian_set_index: 0

nomad:
  home:               "0x92d3404a7E6c91455BbD81475Cd9fAd96ACFF4c8"
  replica_ethereum:   "0x740bA4f3C2FE3B6c673Ce27Ad24218f9B4E2C803"

docs.title: 5.1 RPC Provider Management
docs.text:
Problem: Free-tier RPC providers (LlamaRPC, Ankr, PublicNode, dRPC) enforce 10–25 requests/second. During initial fork, Anvil issues hundreds of eth_getStorageAt calls. Without fallback and rate limiting, experiments fail mid-fork with opaque timeout errors.

Provider registry in testbed.yaml: 

providers:
  ethereum:
    - url: "https://eth.llamarpc.com"
      name: llamarpc
      priority: 1                      # tried first
      rate_limit_rps: 25               # max requests per second
      type: free
      archive: true

    - url: "https://rpc.ankr.com/eth"
      name: ankr
      priority: 2                      # fallback
      rate_limit_rps: 15
      type: free
      archive: true

    - url: "https://eth-mainnet.g.alchemy.com/v2/${ALCHEMY_KEY}"
      name: alchemy
      priority: 3                      # used only if ALCHEMY_KEY is set
      rate_limit_rps: 30
      type: paid
      archive: true

  polygon:
    - url: "https://polygon.llamarpc.com"
      name: llamarpc
      priority: 1
      rate_limit_rps: 25
      type: free
      archive: true

Fallback behavior: 1. Chain Manager tries the highest-priority provider first 2. If the provider returns 429 (rate limited), 5xx (server error), or times out (30s default): - Log a warning: "Provider llamarpc rate limited, falling back to ankr" - Rotate to the next provider in priority order 3. If all providers fail, abort with exit code 5 and a clear error listing what was tried 4. Providers requiring environment variables (e.g., ${ALCHEMY_KEY}) that are not set are silently skipped — not an error

Rate limiter implementation: - Token bucket algorithm per provider - Configured via rate_limit_rps — defaults to 25 if not specified - Applied transparently by the Chain Manager before every RPC call - Rate limit state is not shared between Anvil instances — each chain has its own limiter

Pre-flight health check — crucible setup phase: 

For each chain in testbed.yaml:
  1. Select the highest-priority available provider
  2. Call eth_getBlockByNumber(fork_block, false)
  3. If the call succeeds → provider supports archive state at this block ✓
  4. If the call fails with "missing trie node" or returns null → provider does NOT have archive state
     → Try next provider
     → If no provider has archive state: abort with exit code 5
        "ERROR: No RPC provider for ethereum supports archive state at block 14211979.
         Archive node access is required. See docs/rpc_providers.md"

Cost estimation — crucible estimate: 

crucible estimate experiments/wormhole_replay.yaml
# CHAIN       PROVIDER     EST. RPC CALLS    EST. TIME (at rate limit)    COST
# ethereum    llamarpc     ~2,400            ~96 seconds                  free
# polygon     llamarpc     ~800              ~32 seconds                  free
# TOTAL                    ~3,200            ~2 minutes
#
# Note: estimates based on similar scenario profiles. Actual counts may vary.

The estimator uses historical RPC call counts from previous runs of the same scenario (stored in the experiment index). For first-time scenarios, it uses a heuristic based on the number of steps and chain interactions.

Operational Modes & RPC Cost Limits: To preserve free-tier RPC quotas and support offline research, Crucible enforces specific operational modes:

Mode Description Support in V1 Online fork mode Uses live archive RPC and populates the Anvil fork cache. Required Cached fork mode Uses previously populated Anvil cache, bypassing paid RPC during reruns. Required Local archive mode Uses a local Erigon/Reth archive node (http://localhost:8545). Supported, but node operation is out-of-scope for Crucible Artifact replay mode Uses exported baselines and JSONL traces without fetching chain state. Phase 3 export goal

To enforce cost control, Crucible reads the rpc_budget from testbed.yaml. If an experiment's estimated RPC calls or USD cost exceeds the budget, the run is aborted before forking unless overridden with --force-budget.

docs.title: 5.2 Fork Cache Layer
docs.text:
Problem: Re-forking the same (chain, block) pair hits the RPC provider every time. With free-tier providers and multiple researchers, this wastes rate limit budget and makes hard resets unnecessarily slow.

Solution: Use Anvil's built-in --fork-cache-path flag to cache RPC responses on disk.

Configuration in testbed.yaml: 

fork_cache:
  enabled: true
  path: ".fork_cache"               # relative to workspace root
  max_size_gb: 20                   # LRU eviction threshold
  shared: true                      # share cache across researchers on the same machine

How it works: 1. On first fork of ethereum at block 14211979, Anvil issues RPC calls and caches every response under .fork_cache/ethereum/14211979/ 2. On subsequent forks of the same chain+block, Anvil reads from cache — zero RPC calls 3. This makes hard resets (re-fork from scratch) nearly as fast as soft resets

Chain Manager integration: 

# When starting Anvil, the Chain Manager adds the cache path:
anvil_args = [
    "anvil",
    "--fork-url", rpc_url,
    "--fork-block-number", str(fork_block),
    "--fork-cache-path", str(cache_path / chain_name / str(fork_block)),
    "--port", str(port),
]

Cache invalidation: - Changing fork_block in testbed.yaml does NOT invalidate existing cache entries — different blocks use different cache directories - crucible clean --cache deletes the entire .fork_cache/ directory - The cleanup policy (§7.1) evicts LRU entries when the cache exceeds max_size_gb

Sharing between researchers: When shared: true, the cache path is absolute (e.g., /shared/crucible_cache/) rather than workspace-relative. Multiple researchers' workspaces can point to the same cache directory, so the first researcher to fork a given block pays the RPC cost — everyone else reads from cache.

# For shared-machine deployment:
fork_cache:
  enabled: true
  path: "/shared/crucible_cache"    # shared directory with group write permissions
  max_size_gb: 50
  shared: true

Files: .fork_cache/ is always gitignored. It is machine-local or machine-shared, never committed.

docs.title: 6. Topology Profiles
docs.text:
A topology profile is a YAML file under topologies/ that declares which modules to load. Switching topology is a flag, not a code change.

# topologies/single.yaml
name: single
description: "One EVM chain, no bridge. For single-chain attack research."
chains: [ethereum]
bridge: false

# topologies/dual.yaml
name: dual
description: "Two EVM chains, no bridge. For testing cross-chain state without relay."
chains: [ethereum, polygon]
bridge: false

# topologies/bridge.yaml
name: bridge
description: "Two chains + active bridge relayer. Full bridge attack research."
chains: [ethereum, polygon]
bridge: wormhole       # references bridges.yaml

Usage: 

crucible setup --topology single     # spin up only Chain A
crucible setup --topology dual       # spin up Chain A + B
crucible setup --topology bridge     # spin up Chain A + B + relayer

docs.title: 7. Snapshot & Reset System
docs.text:
Three-tier reset model:

Tier Command Mechanism Target Time When to Use Soft Reset crucible reset anvil_loadState from baseline snapshot < 5 seconds Between experiment phases, most common Medium Reset crucible reset --medium Kill + relaunch Anvil + restore snapshot < 30 seconds After Anvil becomes unstable Hard Reset crucible hard-reset Full kill + re-fork + re-bootstrap < 5 minutes Changing fork block, corrupted state

Baseline creation flow: 

crucible setup
    │
    ├── 1. Chain Manager starts all chains from testbed.yaml
    │
    ├── 2. Run bootstrap.py (if present)
    │       ├── Deploy mock tokens / contracts
    │       ├── anvil_setBalance for test accounts
    │       ├── anvil_impersonateAccount for deployers
    │       └── Set up bridge contracts if not at fork block
    │
    ├── 3. anvil_dumpState for each chain → states/baseline/<chain>.json
    │
    └── 4. Compute + write golden baseline fingerprint
            → states/baseline/<scenario>.baseline.yaml

Named snapshots (for mid-experiment checkpoints): 

crucible snapshot post_deploy        # save current state
crucible snapshot mid_attack         # save after step 2
crucible restore post_deploy         # jump back to that point

State file handling: - All state files under states/ are .gitignored — they are large (100s of MB) - states/baseline/ fingerprints ARE committed — they are small YAML files - Fork cache (.fork_cache/) is separate and reused across sessions

docs.title: 7.1 Storage Management & Cleanup Policies
docs.text:
Problem: A single anvil_dumpState produces 100–500 MB. With 6 benchmark attacks, multiple snapshots, checkpoints, and 4–10 researchers, disk usage can exceed 50 GB rapidly.

Cleanup configuration in testbed.yaml: 

cleanup:
  max_log_age_days: 90           # auto-delete log directories older than this
  max_log_size_gb: 50            # warn when total log size exceeds this
  max_cache_size_gb: 20          # fork cache eviction threshold
  max_checkpoints: 10            # keep only the last N checkpoint sets per scenario
  warn_at_disk_percent: 85       # emit warning when disk usage exceeds this
  auto_clean_on_setup: false     # if true, run cleanup before every `crucible setup`

Enhanced crucible clean subcommands:

Command Effect crucible clean Kill all Anvil processes, remove PID files and temp state files. Keep baselines, logs, and cache. crucible clean --logs-older-than 30d Delete log directories older than 30 days crucible clean --logs-older-than 7d --dry-run Show what would be deleted without deleting crucible clean --states Delete all non-baseline snapshots (named snapshots, checkpoints) crucible clean --cache Clear the fork cache (.fork_cache/) crucible clean --checkpoints Delete all experiment checkpoints crucible clean --orphans Kill orphaned Anvil processes + remove stale PID files crucible clean --all Nuclear option — everything except committed files. Equivalent to git clean -fdx for generated directories. crucible clean --all --dry-run Show the full cleanup plan without executing

Disk usage reporting: 

crucible status --disk
# DIRECTORY          SIZE        FILES    DESCRIPTION
# states/baseline/   1.2 GB      4        Golden baseline snapshots
# states/snapshots/  3.5 GB      12       Named snapshots
# states/checkpoints/ 800 MB     3        Experiment checkpoints
# logs/              8.2 GB      156      Experiment log bundles
# .fork_cache/       12.1 GB     —        RPC response cache
# datasets/          450 MB      5        Exported artifact bundles
# ─────────────────────────────────────
# TOTAL              26.3 GB              Disk: 42% used (62 GB free)

Automatic cleanup triggers: 1. Before crucible setup (if auto_clean_on_setup: true): remove checkpoints older than max_checkpoints 2. At end of every experiment run: check total log size against max_log_size_gb and warn if exceeded 3. On crucible fork: check fork cache size against max_cache_size_gb and evict oldest entries if exceeded

Eviction order for fork cache: Least Recently Used (LRU). Each cache entry has a last-access timestamp. Oldest entries are evicted first until the cache is under the size limit.

docs.title: 8. Attack Scenario Packs
docs.text:
Convention over configuration. Each attack is a self-contained directory. Adding a new attack means adding a folder — nothing else changes.

Pack structure: 

attacks/<name>/
├── plugin.yaml         # Plugin manifest (§9.3) + attack-specific metadata
├── evidence.yaml       # Historical basis, scope, and assumptions
├── steps.py            # OR Exploit.s.sol — the actual attack logic
├── Exploit.s.sol       # Foundry script (optional)
├── verify.py           # Post-attack assertion script
└── expected_state.json # Expected balance/storage diffs for auto-verification

Note: Attack packs use the same plugin.yaml manifest format as all other plugins (§9.3). The fields below extend the base manifest with attack-specific metadata (cve, date, funds_lost_usd, phases).

plugin.yaml for an attack pack — full schema: 

# Base plugin fields (§9.3)
name:    "wormhole_guardian_bypass"
type:    attack
version: "1.0.0"
author:  "research-team"
entry:   "wormhole_guardian_bypass.py"
class:   "WormholeGuardianBypass"
requires:
  crucible: ">=1.0.0"
  chains:   [ethereum]

# Attack-specific extensions
cve: "N/A"
date: "2022-02-02"
funds_lost_usd: 326000000
topology_required: bridge

requirements:
  - guardian_set_index: 0
  - wormhole_core_deployed: true

phases:
  - id: 1
    name: "environment_setup"
    description: "Verify guardian set and core contract state"
  - id: 2
    name: "exploit_execution"
    description: "Forge fake VAA with uninitialized guardian set"
  - id: 3
    name: "asset_extraction"
    description: "Complete token withdrawal via forged VAA"
  - id: 4
    name: "verification"
    description: "Assert attacker balance and bridge depletion"

expected_state.json: 

{
  "chain": "ethereum",
  "assertions": [
    {
      "type": "balance_gte",
      "account": "0xAttacker...",
      "token":   "0xWETH...",
      "value":   "93750000000000000000000"
    },
    {
      "type": "balance_eq",
      "account": "0xWormholeVault...",
      "token":   "0xWETH...",
      "value":   "0"
    }
  ]
}

Researchers can run individual phases: 

crucible run wormhole_guardian_bypass --phase 1
crucible run wormhole_guardian_bypass --phase 2
crucible run wormhole_guardian_bypass --phases 1,2,3

docs.title: 8.1 Evidence Manifest
docs.text:
To ensure academic credibility, every attack pack must include an evidence.yaml file defining exactly what is being reproduced, what assumptions are modeled, and what is excluded.

evidence.yaml schema: 

incident:
  name: "Nomad replica message validation exploit"
  date: "2022-08-01"
  affected_bridge: "Nomad"

sources:
  advisories:
    - "https://bridge-hack-report.example.com"
  tx_explorers:
    - "0xExampleTransactionHash"

historical_scope:
  chains: ["ethereum"]
  fork_blocks:
    ethereum: 15259101
  affected_contracts:
    - "0xNomadReplicaContract"

reproduction_scope:
  fidelity_level: L2
  exact_tx_replay: false
  modeled_components:
    - "attacker action"
    - "bridge message format"
  excluded_components:
    - "private key acquisition"
    - "relayer latency"

acceptance:
  required_assertions:
    - "attacker_balance_increases"
    - "bridge_liability_changes"
    - "vulnerable_validation_accepts_invalid_message"

docs.title: 8.2 Review Gates for Attack Packs
docs.text:
New attack packs are subject to strict academic-quality review gates before inclusion in the benchmark suite:

  1. Manifests: Both plugin.yaml and evidence.yaml pass schema validation.
  2. Evidence: Sources and modeled/excluded assumptions are explicitly declared.
  3. Assertions: At least one positive assertion proves the exploit effect, and one negative assertion proves the baseline was not already compromised.
  4. Repeatability: The determinism test passes on two consecutive runs (canonicalized state diffs match).
  5. Exportability: The exported data bundle includes all necessary reproduction instructions.

docs.title: 9. Modular Architecture & Plug-and-Play System
docs.text: This section is the canonical reference for how modularity works in Crucible. Every major subsystem is a replaceable unit. Nothing in crucible/core/ ever imports a specific chain, bridge, or attack by name — it only ever speaks to interfaces.

docs.title: 9.1 The Module Contract
docs.text:
Every pluggable unit in Crucible — chain, bridge, attack, instrumentation — must satisfy two things:

  1. A plugin.yaml manifest in its root directory
  2. An interface implementation — a Python class inheriting from the correct abstract base

If both are present and valid, the plugin is discovered and usable. No registration. No config changes. No core code changes.

docs.title: 9.2 Module Types and Their Interfaces
docs.text:
Chain Module (crucible/interfaces/chain.py): 

from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

class ChainPlugin(ABC):

    @abstractmethod
    def fork(self, block: int, rpc_url: str, port: int) -> None:
        """Start an Anvil fork at the given block. Must be idempotent."""

    @abstractmethod
    def snapshot(self) -> str:
        """Persist current state. Returns an opaque snapshot_id."""

    @abstractmethod
    def restore(self, snapshot_id: str) -> None:
        """Restore to a previously snapshotted state."""

    @abstractmethod
    def reset(self) -> None:
        """Restore to the golden baseline snapshot."""

    @abstractmethod
    def collect_logs(self, from_block: int, to_block: int) -> list[dict]:
        """Return all transaction logs in the block range."""

    @abstractmethod
    def collect_traces(self, tx_hash: str) -> dict:
        """Return the full execution trace for a transaction."""

    @abstractmethod
    def health_check(self) -> bool:
        """Return True if the chain is responsive."""

Bridge Module (crucible/interfaces/bridge.py): 

class BridgePlugin(ABC):

    @abstractmethod
    def metadata(self) -> dict:
        """Return name, version, chain_pairs supported."""

    @abstractmethod
    def deploy(self, chain_a: str, chain_b: str) -> dict:
        """Deploy bridge contracts. Returns deployed address map."""

    @abstractmethod
    def configure(self, config: dict) -> None:
        """Apply validator set, guardian keys, relayer addresses."""

    @abstractmethod
    def connect(self) -> None:
        """Start message-passing simulation (relayer process)."""

    @abstractmethod
    def reset(self) -> None:
        """Reset bridge state to post-deploy baseline."""

    @abstractmethod
    def destroy(self) -> None:
        """Tear down relayer and clean up deployed contracts."""

Attack Module (crucible/interfaces/attack.py): 

class AttackPlugin(ABC):

    @abstractmethod
    def metadata(self) -> dict:
        """Return name, version, topology_required, chains_required."""

    @abstractmethod
    def requirements(self) -> list[dict]:
        """Return preconditions that must be true before execution."""

    @abstractmethod
    def prepare(self, context: ExperimentContext) -> None:
        """Set up attacker accounts, approve tokens, deploy helper contracts."""

    @abstractmethod
    def execute(self, phase: int | None = None) -> AttackResult:
        """Run the attack (or a single phase). Returns structured result."""

    @abstractmethod
    def verify(self) -> VerificationResult:
        """Check expected_state.json assertions against current chain state."""

    @abstractmethod
    def rollback(self) -> None:
        """Undo any persistent changes not handled by snapshot restore."""

    @abstractmethod
    def cleanup(self) -> None:
        """Remove temp files, kill helper processes."""

Instrumentation Module (crucible/interfaces/instrumentation.py): 

class InstrumentationPlugin(ABC):

    @abstractmethod
    def attach(self, chain_name: str, rpc_url: str) -> None:
        """Subscribe to chain events for this experiment run."""

    @abstractmethod
    def collect(self) -> list[dict]:
        """Pull buffered events/traces since last collect()."""
